title: Penn State CB Zion Tracy emerging as hot name in spring ball with ‘chess piece’ versatility
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Last Tuesday, Matt Campbell didn’t shy away from highlighting a returning senior as a player who has caught his eye.

At that point, Campbell had seen a handful of Pinstripe Bowl practices, winter workouts, watched film and only had one spring practice as the head coach of Penn State, but it was gushing praise he had for a cornerback.

“The one young man that has stood out to me from the day that I’ve gotten here to where we’re at — I think Zion Tracy is one of the best football players that maybe I have coached, to be quite honest with you, in terms of talent, ability,” Campbell said.

For Tracy, who has appeared in 40 games over the last three seasons and the last two as the starting nickel cornerback, consistency has been important.

“I’d say trusting my process and things I've been doing in the past, like my routine,” Tracy said. “Just keeping up with that keeps me on the same constant focus every day.”

Tracy is one of the older presences in the cornerback room, trailing behind just redshirt senior Audavion Collins as the eldest in the unit.

Now entering Year 4 at Penn State, Tracy is taking it upon himself to emerge as a leader under position coach Terry Smith.

“I've been more vocal with the team, just trying to raise everybody up,” Tracy said. “If someone feels like they having a bad day, I'll speak, everyone can hear me, get that energy up type of thing.”

As far as on the field, Tracy has been taking on a versatile role in the defensive backfield. His role at Penn State has been as a nickel corner with a sporadic showing of being a backup on the outside.

With Campbell taking over, and defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn and safeties coach Deon Broomfield joining the staff, Tracy is now seeing work at safety. Tracy has had conversations in the past about seeing work at the spot, but the current set of spring practices is the first time he’s seeing burn at the position.

“Being 10 yards back, I'll be able to see more, like when emotions happen,” Tracy said. “When I'm at nickel, I'm closer to the ball, so if something will happen it’s quick, right in front of me. So it could be easy to miss, and things like that. Safety is deep, so you could see everything.”

There are a lot of moving parts in the safety room, as Vaboue Toure’s the only returning scholarship safety for Penn State to join the influx of transfers. To help Tracy learn more about the position, he hit up former teammates Zakee Wheatley and Jaylen Reed to pick their brains about the spot.

Despite the limited experience, Campbell believes Tracy’s versatility in the secondary, paired with being an option at punt returner, where Tracy said his goal is to be the starter, can make him a dangerous player in all facets.

“I think Zion can be one of the best corners in the country. I think he can be one of the best safeties,” Campbell said. “I think he can play nickel. He can do a lot. And I think when you have a chess piece like Zion, it gives you the ability to start to kind of navigate the rest of the defensive structure around him.”

Campbell has seen Tracy make big plays, highlighting his 31-yard pick six against Ohio State at Beaver Stadium two years ago. He believes there are more impact moments in store for the veteran defensive back.

In a similar sentiment, Tracy wants to achieve what he thinks he’s always been capable of.

“Proving to myself to be the best I can be,” Tracy said. “Coming here as a freshman, I already knew the things I could do on the field, but I want to really prove that to the world, really prove it to myself.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell talks practice goals, D’Anton Lynn during Week 2 of spring ball
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
With his core values now plastered on the walls of Penn State’s team meeting room, Matt Campbell took to the podium in Lasch Building on Tuesday after his squad’s fourth spring practice.

There, Campbell discussed his goals for spring practice and some players who have stood out so far.

Here’s more of what Campbell had to say.

Who’s stepping up?

Campbell confirmed reports that quarterback Alex Manske is out for spring ball, but said the Iowa State transfer will be ready for fall camp.

With Manske out and Rocco Becht limited, plenty of younger guys like Peyton Falzone and Kase Evans have been getting meaningful reps this spring. But it’s a veteran presence who has stood out to Campbell.

“Connor Barry, he's come in, and he's really done a great job, probably been the surprise so far, of four days of practice, just his ability to get quality reps,” Campbell said. “I think we were really excited when Connor decided to come here, allowing him to be with some of our veteran players, and watch how he operates.”

Becht isn’t the only veteran starter out for spring practice, however, as the Nittany Lions’ injury list is quite long right now. That group includes wide receivers Brett Eskildsen and Chase Sowell, who are expected to be two of Penn State’s starters.

In their absences, Campbell mentioned Karon Brookins, Zay Robinson and Koby Howard as younger players who have stepped up so far.

“To be able to evaluate your football team, I don't know if you could ask for a better situation, for those young guys to be able to step up and kind of show what they can do,” Campbell said. “And honestly, they've been impressive so far, and so it's great to see.”

D’Anton Lynn

The splash hire for Campbell this offseason was defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn, who Penn State poached from USC and is a former letterman, marking Campbell’s first time with a defensive coordinator besides Jon Heacock in 12 years.

As Lynn works to install his scheme and the players get a taste of his coaching style, Campbell has kept a watchful eye and loves what he’s seen so far.

“Watching coach Lynn teach, I think one of the great gifts that he has, and what was so impressive to me even through the interview process, is just his ability to articulate what he wants out of the defense, what the expectations are,” Campbell said. “He's a great holistic teacher, and obviously his time in the National Football League makes you be that. Your time restrictions are so short in that league that you got to be a great communicator and a great teacher.”

Safeties coach Deon Broomfield said last week that Lynn is striving for “obnoxious communication” this spring, and Campbell has enjoyed watching his new defensive coordinator get the coaching staff on the same page.

“I think that's been the fun thing to watch him, number one, align the coaching staff on verbiage, communication, everybody speaking the same language,” Campbell said. “And then to watch that coaching staff start to implement what the defense needs to look like, what are the standards? How are we communicating through everything? And I think they've done an unbelievable job.”

Spring practice goals

Campbell has the tough task of getting this brand-new Penn State team ready to play football in five months, and spring practice is a crucial aspect of that.

But Campbell’s goal for these 15 practices is to build a mentally and physically tough team that can go through hard times and come out stronger.

“Don't get slow and right twisted with intentful, meaningful, passionate reps,” Campbell said. “And there are wins and losses on every rep that happens in the sport of football. And what I want to see is, how do we respond? How do you respond when it goes good? How do you respond when it doesn't go well? Because all those things are coming, right?”

Campbell referenced Duke’s recent men’s basketball loss to UConn in the Elite Eight, emphasizing that no team is perfect and there are going to be losses along the way, even if that’s a single rep in practice.

“We all want to be great coaches, and we want it to all look perfect, but that's not the world we live in,” Campbell said. “Tough times are going to happen. Good things are going to happen, and it's the team that's mentally tough enough and physically tough enough to be able to just consistently keep showing up and being the best in the moment, those are the ones that have the ability to win.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball drops home stand behind inconsistent bats, pitching woes
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions earned mixed results over their five-game homestand against three different opponents and finished 2-3 on the week, which dropped their record to 8-18 on the season.

Penn State won 8-6 over St. Bonaventure on its first Dollar Dog Night of the year, but then endured a shutout 9-0 loss to Bucknell in the next game.

The homestand wrapped up with a Big Ten series loss against Illinois. While the blue and white started strong and recorded a comeback 9-8 win in its series opener, the squad lost 16-5 and then 6-0 in the next two games.

Now, the Nittany Lions stay home and face UCF at 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday before traveling for an away conference series against Nebraska this weekend.

Here are some common themes that occurred throughout the last week of games.

Inconsistent offense

While Penn State recorded 22 runs over the span of these five games, the team was also shut out twice by both Bucknell and Illinois. The blue and white totaled just five hits in its first shutout loss and only three hits in the series finale loss to Illinois.

The bats have been up and down throughout the campaign as the offense led by junior Bryce Molinaro and senior Michael Anderson sometimes clicks on all cylinders, but is also completely shut down in many losses. Despite the few shutout losses, Penn State’s offense rallied down 6-0 in the series opener against Illinois and accumulated 11 hits in the win.

Additionally, the blue and white rely on power with a tied sixth most home runs in the Big Ten with 31 and the squad continued raking. Anderson homered twice last week to bring his team-best total to 11 on the season. Molinaro trails behind Anderson with seven dingers, after he belted two as well.

However, the Nittany Lions need more consistency from their lineup. It’s essential for the blue and white’s bats to provide runs because their pitching staff has struggled with limiting runs all season.

Bad pitching continues

There’s been one main theme to the Nittany Lions' struggles this season and that is their pitching staff woes. The squad continues to possess the worst team-ERA in the Big Ten at 8.36 while also surrendering the most hits and runs.

In the loss to Bucknell, freshman Ethan Bauerschmidt delivered a rough start, surrendering five runs on three hits and two walks over 2.1 innings.

Junior Mason Horwat lasted just 1.2 innings in his start in the series opener against Illinois. The Fighting Illini jumped on the Pittsburgh native and plated six runs on three hits and five walks.

Sophomore Ben Hudson followed his quality outing against Purdue with a rocky start to Illinois. The Medford, New Jersey, native allowed six runs on five hits and three walks over just three innings.

While freshman Isaiah Shayter had a solid outing in the series finale against Illinois, allowing just three runs over 5.2 innings, the bullpen didn’t offer much relief. Senior Dimond Loosli allowed two runs over just 0.2 innings.

With the pitching staff still struggling, it will be hard for Penn State to turn the season around until its pitchers can settle down opposing teams’ lineups.

title: Three executive branch tickets participate in annual UPUA debate
author: Tyler Nolt | The Daily Collegian
description:
Leading up to the University Park Undergraduate Association’s (UPUA) annual election on Wednesday, the student government has historically hosted a debate to showcase the candidates to the student body.

Each executive ticket — Mann-Calderon, Hogan-Lopez and Hatch-Smith — was given a chance to introduce themselves and their platform. Each ticket was then asked questions by the UPUA’s election commission, which comprises the judicial branch.

Candidates were allocated three minutes per question and another two minutes for rebuttal.

What is the single biggest issue facing Penn State students today, and how do you plan on addressing it?

Tyler Mann shared his belief that the most significant issue is connectivity among students. He described this both in the context of student-to-student and student-to-representative relationships.

To fix this, Mann, a fourth-year studying integrative science, said he would be present on campus, including showing up to student organization events along with hosting more public office hours.

Kat Hogan followed by saying accessibility of basic needs was the largest issue. She referred specifically to the accessibility of food, housing and mental health services. To address this issue, Hogan, a third-year studying energy engineering, pointed out many campus organizations she intends to collaborate with.

Last to answer was Akito Hatch, who said the student body’s biggest issue is “receiving the best student experience that students can.”

Hatch, a third-year studying criminology and political science, elaborated on the ideas of the other tickets, pointing out their benefits to the student body, and contributing to their time in college.

“I think that this ultimately all kind of adds to our one goal, which is receiving the best student experience that we can kind of give each other,” Hatch said.

How do you plan on leading an executive branch that can do an effective job of creating a more cohesive environment between all three branches of UPUA?

Hogan pointed to her and Lopez’s experience within UPUA to showcase familiarity with the workings of each branch. She then presented her idea to utilize meetings between the leaders of each branch more than the 20th assembly did, as well as continuing to develop an outline of UPUA’s leadership positions.

Hatch said there are a number of positive relationships formed through collaboration within UPUA, and those should be maintained. However, he acknowledged there are some downsides to this, and stricter leadership from the executive branch is necessary.

Mann highlighted his time as president of the Association of Residence Hall Students (ARHS). He said his leadership style was one of motivation, and he would want to focus on empowering leaders within UPUA to continue improving the lives of fellow students.

“I don't want to just hold people accountable,” Mann said. “I want to motivate them to be the best versions of themselves and use the position of president in order to provide them the opportunities to best accomplish it.”

How will you improve the transparency of the UPUA and ensure that the organization is making effective relationships with the student body, student organizations and our community partners?

Hatch started by saying UPUA is already a transparent organization, and that transparency and outreach shouldn’t be major focuses. He said UPUA should instead focus on “things and items that students care about.”

Caleb Calderon, vice presidential candidate on the Mann-Calderon ticket, didn’t share in Hatch’s perspective, and instead said the most important aspect of transparency within UPUA should consist of elevating the organization's visibility among students. Calderon, a second-year studying computer science, said his concern is that a large number of students may be unaware of UPUA and its work.

Again referencing the work she has already done, Hogan said as speaker of the assembly she has implemented new initiatives to improve transparency. The initiatives mentioned include publishing the voice recordings of general assembly meetings, posting meetings agendas to social media and making voting and attendance records public.

What is one specific, tangible policy or initiative that you plan on addressing once you take office, and what steps will you take to ensure it gets done?

Mann started by expressing a deep admiration for the work of the legislative branch, followed by his plan to further connections between said branch and the student body. This includes reaching out to other student councils and student representatives, along with providing opportunities for representatives and fellow students to connect.

Maria Lopez, vice presidential candidate on the Hogan-Lopez ticket, said she would address the safety of international students. Lopez, a third-year studying political science, said she would do so through the creation of safe spaces on campus.

“We have already worked with Student Legal Services,” Lopez said. “We plan to keep on working with them in order to have forums and have discussions and organize these meetings funded by the UPUA, organized by the UPUA, where people can help us make safe spaces.”

Hatch said an initiative he’d like to implement is better promotion of the events of registered student organizations. UPUA does well to ensure these events are funded, but more could be done to garner interest, he added.

What do each one of your tickets feel is the biggest problem of UPUA, and how does your ticket plan to address it?

Hogan said transparency was on her mind, but she also sees a need for more internal efforts to motivate and empower legislatures. She said as president, she would continue showing up to as many meetings as possible across UPUA’s branches.

Hatch built on Hogan’s answer and said stronger leadership would be the best motivator for student representatives. The second part of his response described a fading of passion, both in the UPUA and throughout the student body as a whole.

Mann pointed to the connections the other tickets have within the student government and said if elected, those prior relationships could get in the way of their work. He said his lack of established relationships would allow him to come in unbiased, and help him to better advocate for the student body.

Undergraduate students at University Park will be able to vote digitally through https://vote.psu.edu/. The ballot will be available on Wednesday at 9 a.m. to 9 p.m.

title: Meet Kat Hogan, Maria Lopez: UPUA presidential, vice presidential candidates
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's note: This article is part of the Collegian’s profiles of all candidates in 2026-27 student government presidential election. Read about the Hatch-Smith and Mann-Calderon tickets.

The University Park Undergraduate Association’s (UPUA) election for its 21st assembly features an executive ticket led by two women, Kat Hogan and Maria Lopez.

UPUA holds an annual election, where students run with the intention to serve their peers and represent them at Penn State. This year’s election day is Wednesday, and students can vote online at vote.psu.edu starting at 9 a.m.

Hogan, speaker of the 20th assembly, is running for president alongside Lopez, chair of the Justice and Equity Committee, who is running as vice president.

The 18th assembly ticket was the last time UPUA had an exclusively women-led executive ticket. Lopez, a third-year studying political science and economics, said Nora O’Toole, president of the 18th assembly and Giselle Concepcion, vice president of the 18th assembly, served as role models during her time in First-Year Council (FYC), which allows first-year students to become acquainted with UPUA.

Hogan, a third-year student studying energy engineering, said she hopes to inspire young women to take positions of leadership the way O'Toole and Concepcion inspired her. She said she wants to continue to have a legacy of female leaders in UPUA.

“Being able to continue on that legacy is wonderful,” Lopez said. “It has been wonderful seeing other women support us.”

Noah Youcheff, co-director for campaign integrity for the campaign and vice president of UPUA, said he’s confident both will be excellent leaders due to his experience working with them.

“I really believe in what they're doing,” Youcheff, a third-year student studying security and risk analysis, said. “I believe in their vision and the different things they're pushing out and I believe in them as leaders. I think they're the best people to lead the UPUA going forward. So for me, it was really a no-brainer.”

Youcheff said Hogan thrives on visionary leadership and encourages her peers to “dream big.” Lopez is a practical person who understands the importance of balancing vision with pragmatism, Youcheff added.

“Kat and Maria are two very empowering women — they raise the spirits and inspire others to work harder with everyone around them,” Youcheff said. “I think the all-female ticket is empowering for definitely a lot of people on campus.”

Lopez said having women leadership allows light to be shed on different, new perspectives.

“It’s important for us to be self advocates,” Lopez said. “It’s important for us to then be able to expand onto that and be advocates for one another.”

If elected, Hogan wants to continue her work related to sustainability on campus. In February, she co-authored a bill with David Washabaugh that supported the ban of single-use plastics at Penn State.

Transparency and student engagement are key initiatives of the Hogan-Lopez campaign and possible presidency, according to Lopez. She said she wants to instate monthly town halls in order to foster better communication between UPUA and the student body.

Hogan and Lopez have filled several positions on their campaign and potential presidential cabinet with student leaders. Many of those selected for cabinet positions have both a professional and personal relationship with the pair, according to Lopez.

“We're also looking for internal cohesion,” Hogan said. “Building community and building a team is very important, and you want people that work together well in order to move forward as effectively and efficiently as we can.”

Both Hogan and Lopez started their UPUA journey in FYC, which is also where the pair first met. Hogan was elected to be the at-large representative for the College of Earth and Mineral Sciences after her stint on FYC. She also served as chair of the Justice and Equity Committee, where she said she was able to continue her passion for student advocacy.

Lopez moved from FYC to the representative position for Latino Caucus following an initial struggle to find her niche within UPUA.

“I was able to see how much change I could drive in campus,” Lopez said.

Hogan said her position as the 20th assembly’s speaker allowed her to work closely with the executive members and develop a strong understanding of UPUA’s governing documents.

“As speaker it was a lot of focusing internally and uplifting people internally,” Hogan said. “It made me also realize by working with exec that there are certain issues, not silly issues, but there's things that we can make a lot more efficient and a lot more effective.”

Together the pair said they hope to continue serving the student body and meeting their needs.

title: Meet Tyler Mann and Caleb Calderon: UPUA presidential and vice presidential candidates
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's note: This article is part of the Collegian’s profiles of all candidates in 2026-27 student government presidential election. Read about the Hatch-Smith and Hogan-Lopez tickets.

Tyler Mann and Caleb Calderon are one of three pairs running for president and vice president of the University Park Undergraduate Association's (UPUA) 21st assembly.

UPUA holds an annual election, where students run with the intention to serve their peers and represent them at Penn State. This year’s election day is Wednesday, and students can vote online at vote.psu.edu starting at 9 a.m.

Mann, a third year studying integrative science, and Calderon, a second year studying computer science, have not previously held positions in UPUA, but Mann said it’s an opportunity for new perspectives.

“The UPUA kind of exists in a bubble, and both other tickets also exist within that UPUA bubble,” Mann said. “I think that they have the desire to reach out and collaborate with other organizations. But coming from the outside, I have the perspective of being the organization that the UPUA collaborated with. By bringing in those external leaders to the executive branch, I think we can best shape the UPUA to push that sense of collaboration.”

Uma Aparicio, campaign manager, said the strength of their campaign is the different experiences across campus as leaders and students that are represented within their team.

“I think those attributes really make us stand out,” Aparicio said.

Mann is the current president of the Association of Residence Hall Students (ARHS,) the vice president for Network Excellence in Undergraduate Science and founder of Multicultural Innovators in Computer Science. He said these experiences have prepared him to undertake the role of UPUA president.

“My leadership journey is anything but linear,” Calderon said. “I really liked leading. I like to make an impact on campus. I just like representing communities and really bringing people together.”

The Mann-Calderon campaign seeks to establish a connection between student leaders and the students they represent.

“‘Redefining tradition’ is the tagline for my campaign, because I want to make sure that the UPUA continues to work to represent students and pass amazing legislation,” Mann said. “But I want to make sure that students feel connected to what it is that the UPUA is doing.”

If elected Mann and Calderon plan to strengthen the bonds between UPUA and the student body, according to Aparicio.

“I feel like a lot of people on campus, especially small organizations, feel like they don't have that connection with UPUA,” Aparicio, a fourth-year studying international relations, said. “Hosting more town halls or just things that bridge that connection to the smaller organizations to the UPUA. That way, if they have questions, they can come and ask us.”

On a university-wide level, Calderon said he wants to empower students through a sense of community.

“I think building real community and really turning student voices into action is the most important,” Calderon said. “I've spent my time here on campus creating spaces where people feel like they belong and I really want to bring that same energy to UPUA.”

Awareness and overall student engagement in UPUA are what Mann and Calderon said they’re looking to address. UPUA’s 20th assembly election saw a low voter turnout, with less than 10% of the undergraduate study body casting a vote.

“I think this campus needs to know more about the UPUA,” Aparicio said. “ I've had so many interactions where I've asked people, ‘Do you know what UPUA is?’ the number one answer is literally no.”

Mann said connecting the Penn State community helps increase the visibility of UPUA.

“I think most students don't know how they can benefit from the UPUA, but truly, the UPUA has the capacity to benefit every student,” Mann said. “Every student has the chance to benefit, and every student has a voice.”

title: Penn State baseball closes home stretch against UCF
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State opened its conference series against Illinois with a comeback 9-8 win in the opening game. However, the last two looked completely different. The Nittany Lions lost in mercy-rule fashion in Game 2, followed by a shutout loss to conclude the weekend series.

The blue and white now welcome UCF into Happy Valley for one game. This contest is set to conclude Penn State’s six-game homestand and marks its second Dollar Dog Night of the season.

The Knights arrive leading the Big 12 with a commanding 8-1 conference record, 18-8 overall. They’re fresh off a series sweep over conference opponent Arizona.

A key part of UCF's offense over the weekend was John Smith III. The Apollo Beach, Florida, native hit three homers and batted in eight runners over the three games. The senior leads the team with a .387 batting average.

The Knights enter the game sitting in the middle of the Big 12 with a .295 batting average and .398 on-base percentage.

An offense that has the ability to get on base regularly has been the struggle for Penn State’s pitching staff. After giving up 30 total runs over the weekend to Illinois, the Nittany Lions now hold an 8.36 ERA, last in the Big Ten.

A glimmer of hope in Penn State’s pitching department over the weekend was Mason Butash. In his last two outings, the junior has struck out seven batters while only allowing one earned run.

However, when put up against an offense of UCF’s caliber, the Nittany Lions will need to rely on their offense heavily.

The bats were hot in Game 1 against Illinois, ultimately cooling off in the final two. Michael Anderson continues to dominate at the plate after picking up his 11th home run of the year over the weekend.

The Haverton, Pennsylvania, native looks to lead his offense against a pitching staff that places second in its conference with a 4.24 ERA.

The Knights have given up the second-fewest earned runs and hits in the Big 12. Starter Camden Wicker has been one of the bright spots for UCF’s pitching. The Viera, Florida, native currently has a 2.78 ERA in seven starts. He sits second in strikeouts on the team with 26, first in the starting rotation.

UCF doesn’t struggle when bringing arms out of the bullpen either. Relievers Max Murray and Kevin Schoneboom both have an ERA under three. They combine for 23 strikeouts on the year and lead a bullpen that has no trouble in taking over a game.

A win over one of its toughest challenges of the season would give Penn State momentum heading into a weekend series against a stout conference opponent in Nebraska.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Meet Akito Hatch and Chloe Smith: UPUA presidential and vice presidential candidates
author: Darina Ayazbayeva I For the Collegian
description:
Editor's note: This article is part of the Collegian’s profiles of all candidates in 2026-27 student government presidential election. Read about the Mann-Calderon and Hogan-Lopez tickets.After this story was published, Akito Hatch informed the Collegian that his campaign manager, Josh Morton, who is quoted in this article, has left the campaign. The reason for his departure is currently unclear.

As the University Park Undergraduate Association (UPUA) election approaches, Presidential Candidate Akito Hatch and Vice Presidential Candidate Chloe Smith are focusing their campaign on transparency, accessibility and addressing students’ day-to-day concerns.

UPUA holds an annual election, where students run with the intention to serve their peers and represent them at Penn State. This year’s election day is Wednesday, and students can vote online at vote.psu.edu starting at 9 a.m.

Hatch’s and Smith’s ticket emphasizes breaking down what they describe as a disconnect between UPUA and students, while also prioritizing issues such as housing, tuition and overall cost of living.

Hatch, a third-year studying criminology and political science, said his involvement with UPUA began during his freshman year. Now a longtime member of the organization, Hatch said his decision to run for president was shaped by his experiences within UPUA.

“I’ve seen where UPUA can improve, and I want to help shape a better version of it,” Hatch said.

UPUA oversees funding derived from student fees, which Hatch said makes accountability and outreach critical.

“We’re given about $150,000 annually, students should care where their money is going,” Hatch said. “We need to do a better job representing the entire student body.”

Hatch said their campaign centers on what he described as “micro-level change,” focusing on improvements that directly impact students rather than large-scale initiatives that may take years to implement.

One of those issues is housing. Hatch said students shouldn’t have to struggle to find housing, and they "deserve to have a place to live.”

Hatch also said representation is central to their campaign’s philosophy.

“We want real movement toward representing not only minority groups and people, but also voices and ideas,” Hatch said. “We shouldn’t judge each other based on ideas, but character — and that supports the idea that UPUA represents all voices, not just those interested in governing systems.”

Smith said she entered the campaign with a different perspective, having not previously been involved in UPUA. She said her decision to run alongside Hatch was influenced by her desire to return to a leadership role, and deliver service-oriented work.

“Our campaign is really about breaking down the barrier between UPUA and the students,” Smith, a second-year studying international politics, said. “There are so many students who don't know what it is or what it does.”

While exploring ways to increase representation within UPUA, Smith said their ticket is looking into adding seats to UPUA to offset underrepresentation, including a potential student veteran seat. The pair said trust and transparency are central to their campaign and key reasons students should consider them.

“I think I provide a different perspective,” Hatch said. “I have experience within UPUA, and my running mate brings an outside perspective. That combination allows us to be better at representing the student body.”

Campaign Manager Josh Morton said he joined the campaign after learning more about its platform.

“I wanted to do whatever I could to help support it,” Morton, a third-year studying international politics, said.

Morton said his role includes coordinating outreach efforts, organizing the campaign and preparing candidates for debates.

“It’s stressful, but it’s also really rewarding,” Morton said.

He said Hatch and Smith’s leadership qualities make them strong candidates.

“You can tell they’re both very passionate and genuinely want to help the student body,” Morton said. “They’re very down-to-earth and determined people.”

Morton added that their combination of experience and perspective strengthens the campaign.

“Akito has experience within UPUA, while Chloe brings connections from outside of it,” Morton said. “That creates a good balance.”

Morton said one of the biggest challenges facing the election is low voter turnout. Similarly, Hatch pointed to a steady decline in participation over the years. The total number of voters last year was 3,773 less than the year prior.

Morton said the decline may be tied to a lack of visible results.

“A lot of students haven’t seen real change,” Morton said. “Many past campaigns promised things like lowering tuition but didn’t deliver, so people lost interest.”

Hatch said the lack of voting and general interest in UPUA isn’t the students’ fault, but rather due to UPUA not addressing issues that affect students personally.Despite this, the candidates said student participation in the election remains important.

“Without the students, we have no UPUA,” Smith said. “If students want their concerns to be heard, voting is one of the most direct ways to make that happen.”

Both candidates emphasized a focus on UPUA reconnecting students with their government.

“At the end of the day, it’s not about the title,” Hatch said. “It’s about the impact you can have on people.”

title: Column | Spring revival
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
In today’s modern and non-agrarian society, it may seem odd that people’s lives, ours included, still somewhat revolve around nature and the current climate.

Thus mine and many other’s favorite time of year at Penn State is the cusp of spring’s arrival.

Like clockwork, the first sunny, beautiful days draw students from all over State College onto the HUB lawn to drink in the weather, play outdoor games or simply lie in the grass and read a book purely for the sake of a change of scenery.

After the long, cold winter — a collegiate hibernation of sorts — we all emerge from our warm, but sometimes lonely apartment cocoons and gather together with virtual strangers to enjoy life’s simple pleasures.

Nothing makes me smile more than watching girls tan and boys play ball under the shining sun. Soon the dreary, rainy days of winter will be over for good — until next fall, anyway.

What could be more “college” than pretending our responsibilities away and playing in the sun with other young people? And what feels extra special about these sacred days is the creation of a third space that we so often normally lack.

If home is the first space and work or school is the second, any public gathering spot becomes the third.

Most young people don’t have these so much anymore. Coffee shops are generally for working and bars can be an expensive splurge.

With this collective reemergence, it seems that all the normal social rules relax somewhat. Classes seem a little bit more optional, strangers are friendlier, even professors seem to be in a better mood. And on the lawn itself, kids are yelling as they spike their volleyballs and the music is as loud as can be.

And you can’t really find the same zeal down south. When the weather is nice year round like in Florida, or if the winters are mild and short like in my home state of Georgia, there isn’t this same awakening, if you will.

Everyone doesn’t decide to hang out on the lawn together. Those first few moments of spring aren’t as special as there’s no drastic climatic change.

But here, these moments are so special. Our unspoken, yet similar desires to enjoy nature, though decided upon independently only later becoming a moment of unity, is such a beautiful thing to witness. The palpable joy could bring a smile to anyone’s face.

We’re also unknowingly repeating rituals of years past. Where there were once spring festivals and harvest gatherings, now there are moments of spring playtime and outdoor enjoyment.

Even as society drastically changes, with our everyday lives practically nothing like those of the same relative conditions even a hundred years ago, some things always stay the same.

A beautiful day is a beautiful day, and it simply must be enjoyed.

The next time one of these days rolls around — hopefully sooner rather than later (put that in your pipe and smoke it, Punxsutawney Phil) — drink in more than just the sunshine.

Drink in the camaraderie and drink in the moment, for it’ll be gone sooner than you think.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Review | Audiences get the ‘best of both worlds’ in the ‘Hannah Montana 20th Anniversary Special’
author: Avery Pelletier I The Daily Collegian
description:
Life may be what you make it, but how do you make it rock?

While there are many ways to fulfill this question, the answer most in touch with the origin of this line would be the newly released “Hannah Montana 20th Anniversary Special,” in honor of the popular Disney Channel show that defined a generation.

As someone who had a “Hannah Montana” inspired senior quote, this special did meet my expectations, though there were moments for improvement.

Hosted by Miley Cyrus, the original star of “Hannah Montana,” this documentary transports you back in time with her Hannah-esque look, complete with the fictional popstar's signature blonde wig and bangs.

The modern twist, however, lies with the co-host and interviewer of this special, a self proclaimed longtime fan, Alex Cooper, host of the popular podcast “Call Her Daddy.”

The special opens with a heartwarming cover of the show’s original theme song, “The Best of Both Worlds,” that immediately sets the tone for the special and shows how in touch Cyrus is with her original character. Following this performance, Cyrus gave an incredibly emotional walkthrough of the original kitchen and living room set, the backdrop of the first scene of the show.

During her first interview with Cooper, Cyrus explained her auditioning process and how the character audiences know and love came to be. These moments show just how young Cyrus was when the show started and how her childhood felt very similar to that of her character.

The middle portion of the show, however, is where I felt it began to fall short.

Billy Ray Cyrus, who is Miley Cyrus’ father on and off screen, made a touching tribute. The father and daughter duo both got the chance to share their experiences of filming the show together. In the original, Billy Ray Cyrus played the part of a loving father and moral guidepost for Miley Stewart and in their interview viewers got to see how this translated to real life.

Tish Cyrus, Cyrus’ mom, not only shared her memories of Cyrus being on the show but also shared how she played a role in the show’s fashion. With a cameo from Hannah Montana’s original closet, viewers were let in on behind-the-scenes secrets of how Cyrus’ own mother helped costume her character’s alter ego.

However, while these cameos felt very authentic to the original, the special could’ve done a better job of incorporating some of the show’s other actors into the special. Beyond clips being shown, Billy Ray Cyrus was the only other original cast member included.

Additionally, the setup of the conversations could’ve been more authentic to the original. While it was touching to see a fan get to interview Cyrus, the special would have benefited overall from hearing group discussions from the cast to explore why the original was so impactful.

Despite these weaknesses, one of the strongest aspects of the special was the music.

Cyrus’ covered classic Hannah Montana songs, and the performances were heartwarming and memorable. Starting out with “The Best of Both Worlds” was incredibly fitting, as every episode included it.

The most surprising moment occurred when Cyrus sang “This Is the Life” halfway through the special. Even though this song was the first featured in the original show, it often goes overlooked by many fans. Incorporating this song made the special feel more authentic and focused on celebrating the show as a whole.

Next came “The Climb” a fan-favorite song from “Hannah Montana: The Movie” where Cyrus showed her true talent in the emotional storytelling of her character climbing through life’s many challenges.

Lastly, the final moments of this anniversary special are what I found to be arguably the best and most emotional. In the final moments, Cyrus debuted a new original song called “Younger You” as a tribute to her younger self.

This moment was a tear-jerker and showed how Hannah Montana impacted both audiences and Cyrus.

While it may have relied heavily on nostalgia, what this special got right wasn’t trying to add on to the original, but focusing on what made it so great in the first place.

In the age of live-action remakes and reboots, this special knows there’s no way to recreate the one-in-a-million original, but there’s a way to honor it.

Even if it may have lacked the original actors who helped make this show so nostalgic, the “Hannah Montana 20th Anniversary Special” incorporated new moments with old to truly make it the best of both worlds.

MORE BLOGS

title: Weekly recap | Penn State softball sweeps Rutgers in Big Ten road series
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State gained momentum against its Big Ten rival, defeating Rutgers for an away series to complete a three-game win streak.

The blue and white took a trip to Piscataway, New Jersey, to face the Scarlet Knights for its third weekend of conference play in the 2026 season. Penn State looked to bounce back after a 1-5 skid in weekends against Wisconsin and Oregon.

Facing issues with consistency on both sides of the ball, the Nittany Lions' performance during their trip to the Garden State was a big deciding factor on how the course of the rest of their season would go, especially during Big Ten play.

Here is a look at what went well for the blue and white against longtime foe Rutgers.

Freshmen led way

In the 2026 campaign, the Nittany Lions’ roster gained seven freshmen and its those players who have set off the historic season.

Starting the weekend off, freshman shortstop Allison Onearce took control of the Rutgers Softball Complex Friday night as she became the third Nittany Lion to have more than one multi-homer in a single season.

The Alpharetta, Georgia, native finished her first game against the Scarlet Knights, going 2-for-3 with four RBIs, scoring two runs and hitting two homers, securing the 8-0 win.

For Saturday’s meeting, it was a constant back and forth between the blue and white and the Scarlet Knights. It was freshman first baseman Gabby Gradishar who solidified the win for Penn State.

At the top of the seventh, the Champion, Ohio, native stepped into the batter's box with two outs and swung at her biggest pitch of the season, hitting her second collegiate home run to left-center field. This resulted in the Nittany Lions taking the lead and eventually winning 7-6.

Gradishar finished the day going 3-for-4 with two RBIs and a homer, turning in her most dominant individual performance of her collegiate career.

To close out its weekend in New Jersey, Penn State took its second consecutive series sweep against Rutgers, winning Sunday's midday matchup 7-5.

Logging six hits on the day from six different Nittany Lions, a solo home run from freshman left fielder Breanna Hanik solidified the game in the top of the seventh. She finished the day 1-for-4, adding two runs on the scoreboard and an RBI on the fourth homer of her rookie season.

Penn State sailed to Piscataway for a Big Ten series weekend and came out with a very strong offensive performance. A needed result for the blue and white, as it looked strong throughout the entire course of the game, especially with offensive sparks coming alive in the later innings from the freshmen. The Nittany Lions outscored Rutgers 22-11 over the weekend, with the defense also coming alive, as well.

Consistency in the circle

The biggest struggle the blue and white have faced is the lack of consistency in the circle late in the game, starting strong and then falling apart.

Success and dominance are what came out of the circle this past weekend. Starting strong as a pair of right-handers for the Nittany Lions, shut out the Scarlet Knights for the series opener.

Juniors Bridget Nemeth and McKenna Young got the job done on Friday's game. Nemeth got the start, allowing one hit, a walk and striking out two batters, finishing the night with a total of 34 pitches.

Young came in for relief, throwing for a total of 5.0 innings and finishing with a clean scoreboard at the end of the first game of the series. The Brodhead, Wisconsin, native matched her season high mark in innings pitched while surpassing her season best with six strikeouts.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton stepped into the circle for the last three innings to halt production from the up-and-down Rutgers offense. Britton sent six batters to the bench after striking them out, allowing an earned run and a hit.

To close out the series, the blue and white had four pitchers hit the circle on Sunday. The only lefty pitcher on the Penn State roster, freshman Brevyn Kellepouris got the start. In that outing, she had a rare start and save, tallying her fifth in both figures.

Kellepouris totaled 64 pitches in Sunday's game, pitching 2.1 innings, surrendering three walks, three hits, two earned runs and a single strikeout. Young earned the credit of the win as the most efficient reliever, tossing 1.2 scoreless innings with zero walks, a single hit and three strikeouts.

Penn State used the depth of its bullpen throughout the weekend, especially on the series finale, bringing life back to the squad as the defense stayed locked.

This past weekend was the exact performance and confidence boost the Nittany Lions needed to have after shortcomings. The blue and white are now 4-5 in conference play and 23-11 overall on the season.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘He’s redefined what the bar even is’ | Sprinter Zaid Nasrawi elevates Penn State club track
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
During Penn State Track and Field Club’s first practice of the season, sprints coach Zaid Nasrawi held a thick binder in his hands which contained an exercise program for the entire season. The act showcased Nasrawi's unwavering devotion to the club team.

A prominent rising star at Penn State’s club level, currently holding the indoor 200m, 4x200m and outdoor 4x100m club records, Nasrawi, a second-year studying mechanical engineering, was named the sprints coach for the 2025-26 season by club president Michael Rohosky, after a successful freshman campaign.

“We choose coaches for all of our event groups, and looking at sprints, Zaid is incredibly dedicated to his craft and to the club,” Rohosky told The Daily Collegian. “It wasn’t really even a question of who I was going to ask to do it.”

Penn State’s club track and field men’s team is the two-time defending National Intercollegiate Running Association (NIRCA) national champions, searching for a third consecutive title while the women’s team placed second at nationals in 2025. Although the club has dominated the national level, Rohosky and Nasrawi knew that changes had to be made to further legitimize the program.

From the beginning of the season, Nasrawi made it a point to improve the structure of the sprint group. Instead of crafting workouts on the fly — as was customary in the past — the sophomore employed a tactical method formed with Olympian Simon Hansen’s 100m program as a baseline.

Nasrawi focused on altering the details of the program to suit individual needs, stating that different runners take the sport more or less seriously than others.

“Zaid really took our sprints program above and beyond this year,” Rohosky said. “I can’t thank him enough for it and the team can’t thank him enough for it. The group has grown so much; they’re so much more of a family now, so that change has been appreciated. Now it’s a lot more organized.”

Serina Julien, the club’s recruitment chair as well as a sprinter, said that she “flourished through (Nasrawi’s) program.” Julien recently broke the club’s women’s indoor 200m record with a 25.67 time, citing Nasrawi’s coaching as a major factor.

“The binder was a testament to how much he cared. It shows so much who he is as a person. He’s here and he’s not playing around,” Julien said. “I knew at the start of the season that there were going to be amazing things happening all around. And it did, especially for him.”

Nasrawi, represented Team Jordan at the 2026 World Athletics Indoor Championships in Toruń, Poland, running the 60m sprint on an international scale against some of the fastest athletes in the world.

He was first notified of the possibility of competing for Team Jordan in November 2025, when his national coach called him to say that if he performed well in the indoor season, he’d have a chance to run on the international level. However, Nasrawi wasn’t yet informed which tournament he was striving for until he’d solidified his position as one of the fastest sprinters in Jordan.

At the Happy Valley Invitational, Nasrawi ran a time of 6.95 seconds in the 60m, securing his spot on the team for the tournament. The time ranked second all-time in the men’s indoor 60m, only 0.03 behind the club record.

It was then that he was told he’d be competing in the largest indoor track and field competition in the world, hosted biennially.

“The first thing that I felt was it was like one of those clips in the NFL Draft — getting hit up and it’s ‘We want you,’” Nasrawi said. “Reality set in and I knew that I needed to dial up my training and tune up the fine details of my routine so that I was at my best for the tournament.”

It was his second time competing for Team Jordan — the first in the 2023 Asian U18 Athletics Championships held in Tashkent, Uzbekistan.

“Coming into (the World Indoor Championships,) knowing that I’ve matured a lot more, it felt like there was a lot more pressure on my shoulders,” Nasrawi said. “It was on a way bigger stage and a lot of people back home were watching.”

Prior to the tournament, Nasrawi went through two days of international travel, landing in Poland a few days before the official meet. Before racing he watched the other competitors prepare for their events, soaking up as much knowledge as he could to take back to Penn State.

Members of the club woke up at 4 a.m. to watch their teammate and coach race against the world’s best. Nasrawi said that he “loves the club” for always supporting him.

In Poland, Nasrawi ran a 7.04 in the 60m, which was his third-fastest time of the season. Nasrawi’s first course of action when he returned home was to share what he learned with his teammates.

“During practice, he’s a supportive coach,” Julien said. “When we’re doing workouts he never gets frustrated with anybody. If he’s doing something new, changing workouts or anything, he always explains why we’re doing it, which I appreciate.”

Julien said that Nasrawi through his work ethic on the national and international stage is putting the team on the map.

“Having him do that is a testament to not only us, but to other club teams,” Julien said. “It breaks down barriers to show people that you don’t need to be D-I or on scholarship to get to those high places.”

Nasrawi still has two more years in Happy Valley to hone his skills and train the club’s sprinters. He and the club have their sights set on potentially completing a three-peat at the 2026 NIRCA National Championships held in Olivet, Michigan, from April 11-12.

Following nationals, Nasrawi said his goal is to join Penn State’s varsity program.

“He’s redefined what the bar even is, not even just raising it,” Rohosky said.

MORE SPORTS CONTENT

title: Recruiting roundup | Where Penn State men’s basketball stands with Dylan Mingo, names to watch
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
If Penn State is going to improve from its 12-20 record from this season, recruiting would be a key part in making that happen.

The transfer portal is set to officially open on April 7, though players can announce their intentions to enter prior to the 15-day window. The Nittany Lions have already been linked to a portal prospect, and they could make a push to flip a highly rated high school player in the 2026 class.

Here’s some early names to watch with offseason recruiting picking up in the coming weeks.

Dylan Mingo

Penn State missed out on 5-star 2026 guard Dylan Mingo when he committed to North Carolina in February, but his recruitment might not be set in stone just yet.

The Tar Heels recently parted ways with head coach Hubert Davis, bringing into question whether Mingo will stick with his original commitment. College basketball insider Adam Zagoria reported on March 25 that it’s “too early to tell” what Mingo will do with his recruitment following the coaching change.

What also hurt UNC’s chances in keeping Mingo on board is assistant coach Marcus Paige departing the Tar Heels to take a job on the Charlotte coaching staff, according to Joe Tipton of On3. Paige was one of the key reasons why Mingo committed to UNC, so this change could lead to Mingo reopening his recruitment.

If Mingo were to back off on his commitment to the Tar Heels, Penn State would be right in the mix again. He said that the Nittany Lions finished second in his recruitment when he committed to UNC, and he’s the younger brother of Penn State star guard Kayden Mingo.

It’s worth noting that the regular signing period begins on April 15, so Mingo has some time to evaluate his options before officially signing with a team. He would become the Nittany Lions’ highest-rated recruit of all time if he were to come to Happy Valley.

Portal names to watch

Indiana University of Pennsylvania (Division II) transfer Dallis Dillard has received interest from Penn State among several other teams, according to The Portal Report. Dillard was a standout for the Crimson Hawks this season, averaging 20.6 points, 5.8 rebounds, 4.7 assists and 2.5 steals per game.

Dillard started 32 games in 2025-26, and he’s in the portal as a graduate transfer. His career started in 2021-22, but he missed the majority of the 2023-24 season due to injury and didn’t play in any game in 2024-25.

As the portal has yet to officially open for Division I players, Dillard is the only player to reportedly get interest from the Nittany Lions. However, there’s a former Penn State transfer target who’s planning on entering the portal in Virginia Tech guard Izaiah Pasha.

Pasha transferred to the Hokies last offseason after spending his freshman year with Delaware in 2024-25. During his portal recruitment last offseason, Pasha took a visit to Penn State and included the Nittany Lions as a finalist.

As such, it’s possible there could be mutual interest again this time around. Pasha is a native of Harrisburg, Pennsylvania, and while he only averaged 2.0 points per game across 18 appearances with Virginia Tech, he was the CAA Rookie of the Year with the Blue Hens in 2024-25.

With Penn State guard Melih Tunca planning on entering the portal, the Nittany Lions likely will need to find a guard with ball-handling potential to back up starting point guard Kayden Mingo. Pasha can offer that as he had a season-high six assists in Virginia Tech’s win over UMES on Dec. 14, and he averaged 3.9 assists per game with Delaware.

There’s also VCU forward Christian Fermin, who is reportedly planning on entering the transfer portal, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready. Fermin played for Mike Rhoades during his freshman year with the Rams in 2022-23, giving Penn State a connection to him.

Rhoades has mentioned the need to add size to the team, and Fermin would fit that as he’s listed at 6-foot-10 and 225 pounds. He’s also a native of Tobyhanna, Pennsylvania, so it’s possible he could be interested in a return to his home state.

Fermin boasts some shot blocking, having averaged 1.2 blocks per game across 90 appearances in his career. This would help fill a need for the Nittany Lions, as they only had 1.7 blocks per game as a team this season.

The senior has one season of eligibility left after redshirting in 2025-26, as he only played in four games. Fermin has averaged 4.0 points and 3.1 rebounds per game in his career.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘I think everybody forgot my name’ | Fetty Wap loves, laments on new studio album ‘Zavier’
author: Ashlyn Kafer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Yeah, baby. Fetty Wap is back and freer than ever.

Fetty Wap, born William Junior Maxwell II, was arrested and convicted in 2023 on drug trafficking charges, and was released early from federal prison in January of this year. Jumping on the opportunity, Fetty Wap immediately dove back into his passion for music.

“Zavier,” released on March 27, takes a different direction from the popular “Trap Queen” and “679” that have boosted Fetty Wap’s name to the top of pregame playlists and DJ setlists. Honing an R&B sound and bringing back his rap-centric flow, he sings throughout the album about unforgiving love and the desire to rectify his past indiscretions.

Fetty Wap joked that the album was written in three days following his Jan. 7 release, featuring 17 tracks and taking control of audiences for 58 minutes.

The album opens with “Right Back To You,” entering with a groovy melody reminiscent of The Weeknd. That is until Fetty Wap’s characteristic “yeah” cuts through the music, breaking into a mix of traditional hip-hop beats with a smooth singing style and steady harmonies throughout the track.

“Right Back To You” plays into Fetty Wap’s release from prison, expressing “I've been gone for a while, but I came right back for you.” The song sets the tone for the rest of the album, entertaining the idea of unspoken feelings festering behind bars.

The next two tracks, “Like a Taylor” and “Real Love,” are slow, seductive and heavily inspired by R&B, going between nostalgia about feeling love under the influence of substances to being intoxicated by natural feelings of love for another.

“Like a Taylor” builds throughout the track, featuring Wiz Khalifa for a bridge breakdown that separates the earnest and honeyed vocals of Fetty Wap, while “Real Love” incorporates his melismatic singing to match the powerful, ballad-ready voice of featured artist Honey Bxby.

Following is “White Roses,” combining pop with jazz elements to create a sultry, yet invigorating, piece that captivates listeners with its change of direction from the typical rap or hip-hop sound Fetty Wap has played into in previous albums.

“Nasty” and “N LUV” connect the past to the present, allowing older fans expectant of his quick and catchy lyricism and fast-paced backtrack to be entertained with the new horizons Fetty Wap is embracing. The tracks feel mismatched among the other songs that flow together with their polished and mellow sound, and the lyrics get lost within the cacophony of beats without intentionality.

The album flips again on “Spot Back,” sensual and infused with love and wanting for another. The track combines bass-forward beats with Rob McCoy’s silky tone, pairing alongside Fetty Wap’s full-bodied timbre as he sings about true passion.

“Favorite Girl” and “Fool For You” further transition back into the even and effortless tone that the album began with. “Favorite Girl” plays in between hip-hop and R&B with its steady harmonics and takes a step back from the melodic singing style Fetty Wap adopted throughout the majority of the album.

If “Fool For You” were to be played without the listener knowing the artist, it may be entirely impossible to tell the track is being sung by Fetty Wap. It takes an approach similar to Dean Blunt or Leon Thomas by emphasizing the lyrical message and showcasing the artistry that had been hiding within Fetty Wap for years.

“Fool For You” sings of regret for making a fool out of a lover and being childish, knowing exactly what you have lost when it's too late, expressing “Just know the love you left will always linger.”

The album makes a switch in “Say When” and “With It Or What,” bringing the vibe of the album up and taking listeners back to 2015, leaning less into soft riffs but shifting into quick hits and allowing the conversation about Fetty Wap’s past to begin.

“BossDon,” “Eastside Mz” and “LYG” blend into each other, feeling out of place among the other songs that highlight yearning, the intensity of love unspoken and coming back to the one you love even after an absence.

Though, this switch is intentional, highlighting Fetty Wap’s life of frivolous spending, substance use and playing with the hearts of people in his life by using the traditional sounds of his past in juxtaposition with the slower tracks at the front of the album.

The album continues with “Real Ones” and “Never Tell,” telling the story of Fetty Wap’s feelings about the mistakes he made that landed him in federal prison, explaining in the dense and shame-filled “Never Tell” that he had to “Keep my mouth duct-taped, I will never rat /

Never squeal, no, I will never tell / I'd rather see a box or spend my life in jail.”

Knowing the blame could be placed on no one else but himself, the album ends with “I Remember/Dear Zavier.” The track features G Herbo, who sings the majority of the lyrics that recount Fetty Wap’s life and rise to fame.

Then the song changes. It becomes heartfelt and acoustically-driven, bringing in Fetty Wap’s vocals, crooning that “I think everybody forgot my name,” showing appreciation for the ones who stuck by him despite his wrongdoings.

“Zavier” takes several twists and turns, introducing a new era of Fetty Wap while acknowledging the faults that got him in trouble to begin with.

The album highlights the mostly unheard vocal ability of Fetty Wap, emphasizing gentle yet impressive riffs and injecting emotion into each note as he moves on from his dangerous past and focuses on the love that surrounds him.

title: Sights and sounds from State College 'No Kings' protest
author: Jailyn Sharpe | The Daily Collegian
description: On March 28, State College community members came together at Old Main for the third 'No Kings' protest. The No Kings movement aims to unite Americans against “chaos, corruption and authority,” according to its website. The first protest occurred in June and the second was in October.

title: Recruiting roundup | Penn State kicks off spring practice with 1st set of visitors, 2027 CB commitment
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Spring ball is back for Penn State, which means there’s plenty of recruits flocking the sidelines to get a glimpse of what the program is about.

So far, the Nittany Lions have had a 2027 quarterback on campus, dished out a handful of notable offers and landed their first recruit.

Here’s where things stand after Week 1 of spring ball.

2027 quarterback board

Penn State hosted William Wood on Saturday, a 3-star prospect out of Xaverian Brothers High School in Massachusetts.

The Westwood, Massachusetts, native holds offers from 13 schools, including Wake Forest, North Carolina, West Virginia and Stanford. He left Happy Valley without publicly announcing an offer, but he will be a name to monitor.

The two names at the top of the board are a pair of 4-stars and top-100 players in Peter Bourque and Will Mencl, who are both expected to visit in the middle of April.

Bourque was the top target from James Franklin’s Penn State staff, but ultimately committed to Michigan. He later backed off of his pledge following the removal of head coach Sherrone Moore and the hire of Kyle Whittingham from Utah.

The staff stopped by Tabor Academy in Massachusetts, where Bourque plays, in January. Penn State will have to battle Georgia, Virginia Tech and Washington for his services, with a recent offer from Miami potentially playing a factor.

Mencl is a newer name to the fold after being offered on Jan. 20. The Chandler, Arizona, native has yet to be on campus, but it’s worth noting Mouser’s also from Arizona. Per Steve Wiltfong of On3, Penn State, Miami, Oregon and Auburn are teams to watch.

Four-star Ole Miss commit Keegan Croucher, who was a top target by the old staff, holds Penn State’s only public quarterback offer. He’s transferred from Cheshire Academy in Connecticut to the Baylor School in Tennessee, which the staff visited in January.

If Penn State wants to make a run at Croucher, it won’t be the only school, as Wiltfong reported Kentucky and Indiana are in the mix.

The only other quarterback to have an offer is 3-star Boise State commit Kael Snyder, per On3. He hasn’t announced the offer himself.

Notable visitors/offers

The first notable visitor came on the 2027 front, with 4-star wide receiver Khalil Taylor in attendance for practice on Thursday. The Gibsonia, Pennsylvania, native is a former Penn State commit and scheduled an official visit following junior day in January.

He’s likely the top wideout on the board and has been to campus 15 times. Despite the coaching turnover a season ago, including wide receivers coach Kashif Moore replacing Noah Pauley last month, Taylor has consistently been a priority since he emerged on the recruiting scene.

Sticking with 4-star pass-catchers, Isaiah Alvarez and Cooper Ohnmacht were also in the building this week. Alvarez was a target by Franklin’s staff, while Ohnmacht has emerged within the last few months after previously receiving interest from Iowa State. Ohnmacht is listed as an athlete, as he also plays safety.

On the defensive side of the ball, a pair of in-state 2027 prospects made it in 4-star linebacker Brandon Lockley Jr. and 3-star Zachary Gleason Jr.

Lockley plays for St. Joe’s Prep in Philadelphia, while Gleason plays for Central Catholic in Pittsburgh.

To round out 2027 products, 3-star cornerback Roman Combs made his first trip to campus, which led to an offer and a scheduled official visit. His 6-foot-4, 200-pound frame projects him as a fit in the new staff’s movement to get lengthier in the secondary.

For the 2028 group, 4-star cornerback commit Deonte Flemings Jr. highlighted the bunch. Flemings wasn’t the only high-profile cornerback visiting, as 5-star Langston Pridgeon and 4-star Nasir Richardson both made it to campus.

A pair of in-state 2028 prospects in attendance were quarterback James Armstrong and offensive tackle Tyrie Davis-Winn.

Armstrong is a native of Aliquippa and was offered by the old staff. He was re-offered at spring practice visit this week.

Davis-Winn left with an offer. The Drexel Hill, Pennsylvania, native stands at 6-foot-8 and weighs 270 pounds.

Semajay Robinson

Penn State landed its first commitment in the 2027 class, with 4-star cornerback Semajay Robinson leaving his first trip to campus by announcing his pledge to the Nittany Lions.

There clearly was a strong rapport with position coach Terry Smith, as the Fort Lauderdale, Florida, prospect only earned an offer on Jan. 27.

There are still ways to go with this recruitment, as the lower-ranked recruit will likely have more teams look his way with National Signing Day months away. He holds a Florida State offer, so it’ll be interesting to see what he does if the Seminoles push harder, or if Miami and Florida make a move.

While that’s still a hypothetical, Smith has proven he can land highly touted talent with national recruiting attention, as he did with sophomore Daryus Dixson.

Smith has a track record of being an elite recruiter and developer of talent, and with Robinson moving quickly despite a mostly over-the-phone relationship with Smith, Penn State is in a strong position moving forward.

As far as what Robinson brings to the field, he’s a lengthy prospect at 6-foot-2.5, 190 pounds. He’s physical off the line of scrimmage, has solid change of direction and is light on his feet in zone coverage.

If you’re not a physical player, Smith won’t take a chance on you. Consider that, along with the new staff’s emphasis on length in the secondary, and Robinson seems like a good start for the future of Penn State.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball stars Sean Harvey, Owen Rose awarded weekly EIVA honors
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Two Nittany Lions took home this week's EIVA awards.

This week’s EIVA award winners helped @PennStateMVBALL to 3 wins vs. ranked opponents:🏐 Offensive Player of the WeekSean Harvey39 kills, .411 Hit%, 1 ace, 12 digs🏐 Defensive Player of the WeekOwen Rose15 kills, .619 Hit%, 4 aces, 16 blockshttps://t.co/Nlcr40r3MA— EIVA Volleyball (@EIVAVolleyball) March 30, 2026

Sean Harvey was named EIVA Offensive Player of the Week following the blue and white's three-game stretch against ranked opponents. The redshirt sophomore had a total of 39 kills across the three games, as well as 12 digs, one ace and a .411 hitting percentage.

But Harvey wasn't the only award winner for Penn State this week.

Owen Rose took home the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week award. The middle blocker had 15 kills, 16 blocks, four aces and a .619 hitting percentage after the three games.

Together, the pair helped the blue and white to get on a three-game winning streak. Now 14-7, the Nittany Lions will be back in action for a two-game road series at 7 p.m. Friday and 3 p.m. Saturday against Charleston.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Mitchell Mesenbrink wins Dan Hodge Trophy
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State has its sixth winner of the most prestigious award in college wrestling.

National champion Mitchell Mesenbrink was named the winner of the Hodge Trophy on Monday afternoon with 66 first-place votes. The announcement was made on Flo Wrestling's radio live.

The award is given to the best wrestler in the country based on record, dominance/bonus-point percentage, quality of competition and sportsmanship.

Mesenbrink was purely dominant against his weight class, something that isn’t seen too often. He notched a 96.3% bonus rate, winning just one match by decision during the season.

The 165-pounder cruised to his second national title, holding a commanding 27-0 record on the season. He capped off his immaculate season with a dominant tech fall over Iowa’s Mikey Caliendo, who’s been considered his toughest competitor during this run, though Mesenbrink beat him nine times in college.

The redshirt junior held a commanding lead in the fan vote, with 68% of the votes going to him, with a total of over 20,000. Oklahoma State’s Jax Forrest had four first-place votes while Josh Barr had one vote.

Other Penn State wrestlers who received fan votes were Levi Hains with 1,427 votes and Luke Lilledahl with 102.

The Hartland, Wisconsin, native joins Aaaron Brooks in 2024, Bo Nickal in 2019, Zain Retherford in 2018 and 2018, David Taylor in 2014 and 2012 and Kerry McCoy in 1997.

The Nittany Lions continue to hold the record for most Hodge winners with six, leading Oklahoma State and Iowa, who both have three in the history of the trophy.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey's Matt DiMarsico signs with Colorado Avalanche
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn State player is off to the NHL system.

The Colorado Avalanche have signed Penn State forward Matt DiMarsico to an entry-level contract beginning in the 2026-27 season. DiMarsico will join the Colorado Eagles on an AHL contract for the remainder of the 2025-26 campaign.

We have signed forward Matthew DiMarsico to a two-year, entry-level contract beginning in the 2026-27 season. He will join the Colorado Eagles on an AHL contract for the remainder of the 2025-26 season. pic.twitter.com/l9knVFSVuM— x - Colorado Avalanche (@Avalanche) March 30, 2026

The Wexford, Pennsylvania, native’s production shot up after being paired with Charlie Cerrato and JJ Wiebusch last season, leading to career years for DiMarsico. In his junior campaign in 2025-26, DiMarsico put up 18 goals and 24 assists for 42 points in 37 games played. DiMarsico scored 100 points during his time in blue and white.

On March 30, 2025, exactly one year before the signing, DiMarsico scored the overtime goal in Allentown that sent Penn State to its first ever Frozen Four.

DiMarsico joins Kevin Reidler as the two Nittany Lions to sign entry-level deals with NHL teams.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Report: Former Penn State men's basketball forward Kachi Nzeh will enter transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State forward Kachi Nzeh is set to search for his fourth collegiate team.

Nzeh, who played at Little Rock this season, will enter the transfer portal, according to Dushawn London of 247Sports.

Little Rock forward Kachi Nzeh will enter the transfer portal per his managers Zeke Rogen and Patrick Kelly of 2020 Managment.Nzeh averaged 11.7 points and 4.8 rebounds this season. pic.twitter.com/IDgsK52Gae— Dushawn London (@DushawnLondon1) March 30, 2026

Nzeh averaged 11.7 points and 4.8 rebounds per game across 32 starts for the Trojans this season. He scored a career-high 30 points in a loss to Arkansas State on Dec. 6.

The Upper Darby, Pennsylvania, native played one season at Penn State in 2024-25, averaging 2.6 points and 2.2 rebounds per game across 29 appearances. He was the primary backup for 7-footer Yanic Konan Niederhauser that year, making two starts and averaged 11.3 minutes per game.

Nzeh spent his freshman year at Xavier, where he averaged 2.5 points and 2.5 rebounds per game in 19 games played.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey goaltender Kevin Reidler agrees to terms with Ottawa Senators
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State goaltender Kevin Reidler is going pro.

Another day, another giant (6'6") joining the ranks!The #Sens have agreed to terms on a two-year, entry-level contract with goaltender Kevin Reidler ✍️ pic.twitter.com/Ga6bMY7iuw— Ottawa Senators (@Senators) March 30, 2026

The 6-foot-6 sophomore netminder agreed on contract terms on an entry-level deal with the Ottawa Senators after being drafted by the team in the fifth round of the 2022 NHL Draft.

The Gävle, Sweden, native was a transfer addition this year from Nebraska-Omaha, and split most of the season in net with fellow goaltender Josh Fleming.

In 18 games, Reidler posted a .901 save percentage and 3.30 goals against average, finishing with an 11-7 record.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's volleyball to play in Wrigley Field against Kentucky
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will be playing in an outdoor game next season.

The Nittany Lions will take on Kentucky in Wrigley Field on Sept. 6.

Volleyball at The Friendly Confines‼️We play Kentucky at Wrigley Field on Sept. 6 🏐#WeAre pic.twitter.com/9GRmkVoPob— Penn State Women’s Volleyball (@PennStateVBALL) March 30, 2026

This will be the first time that the blue and white will play an outdoor game.

It'll be a homecoming for multiple Penn State members. Coach Katie Schumacher-Cawley will coach in her hometown, while junior Ava Falduto and freshman Haley Burgdorf will play in their home state at the Chicago Cubs' home stadium.

The Nittany Lions and Wildcats have played each of the last two seasons, with the blue and white taking the 2024 matchup in a reverse sweep in Lexington, and Kentucky getting revenge in 2025 with a sweep in Rec Hall.

The teams will be a part of the Big Ten/SEC Volleyball Challenge Week, with Nebraska and Missouri playing in the other match. The match will be at 7 p.m. or 9 p.m. EST on Fox.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's lacrosse stays stagnant in latest poll
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Despite earning its third straight loss on Sunday, things are staying steady for Penn State.

The Nittany Lions remained at No. 21 in the latest IWLCA poll on Monday after falling 14-13 to now No. 1 Maryland.

NEWS - @MarylandWLax Rises to No. 1 in Division I Coaches PollFull release: https://t.co/EY4aVJHGlt pic.twitter.com/4L1Bnbu1al— IWLCA (@IWLCA) March 30, 2026

The blue and white have been 21st in the country for the last three weeks, despite losing in each game since, including three straight ranked opponents.

Penn State will continue its stand against ranked opponents when it takes on No. 25 Ohio State on Sunday.

title: SEE IT: Penn State women's lacrosse's Rocquette Allen earns spot on SportsCenter's Top 10
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian

Freshman Rocquette Allen checked in at No. 2 on the SportsCenter's Top 10 with her backwards, lying down goal.

duh 🤷‍♀️Checking in at number ✌️ on #SCTop10 pic.twitter.com/VK3LQmX6mo— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) March 30, 2026
