title: 'We came up a little bit short' | Penn State men's hockey's loss left expectations unfulfilled
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — Penn State outspent nearly every team in Division I hockey this year.

Its prize: One season with Gavin McKenna, the consensus No. 1 overall pick in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft, who brought more eyes and greater expectations to the program.

Penn State fans lauded the move. Many believed it signaled a willingness to go “all in” on winning a national championship.

But after Friday’s 3-1 loss to Minnesota Duluth, the Nittany Lions’ spending spree yielded a mere first-round exit — two rounds worse than last year’s Frozen Four run.

Before the NCAA Tournament, Guy Gadowsky said the pressure surrounding the program was real — a learning experience for a team not used to that much national attention. But he downplayed the need for the Nittany Lions to match, if not improve upon, last year’s Frozen Four appearance.

His stance remained unchanged following Penn State’s first-round exit.

“There's eyes on us now. There's new expectations. A lot of things happened that put eyes on us. And it was a learning experience,” Gadowsky said. “Would I go back and do some things a little differently? Sure, but it was our first time as a program through this. I'm just so proud of the guys for grinding through all of that. And for them to make it here, I know we didn't get back to the Frozen Four, but to make the tournament is a heck of an accomplishment.”

The Nittany Lions reportedly spent “in the ballpark” of $700,000 to land McKenna, according to ESPN, outbidding Big Ten rival Michigan State. It was an NIL figure never seen before in college hockey, and a statement to fellow contenders that Penn State was all in on winning a national title.

They also added defenseman Jackson Smith, who was selected No. 14 overall by the Columbus Blue Jackets in the 2025 NHL Entry Draft. Insert Smith and McKenna's talent to a veteran roster, and Gadowsky had built a team picked by many to make a deep postseason run.

And it wasn’t just outsiders who placed high expectations on Penn State.

Gadowsky said in September that the goal was to return to the Frozen Four. He mentioned later in the season that the final thing left on the program's checklist was a national championship, which the Nittany Lions eyed after a seven-game winning streak in January.

But amid a plethora of injuries and under an immense spotlight from September through March, Penn State’s season fell short of its ultimate goal.

“We came up a little bit short,” Reese Laubach said. “It hurts. None of us are going to forget this feeling right now in the locker room, hugging guys, crying like that. There's a lot of motivation to work through the summer and beginning of the year to get back to that point and go even farther. I have no doubt in my mind with the group we got coming back, we will be back.”

Laubach is correct. While McKenna is a near lock to turn pro, and Charlie Cerrato and Aiden Fink could follow by signing entry-level contracts, next year’s roster is poised to retain several key members.

Smith told The Daily Collegian he will return for his sophomore season. Laubach all but said he’ll be back as well. Add in captain Dane Dowiak and goaltender Josh Fleming, who stopped 36 of the 38 shots he faced against Minnesota Duluth, and the Nittany Lions could be dangerous once more.

Gadowsky called this season a “transformational year,” one that taught both he and the program several lessons they hope to carry into next season.

“Ever since we got to the Frozen Four, it has been a whirlwind of attention, eyeballs, expectations, all of it,” Gadowsky said. “I learned a lot this year. This year, it was tough to stay away (from outside noise) … I think the players did a great job because it could have gone south a number of times, and it didn't. They held it together, and they grinded it out, and they got (to the postseason) because of that.”

The Nittany Lions understood the lofty expectations placed on them. And they never shied away from placing similar ones on themselves, even after making their third NCAA Tournament in four years.

Before Friday’s contest, Jarod Crespo said the goal was to win a national championship. The senior defenseman said the team’s regular-season success would mean “nothing” if it couldn’t achieve that goal.

And while Friday’s loss ended those title hopes, Fink believes Penn State lived up to the preseason hype.

“The three years I've been here, all you expect is a national championship, and when you lose, it sucks,” Fink said. “The three years that we've lost, especially last year and this year, we had high expectations, but I still thought we lived up to them. And I think (the team) is going to come back, and this program is going to be unbelievable.”

Dowiak backed Fink’s sentiment of championship-or-bust expectations in Happy Valley. And while the group he captained fell short of its goal, he left his junior campaign proud of the fight and perseverance his teammates showed amid a tumultuous, adversity-filled season.

“We had a lot of high expectations this year and a lot of pressure," Dowiak said. "Just really proud of the boys for battling through a lot of adversity. It sucks not winning in the end, but Penn State nation knows that every single night, we put our best foot forward and gave it everything we had.”

title: Penn State men's hockey defenseman Jackson Smith confirms return for 2026-27 season
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — Jackson Smith is heading back to Hockey Valley.

The freshman defenseman was Penn State’s first-ever first-round draft pick, taken No. 14 overall by the Columbus Blue Jackets in the 2025 NHL Draft.

Penn State defenseman Jackson Smith told me he will be returning to the Nittany Lions next year.“This place means so much to me after one year. I can only imagine after two.”— Chase Fisher (@chase_fisher4) March 28, 2026

title: Penn State men's hockey falls to Minnesota Duluth, ends season in first round of NCAA Regionals
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — A loose, relaxed Gavin McKenna said in the Penn State locker room after practice on Thursday: “We’ve been working all year for it.” The ‘it’ McKenna references — a national championship.

Step 1 of that process began Friday, as the third-seeded Nittany Lions laced their skates and hit the ice to battle No. 2 seed Minnesota Duluth in the first round of the NCAA Tournament.

With a desire to reach its second straight Frozen Four, Penn State battled the Bulldogs tough, but ultimately came up short 3-1 on Friday, ending the most-anticipated season in program history.

Here is how the Nittany Lions' season came to an end.

First period

Penn State looked like the better team from the jump. The Nittany Lions were faster and continued to win puck battles along the boards in the early parts of the first period. And then they struck first.

Defenseman Casey Aman pinched in from the blue line to keep in a puck near the nine-minute mark of the first period. After his keep-in, Aman laced a cross-ice feed to forward Shea Van Olm at the left dot, who moved in and lasered a shot past Minnesota Duluth goaltender Adam Gajan, putting Penn State up 1-0.

However, despite the Nittany Lions continuing the offensive pressure, the Bulldogs responded with 4:58 remaining in the first frame. And who else to score the equalizer than forward Max Plante, Minnesota Duluth’s point leader, who is fifth in college hockey in scoring.

Plante’s tally came from a back-door tap-in to the right of Penn State goaltender Josh Fleming, as he crashed the net and received a cross-ice feed from his brother, Zam Plante, to tie the game 1-1.

The shot count in the first period finished in Penn State’s favor, 17-9, as it controlled the pace of the game in the first 20 minutes.

Second period

The second frame took on a much different pace than the first. Minnesota Duluth began the period with full control of play, garnering extended offensive-zone time and limiting Penn State’s chances on the rush.

A Nittany Lions penalty for too many men on the ice gave the Bulldogs a power play in the early portion of the second frame, but Minnesota Duluth could not convert nor generate any high-danger chances.

The Bulldogs man-advantage was the only one for either team in the second frame, with the period finishing in a 1-1 tie.

Minnesota Duluth outshot Penn State 17-6 in the frame, with many Grade-A chances on Fleming. Of Fleming’s 17 second-period saves, two of them came with a Bulldog all alone directly in front of him.

On the Nittany Lions' end, their best offensive chances came in two separate 2-on-1’s with forward Reese Laubach. In one instance, Laubach rang a shot off iron. In the other, he made an extra pass to forward Aiden Fink, but Fink was at a tough angle and could not convert on the empty net.

Third period

Minnesota Duluth once again took control in the early parts of the third period. The Bulldogs’ Kyle Gaffney earned the first high-danger chance of the frame, as he was all alone in the slot. Fleming, however, made a tight-space glove save.

Minutes later, forward Scout Truman leaked into the front of the goal and got off a backhand shot, which Fleming saved as well.

Minnesota Duluth’s best chance, though, came at the 11-minute mark with Fleming going post-to-post to save a Max Plante shot. Laubach responded shortly thereafter with a backhand shot in the crease on Gajan, which was saved.

The game took a turn with just five minutes to play in the third period. After Fleming made save after save to keep Penn State in it, defenseman Grayden Siepmann forced a defensive zone turnover from McKenna and forward Hunter Anderson picked a corner on Fleming, giving the Bulldogs a 2-1 lead.

Anderson’s goal was the backbreaker for the Nittany Lions, as they would fall 3-1 to Minnesota Duluth. Defenseman Ty Hanson hit the empty-net goal.

Injury report

Penn State center Charlie Cerrato collided with forward JJ Wiebusch in the second period, enduring what looked like a significant injury. Cerrato returned late in the third period.

title: Penn State baseball catcher Nate Voss ruled out for the season due to surgery
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions will play without catcher Nate Voss for the remainder of the season due to an injury that required surgery.

“Nate Voss is out for the year with surgery," coach Mike Gambino told the media following a 9-8 win over Illinois on Friday. "The second or third inning against Air Force, the slide and they got him.”

The senior only appeared in one game this season as the injury reportedly occurred in the first game of the year.

Voss played in 43 games last season, posting a .245 batting average and hitting eight total home runs.

title: Penn State baseball utilizes late-game offensive surge in win over Illinois
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s offense has shown signs of inconsistency in its last few games. In one game, it erupts, scoring 14 runs. While the other shows a donut on the scoreboard.

After starting pitcher Mason Horwat gave up six runs in the first two innings, it was up to that same offense to mount a comeback against Illinois. Using the middle innings to spark a rally, the blue and white won Game 1 of the series 9-8 over Illinois.

“I think the biggest thing is we’re never out of the game,” Michael Anderson said. “It doesn’t matter if our team scores six, 10 or one, we are always in the fight.”

It didn’t take long for Penn State to have to fight from behind as the Fighting Illini took a demanding 6-0 lead as the bottom half of the second inning rolled around.

Horwat struggled early, only managing to earn one strikeout and allowing six earned runs off of three hits. Mason Butash came in to relief and was able to strike out three batters; however, he gave up a solo home run to Collin Jennings before being pulled in the sixth inning.

“The shape of (Butash’s) fastball has improved, the execution of his breaking stuff has improved, and now you also see the confidence tick up,” coach Mike Gambino said. “So, he’s done a really, really good job.”

With many early challenges threatening the Nittany Lions, it was up to an offense that wasn’t able to put up a single run in their last game.

It was quite the opposite showing this time around, once the home half of the fourth inning appeared. Bryce Molinaro ignited a short-lived spark in the fourth with a double off the center field wall. Jayden Davis followed suit with his second triple of the season after he hit a shot into right field. Davis would later score off an Avery Smith groundout.

The spark wasn’t completely put out just yet for the Nittany Lions. Five runs reached the plate in the fifth inning, tying the game at seven.

A pair of opening singles put a runner in scoring position with no outs. Anderson flew out to deep center field, scoring a runner from third. After a walk, Spencer Barnett singled in Cohl Mercado. Molinaro loaded the bases with a single, ultimately leading to three of the runners scoring before Jesse Jaconski grounded out to end a five-run inning.

“I’d say probably at 7-4,” Gambino said when knew the momentum was in the Nittany Lions favor. “It was like ‘alright boys, here we go’ and I think the key to this game was Butash. Butash settles this thing down and gives us a chance.”

With an additional run coming in the sixth inning, Penn State was able to reach home at least once in three straight innings. The Nittany Lions last accomplished this against Georgetown on March 11.

Those in attendance were sent home happy after Anderson hit a 413-foot home run over the center field wall in the eighth inning, ultimately being the game-winner. Penn State’s offense finished the game with 11 hits, beginning its conference series against Illinois with a powerful offensive display.

“Today was just about today. So, I wanted to feel good there, I wanted to enjoy the clubhouse,” Gambino said. “When we show up tomorrow, it’s a new day. It’s just back to one day, stay present, stay positive and keep working.”

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball earns late win over Illinois in series opener
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State went into its game against Illinois on Friday coming off a 9-0 shutout loss against Bucknell on Wednesday. The last time the Nittany Lions saw the Fighting Illini was the 2024 season, where Penn State only won one of their four meetups.

Ultimately, Penn State got back into the win column with a 9-8 victory over the Fighting Illini.

“The thing I saw better from Wednesday to today was about effort,” coach Mike Gambino said.

Mason Horwat started on the mound for the blue and white. Throughout the season so far, Penn State has struggled with pitching, switching their pitcher as many as seven times just last game against Bucknell.

Horwat opened up play by walking senior outfielder Nick Groves, but quickly followed that up with a strikeout.

After another walk and a base hit, bases were loaded with Collin Jennings at bat. Jennings hit a fly to center field and was caught out, but not before Illinois could bring a run in.

Shortly after, Kyle Schupmann hit a single between the blue and white’s 5-6 hole, and the Fighting Illini were quickly up 2-0 by the end of the first inning.

Mitch Dye opened up pitching for Illinois, allowing Cohl Mercado to get a base hit to right field. While batters got on base for Penn State, two base-hit outs paired with a strikeout allowed them no time to score before the first inning was concluded.

At the top of the second, after two walks and one hit, the bases were loaded. After Horvath's fifth walk and a base hit from AJ Putty, the Fighting Illini scored twice, advancing the score to 4–0.

Penn State called a timeout as Collin Jennings earned a two-run single after a base hit down the first base path to the outfield. As Illinois continued its six-run stretch, Mason Butash was called to take over the mound.

Butash last pitched against Bucknell on Wednesday, where he struck out four batters. The junior was able to replicate that for the Nittany Lions’ third out to close the inning, striking out Schupmann looking.

At the bottom of the second, Jayden Davis got a base hit to left field, getting on base for the blue and white.

Despite loading the bases, Illinois was able to end the inning on a double play, with Preston Yaucher grounding out.

Butash returned to the mound for the third inning, striking out Daniel Contreras for the first out. The next two outs quickly followed and Penn State escaped the third inning holding the Fighting Illini scoreless despite a strong opening two innings.

These quick three outs are reproduced on Illinois’ end, and the score remains at 6-0 going into the fourth inning.

Scoring finally opened up for Penn State after it shut out Illinois at the top of the fourth. Bryce Molinaro opened up the hitting, hitting the center-field wall and getting to second base

Jayden Davis was quick to follow up this offensive momentum, hitting to right field and sending Molinaro home.

Avery Smith also added to the RBI column, grounding out, but sending Davis home to score the Nittany Lions’ second run, and cutting the Illinois lead to a four-run margin.

On the contrary, Illinois answered back with a big hit of its own. Jennings continued with a hot bat, hitting a home run over the right field fence to add to the Fighting Illini’s six runs. This hit gave Jennings his fourth RBI of the day and made him two for two while at bat.

The blue and white were able to hold Illinois to one run, concluding the top of the fifth after Brayden Mazzacano was picked off by Butash to first base.

A lengthy review called Michael Anderson’s hit to left field a foul ball. He battled two strikes and eventually flew out to center field. This, however, allowed Yaucher to run home from third, closing the lead to 7-3.

Spencer Barnett continued the hitting streak, sliding a single past Illinois’ first baseman. This sent Porter to second and Mercado home to score, narrowing Illinois’ lead to three. Molinaro followed with a single to right field, loading the bases for Penn State with Davis up to hit, all with one out.

Davis delivered, sending Porter home to advance their run total to five. Smith then earned another RBI and put Penn State within one, after he was flied out to left field.

Penn State officially tied the score when Yaucher singled to first after an error at shortstop. This sent Molinaro home to score, and kept bases loaded for Jesse Jaconski’s hit, who was then grounded out to end the inning.

After two singles from Illinois to the left side, Jack Zebig hit again to left field, this time, sending Groves home to break the scoring standstill.

With this, junior Kyle Emmons was sent in to finish the inning on the mound. Emmons was able to quickly end the inning on a double play, grounding out from Yaucher to Davis to Barnett at first.

“We’ve spent a lot of time and work on (Davis’) feet and his hands in that double play to quicken up and quicken his arm swing,” Gaminbo said. “It turned into an elite double play.”

Penn State saw a quiet hitting inning until Davis singled to center field. This sent Anderson home to tie up the score at eight, with two outs against the blue and white.

Illinois opted to switch Kyle Remmington in for Sam Reed to finish the inning. Remington was able to end the inning on his first batter. This sent Avery Smith back to the dugout after he struck out looking.

Both teams held each other scoreless in the seventh inning.

At the top of the eighth, Emmons was able to get one strikeout. Michael Farina for Illinois was fouled out and Groves was caught stealing to wrap up the inning.

Anderson opened up hitting for Penn State with a homerun off the first pitch to put Penn State in the lead for the first time all game. He was the only Nittany Lion to get on base that inning, and Penn State went into the ninth inning with a small one-run cushion.

“I mean, the worst thing to be when Big Mike is around is a baseball,” Gambino said.

At the top of the ninth, Penn State was able to get the first two outs quickly, with Emmons striking out Zebig and Putty being grounded out to shortstop.

The last out and the sealing of Penn State’s win came solely from Emmons, striking Schupmann.

Up Next

Penn State will continue its series against Illinois at 2 p.m. on Saturday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball gets back on track, dominates Rutgers in series opener
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After dropping two of three games to Wisconsin last weekend, Penn State fell to 1-5 in Big Ten play. The Nittany Lions had a chance to get back on track with a trip to Piscataway, New Jersey, taking on Rutgers.

In Friday's series opener, Penn State showed out on both sides of the ball, defeating Rutgers 8-0.

Right-hander Kassandra Gewecke got the nod for the Scarlet Knights, allowing two runners to sneak into scoring position. The sophomore pitcher was backed up by her defense, preventing any runs from coming across.

Coach Clarisa Crowell turned to right-hander Bridget Nemeth to start things off in the circle for the blue and white. Nemeth allowed a double to first baseman Baileigh Burtis, but Rutgers didn’t do any more damage, sending things to the second inning scoreless.

Both pitchers remained dialed in their second times out, each allowing just one runner to reach base.

Penn State welcomed the top of the order back to the plate in the third frame. Center fielder Natalie Lieto and left fielder Breanna Hanik surrendered groundouts, but third baseman Brooke Klosowicz worked a walk to keep the inning alive.

Shortstop Allison Oneacre worked Gewecke to a full count before launching her 11th home run of the year, giving the Nittany Lions a 2-0 advantage.

The two-out rally continued as a double from Jordan George brought in second baseman Michela Barbanente for another blue and white run.

Now with a cushion on Penn State’s side, Crowell took advantage of her pitching depth, bringing in right-hander McKenna Young in the third frame, who, with the help of her defense, put Rutgers down in order.

The Scarlet Knights made a pitching change themselves as right-hander Brooke Shifflett entered the circle for Gewecke. Shifflett had a stellar first inning, striking out the side to hand the bats over to Rutgers in the bottom of the fourth inning.

Rutgers put two runners on after Burtis reached on an error and Young walked third baseman, Addie Osborne. However, Young escaped the threat as she recorded her first two strikeouts of the game.

Shifflett walked Klosowicz with one out, bringing up Oneacre in a similar situation to the freshman's previous at-bat. The situation turned out to be not just similar, but identical as Oneacre launched her second long ball of the day, extending the Penn State lead to 5-0.

Barbanente tripled down the right field line, followed by a walk from George to put two Nittany Lion runners on base. Right fielder Frankee Flesher grounded to Scarlet Knights’ shortstop Riley Hwang, who attempted to catch Barbanente at home.

Hwang's throw drifted far left out of reach for catcher Charleigh Schuettler, allowing Barbanente to score and putting runners in scoring position for Penn State. The Nittany Lions didn’t add any more runs, holding a 6-0 lead heading into the bottom of the fifth frame.

Young and the Penn State defense posted another clean sheet in the bottom of the inning, as they entered the sixth frame still with a comfortable lead.

The Scarlet Knights made another pitching change, bringing in Marley Caldwell as the Rutgers third arm of the night.

After recording a quick out, Caldwell faced Lieto at the top of the Nittany Lions’ order. After previously being hitless, Lieto found her spot on the bat, launching a left field home run for the blue and white’s third long ball of the night, giving the blue and white a 7-0 lead.

In the bottom of the sixth inning, Rutgers put its leadoff runner on after Hwang reached on an error from first baseman Gabby Gradishar. That was all the Scarlet Knights were able to do as Young stayed composed and capped the frame with her fifth strikeout of the game.

Flesher roped a double for the first extra base hit of her collegiate career, and was brought home on a pinch hit single from Macy Chamberlin as Penn State led 8-0 heading into the bottom of the seventh inning.

Rutgers entered its final at-bat, needing a breakthrough with just one hit on the night. The Scarlet Knights couldn’t get a read on Young as the Penn State pitcher completed the no-hitter in relief.

Injury Report

Infielders Kirsten Finarelli and Kaylie Walters remain out with injuries.

Up Next

Game 2 between the Nittany Lions and Scarlet Knights is set for 3 p.m. Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 17 Penn State men's volleyball takes down No. 19 NJIT in straight sets
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a neutral-site victory over rival No. 15 Ohio State earlier this week, No. 17 Penn State returned to Happy Valley for a weekend series with No. 19 NJIT.

The Nittany Lions’ first of two matches against the Highlanders was a quick one, with the blue and white securing a three-point victory on Friday night.

The match opened with the teams trading points, but the Highlanders would take the first multi-goal lead after outside hitter Wiktor Nowak powered it onto Penn State’s side of the floor to bring the score to 4-2.

After a series of errors by both teams, an attack error by Nowak brought Penn State within a point of the Highlanders. NJIT began to build a lead over the Nittany Lions with a four-point run, highlighted by an epic rally that culminated with a kill from Nowak to put the Highlanders up 10-5.

Penn State went on a run of its own following Nowak’s kill, as the blue and white took six of eight points to claw back to a 12-12 tie with the Highlanders.

A service error from Michael Schwob saw NJIT take a slight lead, but Schwob would deliver an assist to middle blocker Owen Rose, who tied the set with his second kill of the match. An attack error from Nowak brought the set into the media timeout with the Nittany Lions up 15-13.

Two quick points after the break forced another timeout from NJIT coach Danny Goncalves. The Highlanders would get a point back after the break, but a three-point run saw Penn State take a commanding, 20-14 lead.

Following a series of errors by both teams, Penn State secured the first set after NJIT couldn’t return a strong serve from Schwob. The 6-foot-6 junior finished the opening set with a team-leading nine assists.

NJIT opened the second set with two quick points, but the Nittany Lions would take seven of eight consecutive points to force another Goncalves timeout with Penn State leading 7-3.

Two Highlander points sandwiched a three-point run by Penn State, followed by back-and-forth play from both squads that resulted in a seven-point lead for the Nittany Lions.

The Highlanders would make a push to close the gap, highlighted by a three-point run finished with a kill from Daniel Latkowski to put an impressive save from Penn State to waste. They’d close the gap to just two points, but a kill from Matthew Luoma gave the blue and white some breathing room late in the frame.

NJIT cut the lead to 21-20 after a kill and block from Alex Heins, leading coach Mark Pavlik to take Penn State’s first timeout.

That break proved beneficial for the Nittany Lions, as a kill from Luoma resulted in a 24-21 lead and set point for the blue and white. Despite a late push from the Highlanders, Penn State would take the set after a hit from Sean Harvey that NJIT couldn’t return, securing the kill as the ball sailed into Rec Hall’s bleachers.

Set three started with yet another set-opening trade of points, but Penn State would jump out to a 6-2 lead after Rose’s seventh and eighth kills of the match powered past the Highlanders’ defense.

Four straight service errors preceded a three-point run for the Nittany Lions, expanding the lead to 11-5 early in the set.

After it dwindled to four, Rose’s ninth kill reinstated Penn State’s six-point lead, putting them up 15-9. NJIT attempted to mount another late-set comeback, but a service error ended the momentum and gave Penn State a 19-13 lead in the closing stages of the frame.

The Highlanders were able to close the gap slightly with their backs against the wall, cutting the lead to four points. Two aces by Gustavo Chrcanovic brought them within reach, making the score 23-21 in favor of the Nittany Lions, but a kill from Bennett Wilson and a huge block from the blue and white sealed the straight-set victory.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions will stay at home for another matchup with NJIT on Saturday before heading to West Virginia next weekend for a two-game series with Charleston.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s hockey senior leadership paves way to third NCAA Tournament
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — Three years ago, Penn State stepped onto the PPL Center ice in Allentown, Pennsylvania, in front of a packed sea of white, making its first postseason appearance in five years, and just the third in program history.

Four current Nittany Lions were on that historic team as underclassmen — Jarod Crespo, Carter Schade, Alex Servagno, and then-injured Ben Schoen. Little did the four skaters know that they would become regular competitors and three-time veterans of playoff hockey.

“You look at the senior group, this is the third time that they’ve been to the NCAA Tournament, that’s pretty remarkable,” Guy Gadowsky said. “I know they’ve learned a lot along the way, and I think that’s why their leadership group is so great.”

Fast forward to the present day, and the now-seniors are gearing up for their second-straight postseason berth, and third in four seasons.

Schoen and Crespo both wear the ‘A’ as alternate captains of this year’s Nittany Lions, joining with Schade and Servagno in leading the blue and white, still in search of its first-ever national championship.

Back in 2023, Penn State was still the new kid on the block, playing in just its 11th season as a Division I program, where an NCAA Tournament berth was few and far between.

Over the last few seasons, however, the Nittany Lions, spearheaded by this senior class, have worked their way up to become serious national title contenders and a top destination for premier collegiate prospects.

“I think it’s super special, obviously, to be here three times in four years, but it's all credit to the guys that came in the program before us. They built this place from the ground up,” Crespo said. “It’s super special for us as a senior class to kind of, you know, reap the benefits of that and all the work that came into it.”

While the group’s freshman season was significant in putting Hockey Valley on the map, last year was the catalyst for the program’s transformation into one of the top teams in the NCAA.

After starting the season 0-9 in Big Ten play and dead-last in the conference standings, the blue and white saw a second half turn around that landed it a spot in its first-ever Frozen Four.

The historic season put Penn State in the national spotlight. After landing top recruits in the 2025 NHL Draft first-rounder Jackson Smith and projected 2026 No. 1 overall pick Gavin McKenna, hosting the second-highest attended college hockey game ever, and earning yet another postseason berth, the lights only got brighter.

While the attention and expectations on the program were unprecedented, the leadership group knew how to handle them.

“It’s different for this program than we’ve had to deal with before, since going to the Frozen Four, having an outdoor game of 75,000 people, recruiting some of the top players available,” Gadowsky said. “There’s been a lot of different distractions, good and bad, that the team has had to deal with. The leadership group has done an unbelievable job.”

In a season unparalleled by any the blue and white have experienced before, the seniors at the helm managed the newfound fame and controlled what they could.

Now the group has a chance to finish what they started back as freshmen, and what they couldn’t accomplish the last two times they had the chance.

“When you look at the roster we have this year, it’s just so high-end,” Crespo said. “If we stick to the values that we’ve learned from those years prior, and put that into the roster we have, I think we’re a really tough team to beat.”

Three years ago, Crespo, Schoen, Schade and Servagno were part of the group that set off a chain of events that changed the Penn State program in ways very few could’ve predicted. Now, they have one more shot together to leave their mark on Hockey Valley history.

“Our goal is to win a national championship,” Crespo said. “Being here three times, we’ve got nothing if we don’t do that.”

title: ‘The cavalry is us’ | Chris Smalls delivers speech on activism, solidarity for SJP’s Week of Action
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Chris Smalls delivered a keystone speech on behalf of Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) on Thursday as a part of the organization’s “Week of Action."

After being fired by Amazon over COVID-19 policies, Smalls advocated for the first ever Amazon Labor Union in 2021, where he remained the director for three years. Smalls was detained by the Israeli military in 2025 while aboard a Freedom Flotilla Coalition boat attempting to deliver aid to Gaza.

“If we don’t do it, no one is going to save us,” Smalls said during his speech. “The cavalry is us.”

Smalls said he was detained and assaulted by Israel Defense Forces, many of whom he described as racist. He was forced to strip naked multiple times and was dropped in the Jordan desert, unlike the other flotilla activists, who were taken to an airport in Tel Aviv. Smalls said he was treated significantly worse than other activists, having been the only black man on the flotilla.

“I did it for them knowing the risks,” Smalls said. “Would I do it again? Absolutely. Without a doubt.”

He also said he was detained at the Miami International Airport on March 25 by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) after going to Cuba as part of a humanitarian envoy, with many of his devices and bags confiscated. Nothing was returned to him, he added.

“Support and solidarity is free, how can you not speak out?” Smalls said. “I came home to the people. To the community. To real love and solidarity. That’s what solidarity is. It’s showing up.”

The energy in the room was hopeful as Smalls spoke, with many nodding in agreement to his statements and cheering him on. At the front of the room by the entrance were 15 paintings by Bee Barker, commissioned by SJP, which reflected the community that has come together to combat the conflict.

“I was born and raised in Philadelphia, I grew up surrounded by art and culture, specifically in murals, and in everyday community members,” Barker said. “I spent 74 hours of my life doing this and I would absolutely do it again.”

As SJP concludes its Week of Action, members hope the speech brings the community together to speak out against marginalized communities, according to Sergio Peña, who organized the talk.

“We really wanted to highlight this idea of solidarity as a way of building people power between us, and to improve our conditions here at Penn State,” Peña, an SJP board member, said. “We hope that these events serve as a starting point for our community members on campus to see what’s possible and how we can invent Penn State in our own way.”

Barker described the event as inspiring and said she now wanted to try to get more connected to people who can help her conduct projects similar to this one.

“I really hope this event inspires a lot more togetherness and community on our campus,” Barker said.

title: 'We returned a lot from what we had last year' | Penn State men's hockey leaning on experience ahead of NCAA Tournament
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — The youngest player on third-seeded Penn State, the No. 9 team in the NPI rankings, has the most postseason, high-pressure game experience. And it just might be why the Nittany Lions can win a national championship.

18-year-old forward Gavin McKenna, the aforementioned youngest player on Penn State, is not even a year shy of a WHL championship in the Canadian Hockey League. Add three years of World Juniors with Team Canada, where he posted 35 points in 19 games, and two other postseason runs with the Medicine Hat Tigers of the WHL, and the resume explains itself.

He, among other freshmen on the Nittany Lions, have plenty of experience with postseason hockey. And that does not even include the remaining core from last season’s Frozen Four run.

“We returned a lot from what we had last year,” forward Matt DiMarsico said on Thursday. “But I just think, kind of speaking to the freshmen, they've all played big games and where they've come from the CHL. So I think for them, they kind of know what responsibility they have here. And having the experience of having mostly the same group (as last year), it's huge.”

Penn State enters its third NCAA Tournament in the past four years and will play its sixth game in that span at 9 p.m. Friday against No. 2 seed Minnesota-Duluth in MVP Arena.

The Nittany Lions have a massive experience boost on that alone compared to the Bulldogs, who are returning to the bracket for the first time since 2022. Additionally, Penn State brought back 16 players from the Frozen Four team and has 12 of those skaters playing on Friday.

“You look at the senior group — this is the third time that they've been to the NCAA tournament,” Gadowsky said. “That's pretty remarkable. I know they've learned a lot along the way. That's why the leadership group is so great. They've learned a lot.”

The senior group truly has seen it all at Penn State. All but a championship. And that experience can help lead the Nittany Lions to one.

They have reached the highest of highs in their trip to the Frozen Four in St. Louis last year. The lowest of lows in their underwhelming 2023-24 season, where the Nittany Lions went 15-18-3 just one year after reaching the second round of the NCAA Tournament.

And that is not to mention the Jan. 31 Beaver Stadium outdoor game, last season’s outdoor contest in Wrigley Field and the improbable second-half run in 2025, where the team played near-flawless hockey for three months to jolt itself into a playoff spot.

“Really the story all year — it has to do with leadership,” Gadowsky said. “(It’s) very different from the story last year, but the leadership that was required this year was essential. Really proud of the job that they have done, specifically because it's different for this program than we've had to deal with before. We've gone on the Frozen Four, having an outdoor game of 75,000 people, recruiting some of the top players available, the Gavin effect, etc. There's been a lot of different distractions, good and bad, that the team has had to deal with. The leadership group has done an unbelievable job.”

Without that leadership displayed by the upperclassmen, Gadowsky said the team would not be where it is right now — on the path to a potential first national championship in program history.

“There were new challenges for us, a new spotlight,” Gadowsky said. “But there's a lot of mental gymnastics that go along with all of that ... they never once used it as an excuse, and they just found a way to continue to be positive and carry forward. And that comes from a locker room. It's way more important what happens when the coach leaves the locker room than when he's in it, and these guys did an exceptional job.”
