title: Movin' On announces Zara Larsson as the headliner for 2026 lineup
author: Sara O'Connell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Movin’ On announced Zara Larsson, Swedish singer and songwriter, as the headliner for this year’s musical festival.

The 2026 lineup was shared at the HUB-Robeson Center's Monumental Stairs on Friday. Movin’ On 2026 will take place May 1 at Penn State's IM Fields with Larsson headlining, along with the Dare, That Mexican OT and Slayyyter also performing at the music festival.

The Battle of the Bands, taking place on March 31, will decide the opening act. Doors will open at 5 p.m., and performances will begin at 5:30 p.m. in the Schwab Auditorium.

After celebrating Movin’ On’s 50th anniversary last year with the All-American Rejects headlining the event, students are eager for this year's event.

Eirene Halas and Natalie West both expressed their excitement about attending the event when Zara Larsson was announced as the headliner.

“We looked at each other in shock,” Halas, a second-year studying biology, said.

Similarly, Neelakant Raghavendra was thrilled by the featured artists.

“Could it be Zara Larsson? No, that's too far, but she’s actually coming,” Raghavendra, a first-year studying marketing, said.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Movin’ On 2026 Headliner Predictions
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s annual music festival Movin’ On is set to announce its lineup at the HUB-Robeson Center’s Monumental Stairs at 1 p.m. Here’s a prediction list of who will take the coveted spot of headlining.

RAYE

RAYE, or Rachel Keen, is a British singer-songwriter who gained popularity with her 2022 hit “Escapism.” Now she is better known for her songs such as “Prada” and “WHERE IS MY HUSBAND!”

As of 2026, RAYE became a Grammy award winning artist as she won the Harry Belafonte Best Song For Social Change Award for her song “Ice Cream Man.” As RAYE is still a growing artist, she is a safe bet for Movin’ On 2026’s headliner.

Chappell Roan

Chappell Roan, who won in 2025 at the Grammy’s for Best New Artist, is next on the list. Known for her hits “Good Luck, Babe,” “Pink Pony Club,” “HOT TO GO!” and countless others, putting her on the list is definitely more of a stretch.

Due to her rising fame, she is an unlikely candidate, but she has not released new music in a while and may be looking for an opportunity to perform.

Zara Larsson

The queen of 2016, Zara Larsson. When Larsson’s song “Symphony” went viral as a meme in the summer of 2024, Larsson took it as an opportunity to rebrand.

Larsson’s old songs such as “Lush Life” and “Never Forget You” became popular again, in addition to her new album “Midnight Sun” gaining attention as well. This would be an epic way to end the school year.

Alex Warren

Ex “Hype House” member Alex Warren launched his solo music career in 2021 with the single “One More I Love You.” Since then, his career has taken off.

Songs like “Ordinary,” “Carry You Home” and “Before You Leave” got Warren nominated for Best New Artist at the Grammy’s. Although he didn’t win, we’re sure his Grammy is coming soon. In the meantime, let’s hope he makes a pit stop at PSU.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse presented with opportunity to bounce back against No. 3 Maryland
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a two-game losing streak, Penn State is looking for any sign of hope as it prepares for its toughest opponent of the year.

The Nittany Lions will face No. 3 Maryland at noon on Sunday, hitting the road for the second time this Big Ten season.

While the last two losses came against then-No. 19 Princeton and No. 8 Michigan, the blue and white will have its hands full against the Terrapins.

All-time matchups

Maryland is one of, if not the toughest, opponents the Nittany Lions have played all time. Since 1973, Penn State has held an 18-45-1 all-time record, holding the short end of the stick.

Before falling 19-11 last season to the Terrapins, the Nittany Lions had won two straight, including a 12-11 nail-biter victory in 2024.

However, the blue and white were on a very long losing streak for the three decades prior. Beginning in 1992, Maryland won 16 straight matchups against Penn State. After a Nittany Lion victory in 2005, the Terrapins won another 17 straight.

Last year, the blue and white hosted Maryland for the White Out game as it looked to escape from a four-game losing streak.

Instead, they fell 19-11 behind three goals from Brooke Hoss and two goals each from Erika Ho, Lauren Saltz and Ellie Wisch. Goalie Sydney Manning recorded a 35% save percentage with seven saves against 13 goals.

2026 Terrapins

Maryland is the last of the Big Ten teams to be undefeated, boasting an 11-0 record.

Through one month, the Terrapins have won every game by multiple goals, with their smallest win coming as a two-goal victory against St. Joseph’s and their biggest wins coming as eight-goal victories against Hofstra and Oregon.

The Terrapins undefeated record has come as a result of dominant attacker Lauren LaPointe, who sits third in the Big Ten with 39 goals.

However, the biggest area of success for Maryland has come from its goalie, JJ Suriano. The junior goalkeeper leads Big Ten starting netminders with a 54.7% save percentage, recording 116 saves against just 96 goals allowed.

Sunday’s matchup

While the Terrapins have experienced a very dominant season so far, Penn State boasts a very talented roster that just hasn’t been able to find its groove the past two weeks.

The Nittany Lions have a pair of Tewaaraton Award Watch Listers in Ho and Delaney Radin, who have had a lot of success so far this year.

Radin leads the Big Ten with 35 assists, including when she tied the school record for most assists in a single game with eight assists against Colgate. While Ho missed a portion of the season due to injury, she still sits fifth on the team with 14 goals.

However, junior Payton Wainman has been the leader for the blue and white, scoring a team-high 30 goals this season.

In addition to the Delray Beach, Florida, native, freshman Rocquette Allen has been a major contributor to Penn State. Allen was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Week three weeks in a row as she’s tallied 25 goals this year.

Penn State is coming into the game with a top-15 offense in the nation, but it has its hardest test against the No. 3 team in the country.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field competes at Raleigh Relays, Knights Invite
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After opening the outdoor season at the Penn Challenge last weekend, Penn State continued its season this weekend with a pair of invitationals.

The Nittany Lions competed in the Raleigh Relays on Thursday and Friday and the Knights Invite on Friday and Saturday.

Here’s a look at how the blue and white competed in Week 2 of the outdoor season.

Thursday - Raleigh Relays

Jacob Smith and Jake Borgesi kicked off the meet weekend with personal bests in the men’s 1500-meters.

Smith finished 108th overall with a time of 3:49.50, followed by Borgesi in 3:51.79 for 145th place.

The men’s 3000-meter steeplechase also saw a pair of personal bests as sophomore Tyler Rader finished 26th in 8:56.61, followed by junior Logan Strawser in 8:58.28 for 27th place.

Friday - Raleigh Relays

Friday was the women’s distance day in Raleigh, as senior Sarah Pickering got things going in the 1500m with a 26th-place finish in 4:19.55.

Fellow senior Allison Dougherty made her steeplechase debut after setting the ninth-best time in school history last season. On Friday, she opened the season in 30th place with a time of 10:37.92.

Amidst a weather delay and scrambling around heats, sophomore Ada Rand competed in her first collegiate 5,000-meter, finishing 21st in 16:04.23. Senior Katie Dallas finished shortly behind in 45th place, earning a personal-best in 16:32.57.

The Nittany Lions wrapped up the night in the women’s 10,000-meter invitational, where Charlotte Costich and Claire Daniels ran record-breaking times.

Costich led the way for the blue and white in 12th place, running 32:57.53 for the third-best time in Penn State history. Daniels finished in 19th in 33:18.60 — the fifth-best in school history.

Friday - Knights Invite

Senior Audrey Friedman got things going in Florida with the women’s javelin throw, where she set a season-best 46.22 meters for third place.

The men’s javelin throw saw even greater success as freshman Nolan Carey earned a personal best 66.08 meters for second place.

Shortly after, sophomore Amelia McBain earned another top-five finish for the Nittany Lions in the women’s pole vault, jumping 3.67 meters for fifth place.

In the women’s long jump, junior Lizzie Schreiber earned a personal best as she finished sixth overall for 5.83 meters.

The women’s hammer throw saw another season-best for the blue and white, as Alex Pancoast finished fourth overall with a throw of 54.38 meters. Gabi Deglau finished just behind in fifth place for 54.24 meters — her best this year.

Graduate student Collin Burkhart earned another personal best as he improved his No. 5 mark in school history in the hammer throw, throwing 66.19 meters for first place.

The Nittany Lions also set one of the best marks in school history in the women’s shot put as sophomore Sasha Garnett threw 16.16 meters for fourth in the meet and ninth in school history. Skye Petrie-Cameron also earned a personal best as she threw 15.87 meters for fifth place.

Penn State found another two top-five throwers in the men’s shot put, led by freshman Bryce Thompson in second place with 17.80 meters.

Sophomore Brady Mider finished shortly behind in fourth place with a throw of 17.03 meters.

The men’s and women’s 400-meter hurdles produced a pair of top-10 finishers as Matt DeMatteo earned a personal best for fourth place in 52.17 seconds. Addyson White finished seventh overall in 1:00.82 for another personal best.

Senior Marie-Eloise Leclair made her Penn State debut in the women’s 200-meter, running the second-fastest time in school history for first place in 22.75 seconds. Alexandria North earned a personal best in 24.09 seconds, finishing in 20th place.

Penn State wrapped up the Knights Invite on Friday with the men’s 200m, where sophomore Jake Palermo ran 20.97 seconds for a personal best in sixth place, followed by Peyton Sommers in eighth place with 21.15 seconds.

Up next

The Nittany Lions stay on the road as they compete in the Florida Relays next Friday and Saturday.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Column | Retire Penn State women’s hockey Tessa Janecke’s jersey number
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Wisconsin Badgers are up 3-2 in the 2026 Women’s Frozen Four with five minutes remaining in the third period. Penn State is deep in its zone and with time winding down, Pegula Ice Arena is losing hope by the second.

Then, Tessa Janecke executed a bold move: cherry picking for a breakaway against the defending national champions. Janecke performed a deke to goaltender Ava McNaughton’s left side, tucking the puck past the goal line to tie the game, 3-3.

It’s in times like these that Janecke delivers. Her clutch tendency, superior game sense, drive to succeed and refined talent all come together in the most historic moments.

Janecke’s production from the minute she stepped on the ice wearing the Penn State logo, day in and day out, lifted the program from a scrappy upstart to a national contender in the Frozen Four. Her spearheading of Penn State’s rise is why her jersey number, No. 15, should be hung in the rafters and retired from future use.

While Penn State hockey hasn’t retired any jersey numbers to date, the Nittany Lions haven’t had a player with such a stacked résumé.

Before Janecke, Penn State’s peak was the 2020-21 regular season. The Nittany Lions went 16-2-2 in the CHA, now the AHA, — the only games played due to the COVID-19 pandemic — and finished with a program-first regular season championship. However, Penn State fell to Syracuse, 3-2, in the CHA Semifinals.

Janecke arrived in Happy Valley for the 2022-23 season and immediately made an impact, scoring 22 goals and adding 25 assists, good for second on the team in both goals and points behind current Toronto Sceptres player Kiara Zanon, leading the team in assists. That rookie season led to Janecke being named the HCA and CHA Rookie of the Year as well as the USCHO Co-Rookie of the Year.

The USCHO honor was shared with Wisconsin’s Caroline Harvey, the most recent Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award winner and three-time national champion.

The Orangeville, Illinois, native racked up her first 50-point season the following year, adopting a playmaker role for a team-high 17 goals and 36 assists, tallying 21 more points than the next-highest teammate, current Vancouver Goldeneyes player Brianna Brooks, who contributed 32.

Janecke tied that figure in her junior season, continuing on as the undisputed leader of Penn State’s point production, scoring 24 goals and adding 29 assists. In the most recent season, Janecke tallied 48 points despite missing eight games due to competing on behalf of Team USA in the 2026 Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games.

Janecke was the first player in program history to represent Team USA in the Winter Olympics, contributing five assists in the tournament to aid the team’s historic run to winning gold over Team Canada. Before the Olympics, however, Janecke won two IIHF Women’s World Championships, having scored the championship-winning overtime goal in the 2025 gold-medal game.

Her late tally in the Frozen Four was her 201st career collegiate point, the 200th coming in the first period to break the ice. She’s the only Nittany Lion to reach the milestone, making her Penn State hockey’s leading point scorer between both the men’s and women’s programs.

Speaking of milestones, Janecke was first named a top-10 finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award in her sophomore season, an award given to the best collegiate women’s hockey player each season. While she’s been named to a top-10 finalist three years in a row, Janecke was named a top-3 finalist — the first woman in program history to do so — in her senior campaign for having led the Nittany Lions through their most successful season to date.

Janecke was also the first woman in program history to be named a First Team All-American selection, having previously won Second Team All-American honors for the past two years.

As the most decorated player in Penn State hockey history, her list of conference awards is long. She’s the three-time AHA Player and Forward of the Year and a four-time All-AHA First Team selection. Janecke was also named the Most Outstanding Player in the 2026 AHA Championship game against Mercyhurst.

Every step of the way, Janecke brought growth and support to Hockey Valley, receiving deafening cheers when her name was called for the starting lineups. In front of over 5,000 fans watching the Frozen Four, the crowd’s volume rose each time she touched the puck. The mere sight of the superstar on the ice made fans more confident.

Because of Janecke’s consistency in showing up not only in the biggest moments but in every little moment in between, Penn State is a national dynasty in the making. It’s hard to deny Janecke’s impact on the program when looking at the hardware she’s earned, but it’s her ability to lead by example and push others to strive for greatness that truly sets her apart.

Penn State’s success drew attention in terms of recruiting. Janecke and the entire senior class inspired young players to commit to play in Hockey Valley, including the AHA Rookie of the Year, Danica Maynard, and All-AHA Rookie Team selection Mikah Keller.

For the success that Janecke brought to the program, for all of the accolades she’s earned and the records she broke along the way, the No. 15 jersey will always belong to the greatest hockey player in Penn State history: Olympic gold-medalist, two-time World Champion, Nittany Lion captain Tessa Janecke.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Urban Dance Troupe brings family, grooves to Penn State
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
You’ll see them perform at local dance competitions, THON and even in the HUB-Robeson Center. But there’s so much about Penn State’s Urban Dance Troupe that many students don’t know.

Urban Dance Troupe, Penn State’s first-official hip hop dance team, is a student-run dance crew with a concentration in hip hop. Right now, the organization is made up of 21 members, all of whom work together to conceptualize, choreograph, teach and perform their routines.

Club President Kenzie Leitzell said she grew up in State College and knew about the dance organization for years before attending Penn State. She said many of the dance teachers she had growing up were members of the troupe.

“We’re really known for doing very classic, foundational hip hop that’s very social and energetic and fun,” Leitzell, a fourth-year art education major, said. “We definitely have fun when we’re dancing.”

Letizell said she feels lucky and grateful to be part of Urban Dance Troupe, a group of people that she said love to dance. She also appreciates their focus on hip hop, something she said can be hard to find outside of a major city.

Urban Dance Troupe participates in most local competitions as well as campus pep rallies and events. Katie Jones said the Penn State’s Best Dance Crew competition is one of the most exciting for her.

“It’s really important to us because we already have a running title with that,” Jones, a third-year studying biochemistry and molecular biology, said. “We’ve won now the past four years.”

Jones said the best part of her club experience is seeing how dedicated each member is to their Urban Dance Troupe family.

Maddie Spiewak expressed a similar sentiment and said those close-knit vibes were a major reason she auditioned for the group.

Spiewak, a third-year studying advertising/public relations, mainly did tap and jazz dance when she was growing up and said she felt compelled to try something different when she came to college.

Walking around the involvement fair her freshman year, she loved the vibes of the students representing Urban Dance Troupe, because they were “so cool” and friendly. Now, she’s found her place in the troupe’s family.

“I went to their open class, and I just fell in love with it,” Spiewak said.

She said she’s loved the innovative dance environment Urban Dance Troupe fosters and unique choreography the group has gotten to learn. Her favorite routine so far has been to a SZA mix from her first year on the team. Jones’s favorite was the most recent Black Eyed Peas routine the group performed during THON.

Many students were able to watch and enjoy Urban Dance Troupe’s THON performance, and that’s what Jones said is so great about the group’s style.

“You can really communicate in hip hop,” Jones said. “There’s so many different ways you can implement different styles into hip hop. There’s always something new.”

She said there’s something universal about the style because of the synthesis of hip hop as an art form. It’s been drawn from many different styles over the years, so it has the potential to appeal to so many people.

“That’s what makes people really connected to it,” Jones said. “It’s a sociable style of dance because you could be from any sort of background and still find the rhythm or find your own way of moving in hip hop.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Review | Harry Styles’ new album ‘Kiss All The Time. Disco, Occasionally.’ brings the dance party
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
Three-time Grammy Award winner Harry Styles amped up the fun with the release of his fourth studio album “Kiss All The Time. Disco, Occasionally.”

The British singer-songwriter reintroduced his fans to the power of the synth, while simultaneously bringing the rave vibes.The album begins with “Aperture,” which also served as the first single, released on Jan. 22 ahead of the full album's release on March 6.

“Aperture” by definition is an “opening or open space.” When Styles sings “Aperture lets the light in / we belong together / it finally appears it’s only love,” he is referring to oneself and how opening up and allowing vulnerability can lead to unexpected outcomes.

The album follows with “American Girls,” a slow yet upbeat song with a steady rhythm. In it, Styles talks about the isolation and loneliness that comes when your friends are all in relationships, but you’re not. This relatable song quickly became a fan-favorite on the album.

After “American Girls,” is “Ready, Steady, Go!” The sadness from the previous song is washed away when the mysterious, almost futuristic vibes of “Ready, Steady, Go!” come on. This is one of the shortest songs on the album, standing at two minutes and 39 seconds.

In this song, Styles, in a way, sends us on a mission, asking the listener to “ready, steady and go.” Here, he throws us into an unpredictable setting, connecting this back to how new relationships can be fast-paced and volatile.

“Are You Listening Yet?” is next and mirrors the tempo of “Ready, Steady, Go!” with an equally urgent feel.Paired with the unstable tone, Styles features heavy drums and bass to keep the audience on their toes.

This song matches the title exactly. It’s a self-reflective song, where Styles is telling the audience to listen to themselves and take action when needed.

The next two songs, “Taste Back” and “The Waiting Game” launch us back into the riding with the top down summer vibes, featuring softer melodies and quaint lyrics.

“Taste Back” is about running into an old partner and realizing they’re a better version of themselves than when you left them. Styles sings “Did you get your taste back or did you just need a little love?” signifying that sometimes people are not right for each other and that is okay.

“The Waiting Game” is where Styles laments about the hopelessness that stationary relationships bring, singing, “You try and you always justify/ playing the waiting game/ when it all adds up to nothing.” It highlights a common dating issue: when one person fears commitment it can be detrimental to the other person so desperately longing to be in a relationship.

Next is “Season 2 Weight Loss.” Definitely one of the more interesting titles, but Styles is referring to a phenomenon that happens in television shows, when after the first season an actor sometimes returns half their weight.

Repeating the line "Holding out, hoping you will love me now,” this song is about the difficulties people face when accepting themselves as who they truly are. They keep asking themselves the same question, “If I change myself will people begin to like me?”

“Coming Up Roses” blesses our ears next. The phrase “coming up roses” simply means that something is turning out with a favorable outcome and Styles exemplifies this through the writing of this song. The theme of this tune is to cherish special relationships and moments while you still have them.

This song is beautiful, and some might even say holy, as the large orchestral break in the middle of the song sounds heavenly, almost like you're ascending.

After you are torn to shreds by “Coming Up Roses,” Styles picks you right back up, throws on your “going out clothes” and sticks you in a club with the next song, “Pop.”

This hasty and exciting song is supposed to make you feel reminiscent of what it feels like to be in a thrilling relationship that both parties know won't last long, and at any second, you might “pop.”

Similar to “Pop,” the next song in the album is entitled “Dance No More,” which is ironic because this song tends to make people want to dance so much more.

It’s jazzy, synth-filled and a brilliant mix of background vocal harmonies. This song, taking a break from relationships, is about the tension between Styles’ loving his stardom and ability to share art, but hating his lack of freedom and inability to just make music.

Just like that we've reached the last two songs of the album. First is “Paint By Numbers,” a softer sounding song accompanied by acoustic guitar and a light drum beat.

Painting by numbers is an activity where a colorless canvas is sectioned and categorized by different numbers, and each number corresponds to a color. The goal is to carefully fill in the numbered areas to reveal the desired image.

The metaphor alludes to the idea that life is messy, unplanned and in no way a predetermined paint by numbers image.

Lastly, “Carla’s Song” is a bit less “ballady” then “Paint By Numbers” but still laced with heart-warming melodies. This song is inspired by Styles’ friend Carla, who helped him find a new genre of music and discover his magic.

Knowing this, “Carla’s Song,” is centered around the idea that there is a whole world undiscovered “waiting there for you.”

Upon release, Styles’ album reached No.1 on the Billboard 200 for two weeks in a row. This was more than well deserved. Styles’ hiatus lasted a long four years, but it was worth the wait for this triumph of an album.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse set for rivalry matchup with No. 6 Ohio State
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a record-breaking win over No. 12 Maryland last week, No. 9 Penn State is hitting the road this weekend for its fourth consecutive ranked matchup against No. 6 Ohio State.

The Nittany Lions are 5-3 on the year after the 10-6, bounce-back win over the Terrapins on Saturday, one that saw a Panzer-best 2,532 fans in attendance. Penn State’s victory was largely fueled by a standout performance from freshman goaltender Preston Hawkins, who made a career-best 14 saves to record a season-low six goals allowed.

The defeat of Maryland also marked the program’s first-ever home victory over the Terrapins, despite the series dating back to 1916.

A continuous storyline throughout each of the blue and white’s past three games has been the absence of attackman Hunter Aquino, the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year, who’s been sidelined with an undisclosed injury. It’s still unclear if Aquino will be ready to go this Saturday.

The Buckeyes sit at 8-1 on the year, with their only loss coming to perennial No. 1 Notre Dame. In that contest Ohio State fell 9-8 at home, the Fighting Irish finding the game-winner from Josh Yago with just under three minutes left in the game.

Ohio State started the year off with dominant wins over Detroit Mercy and Cleveland State, those coming by scores of 19-3 and 17-7, respectively. The Buckeyes then rattled off victories over Bellarmine, Air Force and High Point before a one-goal thriller with Georgetown set them up for the matchup with Notre Dame.

After the home loss, Ohio State was able to get back on track, beating Denver 6-4 in Columbus. It followed that low-scoring affair with a second, taking down Rutgers 6-5 in last week’s Big Ten opener.

The Buckeyes are led in scoring by Garrett Haas, who has recorded 34 points up to this clash. His goal count of 19 is tied for the team lead with fellow attackman Alex Marinier, who follows close behind Haas with 23 points.

Those offensive stars aren’t the focal point of this Ohio State squad, however, as the team’s defense has allowed a nation-leading 6.33 goals per game.

A large part of that is due to the performance of goaltender Caleb “Big Tasty” Fyock, who ranks fifth nationally with a .582 save percentage. Fyock’s brother, Aleric, also played goaltender at Penn State, graduating in 2023.

However, Fyock left the Buckeyes’ matchup with Rutgers with an undisclosed injury, so Ohio State’s goaltending situation in the matchup with Penn State is up in the air.

Another intriguing matchup will be at the faceoff dot, as specialist Jack Oldman ranks tenth nationally in faceoff percentage, not far behind Penn State’s Reid Gills, who ranks seventh.

Penn State holds a 21-8 all-time record over the Buckeyes, with Ohio State getting a 13-9 win last season in Happy Valley. The series has swayed towards the blue and white in recent years; however, the Nittany Lions have won eight of the last ten matchups dating back to 2016.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘It just feels isolating’ | Women students pursue careers in male-dominated fields, despite gender disparities
author: Annabella Chaklos I For the Collegian
description:
For many women students, obtaining a degree at Penn State means existing in spaces that weren’t created with them in mind. Regardless, students in these fields continue to advocate for themselves and their place in higher education.

Traditionally male-dominated fields such as healthcare and agriculture have gradually welcomed women, but many pursuing these fields say their experience is not without flaws. As a result, student organizations like Minority Women in Health Fields (MWIHF) are providing a space where women can seek guidance as they navigate these fields.

“It’s important to uplift women, especially in spaces where they have historically been excluded,” Kennedy Erby, founder and president of MWIHF, said. “Representation matters, but recognition and support are what allow women to truly thrive.”

As a third-generation college student, Erby, a second-year studying veterinary and biomedical sciences, said her mother and grandmother have always instilled in her the importance of education, and she wants to empower other women toward success.

“As a black woman in this space, there are times when I feel overlooked or underestimated,” Erby said. “Those experiences have motivated me even more to succeed and to create spaces where others feel seen and supported.”

Erby currently works as an intern at the Frost Entomological Museum and plans to be a research assistant in the Mactinger Lab, focusing on veterinarian entomology. Post-graduation, she plans to pursue a Ph.D. in pathology or biomedical science, with a focus on vector-borne and zoonotic diseases.

“My major has opened so many doors and exposed me to a wide range of career paths within agricultural and animal health,” Erby said. “It’s shown me that there isn’t just one way to make an impact in this field. I’m interested in disease at a deeper level and contributing to the research that impacts both animal and human health.”

Erby said women belong in every space they walk into, and she wants to foster a community where women feel supported and confident to pursue their career goals.

“To me, being a woman in this field means showing up authentically, taking up space and opening doors for others,” Erby said. “It means contributing to change while also building community.”

Christina Bender said she finds community from the women in her field through Women in Turfgrass, a national organization that aims to advance opportunities and give connections for women in the turfgrass industry. She originally got in contact with the organization while working in the 2024 U.S. Women’s Open Golf Championship.

“It has really been such a great support system for me,” Bender, a fourth-year studying turfgrass science, said. “All those questions you can’t really go ask a guy at work, you can ask the other girls you have contact with. We’re always there for each other no matter what.”

Bender has worked both the 2024 and 2025 U.S. Women’s Open Golf Championships, and has a job as an assistant superintendent of Union League National Golf Course in New Jersey.

Despite her success, she said there are still a few things that make it difficult to be a woman in her industry.

“It just feels isolating sometimes,” Bender said. “People get together and do homework, but there’s not a ton of girls to bounce ideas off of each other.”

Alexis Otstott said she also feels that isolation in her own field. She said being surrounded by men in the agriculture field is the norm, but she’s slowly seeing a shift as she and others push for more diversity.

“Sometimes with male professors, it’s hard to ask questions, or (it) feels like they don’t want to talk to us,” Otstott, a third-year studying agricultural science, said. “While I was interviewing with companies in Atlanta, there was a man who was short with me and had an attitude. And from what other girls said, only short with them, and not the boys.”

Otstott said her biggest joy comes from talking with younger girls about the agricultural industry and encouraging their interest within it.

Otstott was crowned the 2026 Honey Queen by the Pennsylvania State Beekeepers Association, allowing her to visit schools, fairs, festivals and other events to inform children and promote the beekeeping industry.

“I always tell the girls, ‘Some day, you could wear this crown,’” Otstott said. “I encourage doing what you love and going after it.”

Representing what agriculture stands for and encouraging the future of the industry is what Otstott said she takes the most pride in. She said she wants the industry to continue growing, especially as more women enter it.

“Without encouragement, this isn’t where I’d be,” Otstott said.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State men’s hockey focused on ‘the game ahead’ amid lofty NCAA Tournament expectations
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — Penn State hears the outside noise.

The Nittany Lions understand the expectations that follow a preseason top-five team. They understand those who say a team that spent sizable NIL money on a 2025 NHL lottery pick and the consensus No. 1 pick in the 2026 NHL Draft should advance to the Frozen Four.

But they don’t care about those opinions. They care about the voices inside their locker room, the ones that they say matter most.

“There are a lot of expectations, but at this point of the year, you've got to be able to put those behind you and not worry about that stuff,” junior forward Reese Laubach said. “We’ve got single elimination games now against the top 16 teams in the country. So I think that's what our focus is on, just focusing on the one game ahead of us right now, and that's tomorrow.”

The Nittany Lions open the NCAA Tournament at 9 p.m. Friday at MVP Arena in Albany, New York, against No. 2-seed Minnesota Duluth.

The Bulldogs, who went 23-14-1 before bowing out in the final of the National Collegiate Hockey Conference Frozen Faceoff, enter Friday as the higher seed. But Penn State is favored, according to Fanduel Sportsbook.

The betting lines mark another instance of Penn State carrying lofty expectations this season — a national storyline following the commitments of Jackson Smith and Gavin McKenna.

“It’s real. I think that we were aware of that going into the season, and yet still underestimated the mental distractions, for lack of a better term, around it,” Guy Gadowsky said. “It's our first time going through something like that. We learned a ton. I think if we're fortunate enough to ever go through something like that again, we'll be more prepared.”

The Nittany Lions reportedly paid McKenna “in the ballpark of $700,000,” according to ESPN, which marked the largest NIL package in college hockey history.

McKenna’s arrival created a buzz surrounding Penn State that never wavered during another 20-win campaign. It led to an outdoor game at Beaver Stadium in front of nearly 75,000 fans, sold-out games at Pegula Ice Arena, and a heightened spotlight on a program that once flew under the radar.

Gadowsky didn’t deny the difficulties associated with more eyes and loftier expectations laid on the players — an adjustment he said his team took in stride.

“We certainly learned a lot (in dealing with the expectations). That's one of the areas where I'm really proud of (the team),” Gadowsky said. “They stayed focused on what they can control, didn't get caught up in all of the noise. And I think they did a really good job. I think the program, staff and our administration did our best to try and navigate our way through that.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball continues home stand with Illinois series
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off a rocky week with a close win against St. Bonaventure on Tuesday and a tough loss to Bucknell on Wednesday, Penn State returns home for a three-game conference series against Illinois. The first pitch is set to be thrown this Friday at 5:30 p.m.

The Nittany Lions have struggled this season, holding a 7-16 record overall and a 2-4 record in the Big Ten, compared to Illinois, which is 12-11 and 4-5 in conference play. The Fighting Illini arrive with more consistent offensive play, posing a threat to Penn State defensively, which has struggled on the mound.

While they were able to come back against the Bonnies in the seventh inning, St. Bonaventure dominated most of the game and established itself in the fourth. They took the lead with five runs, two against pitcher Frankie Sanchez, and three against Mason Horwat.

The blue and white faced the same problem against Bucknell, going through seven pitchers throughout the duration of the game. The Bisons scored nine runs and shut out the Nittany Lions. Penn State had strong hits, including a potential home run by Spencer Barnett, but each seemed to always find a glove.

Penn State will have to play to its offensive strengths this weekend to really make a statement against Illinois, and lean away from relying on its pitching staff, which needs more time to develop.

Key matchups

With that being said, the two teams are met close to evenly on the offense, with both having strong individual players over a .300 batting average. For the blue and white players like Michael Anderson, Bartnett and Jayden Davis have led the pack, but haven't been enough to translate into overall runs.

For Illinois, their two key hitters have been AJ Putty and Nick Groves, both hitting impressive figures at .368 for Putty and .341 for Groves. These players will be the focus for the Nittany Lion pitching staff in order to limit runs on the board.

Something that could come in handy this weekend for Penn State are their players, like Anderson and Bryce Molinaro, known for their home runs.

Anderson posts the highest on the team this season with ten, while Molinaro has six. Both are star players who have the potential to change the course of the game with one swing of the bat.

On the mound

Defensively, Penn State will face the most competition; Illinois comes in with a significantly lower ERA, the team average landing at 4.42 compared to Penn State’s 8.04.

Pitchers like Reagan Hall for the Fighting Illini post ERA figures as low as 2.95, where the familiar faces of the pitching staff for Penn State, like Sanchez, arrive at 8.40, while Ethan Bauerschmidt sits at 9.24. No Nittany Lion arm posts an ERA lower than 4.00, except Michael Anderson, who has seen only 1.2 innings of work.

With a Big Ten matchup set for the weekend, there’s a potential for a packed park, which could help bring momentum in the bullpen, giving the Nittany Lions the chance to show who they really are.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘It’s been great’ | Penn State players explain benefits of morning practice
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Marcus Neal Jr. is used to practicing in the morning after spending the last two seasons playing under Matt Campbell.

Cooper Cousins, who’s spent his last two years playing under James Franklin, is not.

Cousins and Penn State are currently getting their first taste of a spring ball run by Campbell, which includes starting the day on the practice field at 9 a.m. The early feedback has been positive, though, as Cousins said there’s nothing better than waking up and playing football.

“Definitely different, definitely a change. That's what comes with it. Definitely getting into the flow of things now,” Cousins said. “Really at this point, no excuse. Who cares if everything's new? We've got to just roll with the flow now. It's been great.”

Campbell said Tuesday he began implementing morning practices during COVID-19, but went back to the afternoon schedule in 2021. He immediately regretted it, as his players’ academic success and on-field success slipped.

Usually, though, Campbell would do morning practices in the fall and afternoon in the spring. But he decided to change that after glowing academic results — Iowa State had a team GPA of around 3.4 the last three fall semesters with morning practice implemented.

“I really love this morning block,” Campbell said. “We did it this spring just to make sure we can work out the kinks for the fall. But I think those things are really big. It gives us the ability to really have a great rhythm to our day, and it gives us the ability to start the day right. And I think the other thing once you get into season, by the time it’s Thursday, you get off the field, our guys physically can be their best by the time they get back on the field on Saturday.”

Neal said when he first arrived at Iowa State, he felt a bit “iffy” about practicing in the morning, but now that he's fully adjusted, he feels the benefits are hard to ignore.

With morning practice, players get the opportunity to have treatments done in the afternoon, and it also gives them a chance to meet with their position groups to go over that day’s practice film.

“It's way more beneficial because I feel like it gives you extra time on the back end of the day to really hone in on some of the things, like treatment, nutrition, stuff like that, instead of going your whole day and then practicing,” Neal said.

Neal said he wakes up around 5:50 a.m. each day to get to the facility around 6:15 a.m. for breakfast and various other responsibilities. That does mean, however, that Neal ends his days early, around 8:30 or 9 p.m.

Safety Jamison Patton, who, like Neal, came to Penn State from Iowa State, enjoys practicing in the morning because he doesn’t come to the practice field tired after spending all day in class and meetings.

“I think that's the biggest benefit, is like you get up and it's the first thing of your day, and then you have the rest of the day to watch film in the afternoon, and you got a lot of time on the back end to recover, like we got tomorrow off, so a lot of time to recover and get our things right,” Patton said.

It may take a few alarms and a scroll on the phone for some players, such as offensive tackle Malachi Goodman, to get up and to the facility, but every player wakes up with one thing on their mind now: football.

“It's the first thing on your mind when you wake up, so that's a good benefit,” Neal said. “You're not worrying about school in the morning, really, you come here, straight football. Getting ready for practice, and you in that mindset.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: 'No gray area’ | Penn State’s defense emphasizing ‘obnoxious communication’ in spring ball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
When new defensive coordinator D'Anton Lynn had his first meeting with Penn State’s defense, he introduced a phrase he expects his unit to use: “obnoxious communication.”

Two practices into spring ball, the returns of obnoxious communication are present. In particular, safeties coach Deon Broomfield explained what the philosophy looks like and how his position group has utilized it following Thursday’s practice.

“That means over-emphasizing your communication,” Broomfield said. “We talk about verbal and non-verbal, so whether it's hand signals, man, whether it's I'm giving a signal, I got to give it back, but leaving no gray area about what we're trying to do as a defense from a call standpoint.”

Broomfield added that overcommunicating will help prevent players from making assumptions on the field. He said if players are left to assume what’s needed, someone could be wrong, allowing an opposing offense to take advantage.

The communication also helps with building chemistry within Penn State’s defense. With a roster comprised of a diverse set of returnees and transfers from Iowa State, along with other programs, Broomfield said players are learning more about each other in these practices.

“I think it's been really interesting because you got a lot of different personalities,” Broomfield said. “So guys are kind of figuring out who's the loud guy, who's the kind of slow to speak, not follower, but man, who's going to really take charge? And the leadership aspect of it has really been coming to fruition. I think it's probably been the biggest thing.”

During Thursday’s practice, the secondary simulated jumping into passing lanes for its individual work.

While watching his position group perform, Broomfield said he has liked the communication between players, which would seemingly align with Lynn’s goal for obnoxious communication. He’s said players are ensuring that everyone is on the same page and is in the right spot for a play.

“I think (Lynn) wants, if you had to ask him, obnoxious communication. I think that's first and foremost,” Broomfield said. “He wants guys playing with extreme effort and really controlling the things that we can control right now.”

Penn State’s defense also uses obnoxious communication within each position group's meetings. Broomfield said everything is clear and concise under Lynn as everybody is using the same terminology, preventing any gray areas from one room to another.

For example, Broomfield said defensive back Zion Tracy has evenly split time between the cornerback meetings and safety meetings as he could have the potential to play three positions. Despite that, Broomfield said communication issues haven’t come up for Tracy with Penn State’s entire defensive unit operating under obnoxious communication.

“I think anytime you play defensive football, you want to make sure that the communication is imperative,” Broomfield said.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State men's basketball forward Miles Goodman entering transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State forward Miles Goodman will be in search of another collegiate team.

Goodman is entering the transfer portal after one season with UT Arlington, according to an X post by Revocruit Transfer Portal that Goodman reposted.

TP Breaking: Miles Goodman, 7’0” forward out of UT Arlington, is entering the Transfer Portal, he tells @Revocruit.The former 4-star recruit ranked No. 95 by 247Sports appeared in 13 games at Penn State Nittany Lions men’s basketball as a freshman before starting 16 games this… pic.twitter.com/2gouvLtMep— Revocruit Transfer Portal (@TP4PT) March 26, 2026

Goodman played in 30 games with the Mavericks this season, making 16 starts. He averaged 3.4 points and 2.7 rebounds with 45.2% shooting from the field in 13.3 minutes per game.

Goodman spent his freshman year in 2024-25 with Penn State, and he entered the transfer portal after only seeing action in 13 games. He originally committed to Oregon, but after he was charged with five misdemeanor counts of invasion of privacy and one misdemeanor count of unlawful dissemination of an intimate image, the Ducks cut ties with Goodman.

The 6-foot-11, 225-pound forward was a 247Sports composite 4-star in the 2024 class. Goodman ranked as the No. 108 player nationally and the No. 13 player from Washington.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Guy Gadowsky speaks on NCAA Tournament, importance of leadership
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
ALBANY, N.Y. — Penn State is in the NCAA Tournament for the third time in four years following another 20-win campaign.

The Nittany Lions won seven straight in January before closing the season on a dry spell. But none of that matters in the tournament, which for Penn State begins at 9 p.m. Friday at MVP Arena in Albany, New York, against No. 2-seed Minnesota Duluth.

Guy Gadowsky spoke to the media Thursday about making the NCAA Tournament, the importance of his team’s leadership and more.

Another tournament run begins

Fresh off the program's first Frozen Four, Gadowsky praised his team for managing high expectations.

The Nittany Lions were pegged as a top-five team in the USCHO preseason poll after adding Jackson Smith, a 2025 NHL lottery pick, and Gavin McKenna, the consensus No. 1 pick in the 2026 draft. High expectations often derail promising teams, especially when they’re pegged on young collegiate athletes naive to their potentially damaging effects.

But here Penn State is, in Albany, for another crack at its first NCAA title.

“Being here at the NCAA (Tournament) is the goal. We're really excited, proud and happy to be here,” Gadowsky said. “You look around the nation, at the great programs that aren't (in the tournament), you have to feel pretty good about yourselves. Really proud of the job that they have done.”

The importance of leadership

Gadowsky lauded his leadership group, which contains captain Dane Dowiak and alternate captains Jarod Crespo, Aiden Fink, Ben Schoen and Reese Laubach, for their guidance through a turbulent season. Few teams have faced the injuries the Nittany Lions have.

But they endured. And Gadowsky couldn’t be prouder.

“I think the story all year has to do with leadership. The leadership that was required this year was essential,” Gadowsky said. “It's different for this program than we've had to deal with before, since going to Frozen Four, having an outdoor game in front of 75,000 people, recruiting some of the top players available, the Gavin effect. There's been a lot of different distractions that we that the team has had to deal with. The leadership group has done an unbelievable job keeping this team positive and grinding.”

Gadowsky understands the spotlight intertwined with a Frozen Four run. He cited the rule changes — which allowed Canadian Hockey League players like McKenna to play collegiately — and last year’s success as things that brought increased attention to the program.

But he’s happy with how his players handled themselves amid a unique season in Happy Valley, one the team hopes continues beyond the Albany Regional.

“It was a new challenge for us, a new spotlight,” Gadowsky said. “There's a lot of mental gymnastics that go along with all of that. And you can easily make excuses for why you don't continue to grind. And these guys never did … They never once used it as an excuse, and they just found a way to continue to be positive and carry forward. And that comes from the locker room.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball set for weekend home series against NJIT
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returns to Rec Hall for the first time in a month when it hosts No. 19 NJIT for a two-match home series this weekend.

Friday’s match is set to get underway at 6 p.m., while Saturday afternoon’s showdown is scheduled for 3 p.m.

Last time out

The Nittany Lions are coming off a 3-1 victory against Ohio State on Wednesday, but suffered three straight losses on their West Coast road trip before that.

Penn State only won a single set in its time in California, coming against Pepperdine on March 11.

On the other end, No. 19-ranked NJIT is coming off a 3-2 victory over Princeton last Saturday.

Before edging out the Tigers, the Highlanders, now 13-6 on the year, were winners of back-to-back matches against St. Thomas Aquinas and Dominican (NY).

Best of the EIVA

Penn State, with a 4-0 record in the EIVA thus far, sits atop the conference standings, while NJIT sits one spot below with a current conference record of 5-1.

It’s not just the similar standings in the EIVA that the blue and white share with the Highlanders, though, as both squads have some of the best offensive and defensive players in the conference, with consistency and efficiency playing a key role.

Both NJIT’s Daniel Latkowski and Penn State’s Ryan Merk are recent EIVA Defensive Player of the Week award winners, with the Highlanders freshman sitting fourth in the conference in blocks per set, averaging 0.97.

Merk is all too familiar with the award, having just won it for the seventh time in his career. The six-foot libero leads the EIVA in digs, averaging 2.29 per set.

The redshirt senior isn’t the only Nittany Lion to lead a statistical category, though, as teammate Owen Rose leads both the conference and the NCAA in blocks per set, averaging 1.33.

The Highlanders’ Wiktor Nowak was also just named the EIVA offensive player of the week after garnering 28 kills against Princeton.

Nowak is second in the conference in kills per set, averaging 3.85, while being the conference’s point leader with 299 on the season.

Team Stats

The blue and white and the Highlanders sit in very similar positions in a few different categories in the EIVA, including points, hitting percentage, assists and opponent assists, kills and opponent kills and opponent digs.

Aside from sitting right next to each other in those categories, the two sides are on pretty opposite ends of the spectrum for just about every other statistical category.

Penn State is the best blocking team in the conference, averaging 2.70 blocks per set, thanks to the help of Rose. Meanwhile, NJIT is second-to-last, averaging 1.91 flat.

The Nittany Lions are also among the best in the conference in terms of digs, averaging 8.79 per set, while the Highlanders sit dead last, recording 7.79 per set.

To follow the same trend, the blue and white average 1.34 service aces per set – good for second-best in the conference. Once again, NJIT sits at the bottom of the barrel, recording 1.10 per set.

While only marginally different in many statistics in the conference, Penn State’s usually reliable offensive attacks might be enough to overwhelm the Highlanders so long as the blue and white’s defense holds up and shows why it sits at the top of so many categories.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball travels to Garden State to face off against Rutgers
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State travels to Piscataway, New Jersey, for a road series against Rutgers. After dropping two games in the first home series of the year against Wisconsin, the Nittany Lions get a chance to bounce back as they continue their 2026 campaign.

So far, the blue and white have struggled against conference opponents with a 1-5 conference record. This weekend is a pivotal time to see if Penn State can bounce back or continue its low standings on the Big Ten charts.

When the Nittany Lions faced the Badgers for their first home series, they had strong moments, which led to historic program records as infielder Jordan George and outfielder Breanna Hanik both had two home runs each.

Penn State continues its season at 6 p.m. on Friday with the first pitch of the series at Rutgers Softball Complex.

The Scarlet Knights are currently 17-15 in their 2026 season, and 3-6 in Big Ten play. Rutgers gets a shot at its second consecutive win after defeating Seton Hall 14-4 in six innings. This came after a three-game sweep against No. 8 UCLA.

Rutgers' Riley Hwang leads the lineup with a .402 batting average and a 1.174 OPS. The sophomore added two home runs under her belt against the Pirates, finishing the game 4-for-5 with five RBIs and four runs scored.

Junior infielder Sam Rohwer has also been successful at the plate, tallying the most homers for the squad with nine on the season.

Right-handed pitcher Kelsey Hoekstra has 65 strikeouts so far this season. After the first week of the season, Hoekstra received honors as the Big Ten Pitcher of the Week.

Rutgers currently sits in the middle of the Big Ten, in tenth place, while the blue and white are in the lower column, ranked 13th in the conference.

Having an overall better season record, Penn State is 20-11 in the 2026 campaign. Rutgers is 17-15 on the season, struggling more in the nonconference slate.

This weekend's series will be the 74th meeting between the two programs. The Nittany Lions have a 13-game advantage over the Scarlet Knights as the 43-30 all-time series record is in the blue and white’s favor.

Penn State coach Clarisa Crowell reported two injuries during her weekly media availability. Freshman infielder Kirsten Finarelli and junior infielder Kaylie Walters are out with unknown injuries, so the Nittany Lions may be without their services going forward.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey begins NCAA Tournament at Albany Regional against Minnesota-Duluth
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Back in February, Penn State coach Guy Gadowsky mentioned a checklist that has been hanging in the team locker room for over a decade, comprised of a list of program goals made before the team transitioned from club to Division I.

Since then, every goal has been checked off but one — a national championship.

The Nittany Lions will get a second-straight shot to complete their list, earning the No. 3 seed in the Albany Regional for the first round of the NCAA Tournament.

“We are absolutely thrilled to be in the tournament,” coach Guy Gadowsky said. “You look around the nation at the great programs, great teams that aren’t there, you have to be very, very excited and happy and proud of the group.”

The blue and white will take on second-seeded Minnesota-Duluth in the Regional Semifinals at MVP Arena in Albany, New York, set for a 9 p.m. puck drop on Friday.

Penn State will return to action after almost two weeks, coming off a 5-2 loss in the conference semifinals to Michigan, the eventual Big Ten Champions.

Two big names to watch this weekend will be the freshman duo, Gavin McKenna and Jackson Smith.

McKenna, recently named a Top-10 Hobey Baker Award Finalist, was the first-ever Penn State freshman, and third skater all-time, to eclipse 50 points in a season, earning the title of the Big Ten Scoring Champion after leading the conference offensively.

The projected No. 1 pick in this year’s NHL draft has been on a hot streak, tallying 15 points in his last six games, including a single-game program record eight points — one goal and seven assists — in the blue and white’s 11-4 win over Ohio State on Feb. 20.

“I just think that what he’s done, in terms of how he’s done it…he’s played with so many different guys, and to put up the numbers that he has,” Gadowsky said. “To do what he did in the toughest year of college hockey ever is incredible.”

Smith became the highest-drafted Nittany Lion in program history after being taken No. 14 overall in this year’s NHL draft, and solidified himself as one of the best defenders in the conference.

The Calgary, Alberta, native’s 11 scores set a new program record for single-season goals by a defenseman, leading all Big Ten blueliners and tying for fourth in the nation.

The second line trio of Matt DiMarsico, JJ Wiebusch and Charlie Cerrato will be another key component if the blue and white hope to make a deep run in the tournament.

Charlie Cerrato made his return from injury after almost a two-month absence in the Nittany Lions’ last regular-season contest against Wisconsin.

The 2025 second-round NHL Draft pick was one of the team’s top producers at the time of his injury, and registered his first goal since early January in the blue and white’s first-round win in the conference tournament against Minnesota.

DiMarsico is Penn State’s leading scorer at 18 goals, with Wiebusch not far behind at 16.

Earning the name of the hottest line in college hockey earlier this season, the group has cooled off slightly in the second half, but their unmatched chemistry and offensive creativity have played a big role in the Nittany Lions’ success this season.

Scouting the Bulldogs

Penn State draws a tough opponent in Minnesota-Duluth, the first time the two teams will meet in program history.

“They’re really an excellent team, really, really well coached,” Gadowsky said. “No matter who you get at this time of year, they’re going to be an excellent team.”

The college hockey power has won three national titles — the most recent a back-to-back run in 2018 and 2019 — and has appeared in the Frozen Four six times.

Since the NCAA switched to a regional format, Minnesota-Duluth is a perfect 12-0 in first-round games.

Earning the No. 2 seed, the Bulldogs hold a 23-14-1 record.

The NCHC unit made it all the way to its conference championship before falling in double overtime to Denver, which also earned a No. 2 seed in the postseason, and will play Cornell in the Loveland Regional.

Minnesota-Duluth has the second-best power play in the nation, headlined by standout brothers Max and Zam Plante. For a Penn State team that leads the NCAA in penalty minutes, staying out of the penalty box will be a crucial component of this Regional matchup.

“It’s very, very obvious how we have to approach this game in terms of discipline,” Gadowsky said. “I think it’s a good thing. It’s got the guys’ attention, that’s for sure.”

The Bulldogs also boast the third-best penalty kill and a top-20 scoring offense.

The Plante brothers are some of the top producers in the NCAA, with Max Plante’s 24 goals tied for third nationally, and Zam Plante’s 10 power-play goals tied for fourth.

Max Plante, the younger of the brothers drafted in the second round of the 2024 NHL draft, recently earned the NCHC Player of the Year honors. Zam Plante, taken in round five of the 2022 NHL draft, earned a spot on the All-NCHC Third-Team.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State OL coach Ryan Clanton preaching dog mentality up front, for himself
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Ryan Clanton stood on the Holuba Hall turf wearing a black cutoff t-shirt with the word “dogs” written in white lettering below a graphic of a dog.

He had a black hat to match, also with the words “dogs” and “offensive line” written on it. On his office door, he has a sticker that says “beware of dog.” It’s a dog-like mentality that Penn State’s new offensive line coach has, and it’s a mantra that he instills in the players he coaches.

“You hear people all the time, even in football, they always talk about being a dog,” Clanton said. “Well for us, it's non-negotiable. You have to have that mentality. If you don't have it, you better find it.”

He found that mentality after being under-recruited out of Bakersfield Christian High School in Bakersfield, California, which led to him playing at the City College of San Francisco, where he lived in a garage for two years and ended up as an All-American. That led him to Oregon, where he ended his time as a captain on the offensive line.

Clanton then worked his way up the coaching ladder, using the mindset of being a dog to earn his keep across various stops, going from coaching at his high school alma mater to Ventura College, Northern Iowa, Iowa State and now Penn State.

The same approach he’s followed throughout his life is what he wants the players in the offensive line to go by, and, so far, Clanton is seeing returns on the investment.

“Luckily, even two days in, we're past the assignment phase and we're getting into body mechanics and how to be a strong, violent football player,” Clanton said. “That's like the key for the offense, young and old. And credit to the old guys here too, because they've been able to almost forget what they already know and learn something. And that's always a challenge.”

Breaking old habits can be an adjustment. All the Iowa State transfers and signees who flipped their commitment to Penn State learned from Clanton or planned on learning from Clanton, but Penn State returnees and other transfers are used to different coaches with different philosophies.

Clanton stressed things like gaining balance and running through contact as part of the adjustment, also citing what he looks for is more of an NFL model. He said there’s nothing wrong with how the other players have been taught, but that’s not how he does things.

“I think it's really the system that we teach. That's the biggest part,” Clanton said. “If there's a lot of memorization or complex things for them in the beginning, they're not gonna have very much confidence. We're trying to break some old habits and get those guys to run off the ball. That's the very first part of what we're trying to do.”

Clanton shared that offensive linemen can be one of two things. They can be a human shield or a hammer. While a human shield sits there and protects the quarterback, a hammer goes out and inflicts pain, making its vicious presence felt.

For Clanton, who has tattoo sleeves on both his arms and legs, it’s all about being a hammer.

If you look at his imposing physique as he barks out instructions at his unit on the practice field, it’s a hammer and dog-like mentality that shines through, for both himself and the offensive line.

“It's our job to get those guys confident, to have that mentality,” Clanton said. “But you got to be able to be violent. You got to want to be violent. You got to want to run through somebody's face. And that's a big part about playing o-line.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Men's lacrosse coach Jeff Tambroni talks UNC loss, upcoming Ohio State matchup
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is trying to build momentum as it has entered Big Ten play. The squad has won three of its last four games and is trying to win back-to-back games after defeating Maryland for the fourth time in team history.

Coach Jeff Tambroni spoke to the media today, and here’s what he had to say.

Scouting Ohio State

Penn State got bullied by Ohio State last season and lost 13-9. This season, Ohio State is the best defensive team in the country and has held every opponent to under 10 goals this season, which could force the Nittany Lions into a change of pace.

“The last couple of games our offense has been grinding offensive possessions, scoring opportunities, and you have to recognize that the defenses that we’ve played over the last couple weeks with North Carolina, Maryland and Ohio State absolutely no different are just super capable units,” Tambroni said. “You have to just move onto the next possession. I think that’s going to be key for us mentally, regardless of what happens. Move on to the next possession and just keep it positive, keep things flowing, and just figure out ways for success.”

In the net, Ohio State has Caleb "Big Tasty" Fyock, who likes to put his body in front of the ball. He’s currently second in goals against average with 6.61, which leads the Big Ten and is fourth in goals allowed with 51.

“You have to be super precise," Tambroni said. "So you have to take what you can get or what they’re giving up. I think you just have to believe that if you take your best shot in the right spot on the goal that you know it’s going to go in and I think that confidence will go a long way.”

Offense steps up

Hunter Aquino has missed the last few games for the blue and white. Despite that, the rest of the attack group has been a strength for the squad and has come up big when the team has needed them to.

“I mean I would say just the attack in general has done a terrific job," Tambroni said. "That’s a tough void to fill even though Hunter’s a young attackman, still growing. He’s got so much growth left in him. I would probably disperse that, the dealt of that has been filled by Kyle, Liam and Jack. I would say all three of them have done a terrific job.”

Mac Hobbs is someone who has heated up. He didn’t see any action in the first few games, but ever since he’s been inserted into the rotation, it has been hard to take him out.

“He’s just ready. He was ready to kind of make the jump," Tambroni said. "He didn’t play in the first six games, but because he just worked so hard every day and stayed focused, you know, instead of being disappointed that he wasn’t playing, he was ready when his number was called.

Preston Hawkins continues to develop

It feels like every week there’s something good that could be said about Preston Hawkins. The freshman has continued to impress, and his game has grown since his first appearance.

“I’m very impressed because that's not something that typically comes in your freshman year," Tambroni said. "This sort of wisdom, humility or resilience, depending on how things are going, but I will say that to his credit, he has rarely, if ever, changed his demeanor and I think it’s served him really well thus far."

You can only play one goalie, and Penn State has four on its roster. Despite the lack of playing time for the other three, the room seems to be tight-knit and very supportive of Hawkins.

“I would say Ben Johnson is phenomenal in his approach," Tambroni said. "I would say he’s probably taken away a little bit of work ethic. Willie Mitchell, who’s a senior, is the consummate teammate, one of the great teammates I’ve ever coached and Hayes Schreiner, I think, resiliency that he has learned from Hayes. The combination of the goalie room probably has been most significant.”

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State women's hockey goaltender Lauren Hernick enters transfer portal
author: Daniel Studer | The Daily Collegian
description:
After the Nittany Lions fell to Wisconsin in the semifinal round of the Frozen Four, goaltender Lauren Hernick entered the transfer portal, according to Gopher Puck Live.

From Frankenmuth, Michigan, Hernick did not appear for the Nittany Lions this season.

In her 2024-25 campaign with the Philadelphia Jr. Flyers, she went 8-3 in 11 starts with a 2.61 goals-against-average with four shutouts.

Hernick served as the third goaltender on the depth chart behind Katie DeSa and Maddy Campbell.

Campbell, most likely to be tabbed as the starter with DeSa graduating, went 6-2 with a .932 save percentage, 1.44 goals-against-average and four shutouts, the third-most in the nation among freshman goaltenders.

Joining the blue and white next season is Morgan Stickney, a two-time medalist in the U18 Women's World Championship. At the 2025 championship in Finland, Stickney and the United States fell to Canada 3-0.

In the six games she played during the tournament, Stickney went 5-1 and led all goaltenders with her 0.67 goals-against-average and .957 save percentage.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Josh Barr gets déjà vu recapping first national championship win
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Josh Barr made his way to the national championship bout last season, but was unable to get the job done. Barr finished in second and was on a mission to win his first national championship this season.

He was comforted by UFC fighter and former Penn State wrestler Bo Nickal last year and his teammates. But then, Barr flipped the script in 2025-26.

The sophomore had an undefeated season, was ranked No. 1 all year and was at the top of the podium to the end the season, winning his first national championship.

“Grateful for my lord and savior Jesus Christ, and that he’s blessed me to being in this position. Right before I walked out I got kind of Déjà vu. I remember being in the tunnel last year and being so grateful to compete,” Barr said. “It’s only right that I give back to him because all this is his, and all this is going to come and go, but Jesus Christ doesn’t.”

Barr lost to Stephen Buchanan of Iowa last season in the national championship. This season he was the wrestler on top of the podium and defeated Oklahoma State’s Cody Merrill 6-2.

That was Barr’s first match this season that he didn’t earn bonus points in.

“He’s a tough kid. He wrestles in a really good position, and he’s got heavy hips. I think I probably didn’t open up as much in that match as I have the matches prior, and I’ll kick myself for that for a while not for the bonus rate or anything because I left a little out there,” Barr said. “I’m not even breathing heavy, hard right now, so I’m not really happy about that.”

In last season's NCAA Championships, Barr wrestled injured. It was known that he had sustained an injury at the Big Ten Championships and it seemed to have affected his performance at the NCAA Championships.

“I was motivated, but I think last year being really grateful to be there ended up taking second or whatever, I was grateful for that, grateful for this year,” Barr said. “It’s not even so much like I’ve mentioned improving results or improving awards, finishes, whatever it is, it’s improving myself from match to match and just doing the best I can to serve the Lord.”

With Barr’s performance this season he is one of the finalists for the Hodge Trophy, which is awarded to the best pound-for-pound wrestler, but Barr isn’t the only candidate from the Nittany Lions. Mitchell Mesenbrink enters as the favorite to win it, and other candidates are Luke Lilledahl and Levi Haines.

But to Barr, it’s not really about winning the award, more so improving both for himself and his peers.

“I think that we’re just focused on pushing each other to be better and better every day. Super grateful for my teammates, and yeah, it’s not really a conversation we have,” Barr said of the award.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's lacrosse's Delaney Radin named to Tewaaraton Award Watch List
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn Stater is eligible for the biggest award.

Attacker Delaney Radin was named to the Tewaaraton Award Watch List on Thursday after a record-setting start to her junior year.

saw this one coming‼️Congrats to Delaney for being named to the 𝐓𝐞𝐰𝐚𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐧 𝐖𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝐋𝐢𝐬𝐭‼️ pic.twitter.com/rwxuB9U4fm— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) March 26, 2026

Radin has 35 assists this season, leading both her team and the Big Ten. Against Colgate earlier this year, she tied the school record assists in a single game, dishing eight assists.

Radin also has 15 goals this year, despite staffing just one game.

Fellow attacker Erika Ho was also named to the watch list earlier this year.

title: Practice observations | Plenty of injury updates with Penn State’s spring practice underway
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
After a long offseason and an even longer regular season for Penn State, the team returned to the practice field for the first week of spring practice with the Matt Campbell era underway.

Holuba Hall was packed from sideline to sideline with recruits, families and former players, like offensive tackle Drew Shelton and linebacker Mike Hull, as the squad went to work with quarterbacks in purple jerseys, the offense in blue and the defense in white.

Here’s what spring ball looked like on Thursday morning.

Injury updates

The first notable missing figure was Iowa State transfer quarterback Alex Manske, who is the favorite to be the backup. Per Sean Fitz of On3, Manske is set to miss spring ball.

Sticking with the quarterback room, Rocco Becht wasn’t a full participant as he works his way back from a torn labrum in his non-throwing shoulder and an AC joint sprain in his throwing shoulder.

He went through some agility work and passing drills that involved short throws to his position mates and the coaching staff, but for the most part was helping direct traffic and offering encouragement to his teammates.

On one end of the field, wide receivers in Chase Sowell, Brett Eskildsen and Peter Gonzalez, defensive end Max Granville and safety Christian Askew worked with assistant strength and conditioning coach Jalyn Baker, who joined the staff after spending last year at LSU.

Sowell and Eskildsen will be limited in spring ball, as Campbell shared on Tuesday. Granville is coming off a long-term injury suffered in the summer. The status of Gonzalez, who wore No. 7 instead of No. 84 during the viewing session, and Askew is unknown.

On the other end of the field, defensive tackles Siale Taupaki and De’Andre Cook, linebacker Tony Rojas and Ethan Black were also rehabbing, mainly doing sprints. Taupaki, Cook and Black are all new sightings on the injury-related front, while Rojas is coming off a torn ACL last year, and likely won’t be back for spring ball, but he said in February there’s a chance.

Linebacker Alex Tatsch, who sustained an injury during practice in the buildup to the Pinstripe Bowl, was on the field with a sleeve on his left leg. Both Tatsch and Rojas briefly hopped into a walk-through rep with the full defense toward the end of the viewing session.

Defensive end Mason Robinson made his return to the practice field after being sidelined all of last year with an injury. Center Dom Rulli was also not seen.

Other names like tight ends Andrew Rappleyea and Gabe Burkle, offensive tackle Anthony Donkoh and safety Jeremiah Cooper, who are all set to miss spring ball, were expectedly not seen participating in any capacity.

Personnel

It’s still early, and only the second practice, but there were a few things to take note of as far as the pecking order amongst position groups.

With Becht limited and Manske sidelined, it was Christopher Newport transfer Connor Barry who took the first-team reps at quarterback.

The left-handed Division lll transfer has thrown for 5,561 passing yards and 55 touchdowns, as well as 596 rushing yards and 18 touchdowns on the ground in his career. He’s also a two-time All-American, and last year was a semifinalist for the Gagliardi Trophy, which is the divisions' equivalent of the Heisman Trophy.

Behind him was freshman Peyton Falzone, walk-on Jack Lambert and freshman Kase Evans.

For the running backs, it was Iowa State transfer Carson Hansen who led the way for the drill work. Ohio State transfer James Peoples and Quinton Martin Jr. rotated between going second and third in drills, while Cam Wallace and freshman D’Antae Sheffey trailed behind.

For the offensive line, Texas State transfer Brock Riker held down the starting reps at center. At left tackle, it was Malachi Goodman and Garrett Sexton on the other side of him. The pair is likely in contention with Owen Aliciene, who backed up Goodman, for the other starting spot, with Donkoh projected to be the starting right tackle when he returns from injury.

For wide receivers, Koby Howard and Karon Brookins held down what were the first-team reps at the time. Howard, alongside Sowell and Eskildsen, are expected to be the starters, but Campbell had high praise for Brookins on Tuesday, calling him a “super talent.”

There was a trio of players taking reps at punt returner in Howard and cornerbacks Zion Tracy and Xxavier Thomas.

Drill work

After starting practice with stretching, featuring plenty of high-fives from Campbell, the team broke into individual drills.

The quarterbacks worked on quick throws to start, beginning the rep with backwards shuffling to simulate dropping back into the pocket.

Running backs worked on ball security, with position coach Savon Huggins punching at the ball, with assistant Sheldon Croney Jr. having them avoid a trash can.

Wide receivers worked on quick routes, then progressed into a ball security drill that required them holding the football under one arm and catch a tennis ball with their other hand.

There were only four healthy tight ends, so the quartet saw plenty of reps catching passes. The offensive line swapped personnel quickly, but often rotated which side of the line of scrimmage they were on.

The defensive line worked on quick sprints, then bending before exploding to pick up a yoga ball rolled at them.

A similar sight from a season ago, the linebackers worked on footwork, finishing the rep by hauling in a pass.
