The secondary practiced punching the ball out, then moving on to work that involved simulating jumping passing lanes.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: ‘Get ready to rock’ with Penn State Thespian Society's ‘Jesus Christ Superstar’
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Penn State Thespian Society presented “Jesus Christ Superstar” this week at Schwab Auditorium.

The musical, by Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice, is a rock-inspired retelling of the crucifixion of Jesus Christ through the eyes of Judas Iscariot. Thespians brought the story to life on campus with bright lights, powerful vocals and energetic choreography, though it’s not exactly the original Biblical story.

The show’s director, Becca Williams, said the difference in the musical’s version of scriptural events is one thing she always wants to stress to audiences.

“I encourage audiences to get rid of their previous notions of the characters and the figures that are in the Bible,” Williams, a second-year studying English, said. “We all know the story — we know how it happens — we know what happens after the show. But I really think that there’s something special about being able to indulge in this performance and see the characters as neither entirely good nor entirely bad.”

Williams said the duality of humanity showcased in the performance makes us feel differently than we may traditionally. We might feel bad for Judas at some moments and be annoyed with Jesus at others because of the way this particular show tells their story.

But, this wasn’t the story Williams was expecting to direct. Williams thought she was directing “Mamma Mia!,” and she had to take a last minute deep dive into the world of “Jesus Christ Superstar” when the troupe changed directions. She said she’s proud of the cast’s ability to take on such a vocally challenging piece.

Ava Zimic, a member of the show’s ensemble, stressed how hard the cast has been working on the production since their auditions, which were at the beginning of the semester.

“The music's really difficult for the cast, but we have a great time singing it,” Zimic, a second-year student studying secondary education said. “We love being on stage, and it's just a cool show. It's different. It's technically a rock opera, so get ready to rock and have a great time.”

One song the cast worked hard to perfect was the classic track “Gethsemane,” and Williams said the entire production played a role in the show’s success.

“I mean, our Jesus, I'm so glad we found him because I don't know if anyone else could have done it as well as him,” Williams said. “He's so talented. And everyone else in the cast is so incredibly talented, too.”

It wasn’t just the on-stage cast that made this show a reality though, as the backstage crew was also a big part of putting together the show’s sets and lighting components.

Alexandria Linke, the vice president of the Penn State Thespian Society, was the technology director of the show. She said it takes a strong group effort to bring their visions to life, from sets to props to light and sound to costumes.

“It's, like, totally different expertises each area,” Linke, a third-year studying political science, said.

The lighting was something special about “Jesus Christ Superstar” that surprised Zimic.

“I didn’t realize until I started, but there’s a lot of strobe lighting,” Zimic said.

She recommended audiences prepare for that, especially if strobe lighting could be a concern, while they prepare to “get down” to songs like “The Temple,” which Zimic said is her favorite.

“It’s just a fun song to do, and I love my costume for it,” she said.

Williams also stressed the importance of fun in their production, and she likes to give the actors the freedom to define their characters.

“While I'm doing my blocking, I'm taking into account that they should be able to take creative freedom with this,” Williams said. ”So I never really give them super strict guidelines.”

That creativity shines throughout the show, from Judas’s studded leather belt to the ensemble shimmying for Christ.

“Jesus Christ Superstar” will be at the Schwab Auditorium Thursday, Friday and Saturday. Tickets are available for purchase online.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Column | Penn State needs to add women’s wrestling to truthfully say ‘Wrestling lives here’
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
When I walked into the Bryce Jordan Center, the home to at least one Penn State dual for 30 years, the jumbotron stated: “This is Penn State. Wrestling lives here.”

It is partially true, the Nittany Lions have won 14 national championships, five Hodge Trophy winners and 65 individual champions. However, the lack of a women’s program has long been a stain on the wrestling program’s 117-year legacy.

In January 2025, the NCAA officially added women’s wrestling as the 91st NCAA championship sport, 33 years after the University of Minnesota-Morris was recognized as the first varsity collegiate women’s wrestling program.

In March 2026, the NCAA hosted the first NCAA Women’s Wrestling Championships in Coralville, Iowa. The competition crowned its first individual and team champions in women’s wrestling, with McKendree earning the team title with 171 points.

Shortly before the final matches at the men’s championships in Cleveland, Ohio, the women’s individual champions were recognized.

Among those were stars like Iowa’s Kennedy Blades, Lehigh’s Audrey Jimenez and McKendree’s Yu Sakamoto. Each of the champions donned their team’s respective colors. Penn State’s iconic blue and white was noticeably missing.

For Pennsylvania wrestlers, Penn State is a dream — one they must forfeit because of the lack of program. In order to compete at the collegiate level, 2024 Pennsylvania State Champion Avry Ryhal, a Mercer, Pennsylvania, native had to look elsewhere.

“My main goal as a kid was Penn State,” Ryhal said. “I unfortunately didn’t get that opportunity but I hope girls in the future do get that chance.”

Among the 54 teams represented at the 2026 NCAA Women’s Wrestling Championships, only four of those teams — Iowa, Lehigh, Sacred Heart and Lock Haven — were featured in the men’s championships as well.

“I never thought that I would be a college wrestler,” Ryhal said. “I was able to participate in nationals last year, but it wasn’t official, but it becoming official just means so much more to me, like all my hard work actually means something now.”

Penn State is in a unique position to grow the sport due to its dominance, and the program is a main reason the 2028 NCAA Championships are being held in U.S. Bank Stadium, the Minnesota Vikings’ football stadium.

The wrestling team brings in over $1 million in ticket revenue, rivaling the revenue of the Penn State men’s basketball and hockey teams which are historically more popular.

The program has too much influence to not display any support or commitment for a women’s team in the first years of the sport being in the NCAA.

“Imagine if we had Penn State, or we had Ohio State, or we had like Michigan in that mix,” Rhyal said. “That would just create so much more diversity in the sport.”

Penn State has a major influence on young wrestlers. There are kids in the sport who dream about wrestling in the BJC in a blue and white singlet. As of today, that isn’t possible for women wrestlers and for that Penn State should be deeply ashamed.

“Sometimes I was like, ‘Do I want to continue?’ like ‘is it even going to grow?’” Rhyal said, referring to how discouraging it was growing up and seeing no women wrestlers at the NCAA level.

But the gradual growth of women’s wrestling on the national level indicates that the addition of a women’s team is not far away. As conversations ramp up at several power programs, we should expect Penn State to begin talks soon. And if they don’t, the university should be ashamed of its role in destroying the dreams and opportunities of many high school wrestlers.

“I see it even growing bigger than men’s wrestling, like women’s wrestling has saved the sport of men’s wrestling,” Ryhal said. “The addition of the girls in the NCAA program, or even just high school programs saved the sport of wrestling.”

To carve the road to implementing a women’s wrestling program, Penn State should first add a female prominent regional training center, like the men's affiliated Penn State’s Nittany Lion Wrestling Club. From there, the program can add more women wrestlers and make the jump to the NCAA level, further growing the sport.

If there’s not any Penn State representation, or any women’s singlets with “NLWC” on it at the 2028 or 2032 USA Olympic Team Trails, then it’s a complete lie for Penn State to say wrestling lives here.

“I think in the next five to 10 years, I hope that some of these bigger programs step on it and make these programs, because there’s going to be so much more girls wrestling,” Ryhal said.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: 30 years of Penn State women’s hockey from the Collegian archives
author: Mercedes Hamilton & Myles Hannak
description:
What began three decades ago as a student-founded club program culminated last weekend in the most accomplished season for Penn State women’s hockey. The Lady Lions made history with its first appearance at the Frozen Four, just weeks after four players represented their countries at the 2026 Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games.

Take a look back at the program’s evolution through the Collegian archives.

1996-97: Humble beginnings

Eighteen young women hit the rink on Oct. 30, 1996, for the then-Lady Icers’ first practice. The team was started by Ellen Bradley and Kathy Beckford and coached by Vinnie Scalamogna, who was the assistant manager and programs director of the Penn State ice pavilion at the time.

Scalamogna told the Collegian that the team would function within the intramural ice hockey program and later petition the student government for club team status. As they worked to get the team off the ground — a process he acknowledged wouldn’t be short or easy — he said the athletics department and then-men’s club hockey coach Joe Battista were supportive.

"Eventually maybe it will become like the Icers," Scalamogna said. "I'd like to have half a dozen games and get some interest. The bottom line is to have fun.”

The Lady Icers later played its first game in January 1997 against the Harrisburg Rockettes, which was a newly-formed travel team of women aged 21-45. Penn State won 5-4.

According to Collegian reporting, the Rockettes tied the game at 2-2, 3-3 and 4-4 before center Ellen Zajko scored a game-winning goal with 2:21 left in the game. She was later named MVP of the game and went home with the puck.

“I’m very psyched for the next game,” an emotional Zajko said after the game. “It was a really exciting win. It was a good game to watch, very evenly matched. That’s how hockey’s played.”

Though the Collegian doesn’t appear to have any record of when the student government officially approved the team’s club status, the archives do document a March 1997 game against Rutgers in which the Lady Icers are identified as a club team.

While the Collegian also doesn't have a record of that game’s score, it reported in December 1997 that the team played Rutgers again at the start of its second season and lost 3-1. Later that same weekend, the Lady Icers faced Chatham College and fell 6-4.

“We’ve got a couple of big games left next semester,” then-Assistant Coach John Phillips said. “Hopefully, we can get things rolling and get some momentum so when next semester comes around, we can keep going.”

1998-99: The Lady Icers compete in first collegiate tournament

In February 1998, 13 players made their way to Potsdam, New York, to play in its first collegiate tournament. Hosted by Clarkson University, the All-East Regional Club Championship Tournament gave the team the opportunity to go head-to-head with other club teams in the region.

“We are not going to get any better as a team until we start playing teams that are better than us,” said goaltender Missy Werner, who recorded her first-ever shutout that weekend over Syracuse University.

The Lady Icers fell 8-2 to Massachusetts, who was the tournament’s runner up, and 8-0 to Trinity College. Still, then-coach Jessica Ferrer was optimistic and learned from the game, including the need to be more aggressive.

“I was very pleased with our team’s performance,” Ferrer said. “We played some teams that could go varsity very soon. They have an established backbone, something we are trying to achieve.”

The following year, the team played its best season in its three-year history with a record of 9-4-1.

2000-01: The Lady Icers become MAWCHA champions after an undefeated season

With just one minute and 21 seconds on the clock, the Lady Icers called a timeout, knowing the University of Pittsburgh needed just two goals to tie the game. Penn State entered the Mid-Atlantic Women's Collegiate Hockey Association (MAWCHA) tournament as the No. 1 seed, desperate to remain undefeated.

The team claimed a 2-0 win over the No. 2 seed Ice Panthers after a heated season rivalry. The Lady Icers beat Pitt in 3-1 and 7-1 in regular season games.

"I'm just assuming that we will be coming home with the first-place trophy. I love banners. I love trophies. I'm very materialistic like that. I can't wait to bring one home this weekend," Pittsburgh's Jess Preite told the Pittsburgh Tribune Review before the tournament, according to Collegian archives.

The Lady Icers read the story before the game, saying it motivated them more.

2001-02: The Lady Icers are invited to compete in its first national competition

The team was one of eight schools to play in the inaugural American Collegiate Hockey Association’s (ACHA) national competition in April 2001.

The Lady Icers faced schools out west for the first time, falling 3-1 in its first game against Arizona State University. The team later fell to Pitt and the University of Wisconsin.

Despite the loss, the team still gained a valuable learning experience, strong sportsmanship and even a piece of memorabilia placed in the Hockey Hall of Fame in Toronto alongside items from the other competitors.

“It’s great to be a part of the history of women’s hockey,” said center Ellen Zajko, who was named one of the strongest players of the tournament.

The following season, the team finished third, and Lady Icers forward Andrea Lavelle was named ACHA Women’s Division Player of the Year.

2012-13: Move to the big stage

The Lady Icers played their last game in the ACHA, a league formed in 1991 and originally built to aid the transition to D1 hockey. When that goal didn’t come to fruition, both the women’s and men’s teams left for the next level.

For the women’s team, it would now participate in the College Hockey America conference, which was eventually renamed to the current league the squad participates in today, the Atlantic Hockey America conference.

“We thought that the ACHA would foster that kind of a movement [to the D1 level],” Battista said. “But, given the cost of college athletics and issues with facilities and Title IX and budgets, the only way you are going to get it is if a Terry Pegula shows up. This would not have happened, of course, without Terry and Kim.”

Former Penn State men’s club team member Josh Brandwene joined to coach the squad in its inaugural D1 season, in which it finished 7-26-2. As for the ACHA, the league is still in operation, with Penn State hockey clubs participating.

2014-15: First home playoff series

For the first time in program history, Penn State hosted a playoff series in Happy Valley at Pegula Ice Arena, which was only in its second full year of operation after opening in 2013. The game would be a sign of what was to come, both on and off the ice.

In the first postseason series at the venue, the blue and white defeated Lindenwood with a combined score of 4-1 over a pair of wins to advance to the team’s first playoff semifinal. In the next round Penn State was sent home by Syracuse, but the program tasted its first bit of success.

In 2016, the Collegian launched an investigation of coach Brandwene for alleged emotional abuse. The findings led to an athletic evaluation of the coach, who retired from coaching a year later.

2017-18: Jeff Kampersal joins Penn State, first pro contract

In 2017, the blue and white snagged coach Jeff Kampersal from Princeton University, following prior success with Guy Gadowsky, another former Princeton coach hired to lead the men’s hockey team in 2011.

Kampersal recorded the most wins in program history and has been in charge for all of Penn State’s most successful seasons. In 2026, Kampersal was named the top head coach in all of D1 women’s hockey.

Also in 2017, Cassie Dunne became the first Penn State women’s hockey player to ink a professional contract.

“I always thought ice hockey ended after college,” Dunne said. “I never thought about playing after school, but after I played for Team USA, it solidified that hockey had more for me and I wasn't ready to give up that sport that I have been playing my whole life.”

2020-21: AHA Accolades

Penn State continued to make history and venture into uncharted territory. Former Nittany Lions’ goalie Jenna Brenneman became the first Penn State alum to hear her name called at the National Women’s Hockey League draft.

Brenneman was drafted No. 24 overall in the fourth round of the 2021 NWHL Draft and was the first goalie off the board.

In that same year, the 2020-21 Penn State unit finished with the CHA regular season title for the first time, but failed to make an NCAA Tournament appearance after a postseason loss in the CHA semifinals.

The season remained historic and crucial to program history, however, as Kiara Zanon earned the program's first AHA Player of the Year Award.

2025-26: Frozen Four

The year started with buzz, as the Frozen Four returned for the first time since 2022.

Throughout the course of the season, the Nittany Lions broke their attendance record for a women’s Penn State hockey game twice. The first of which came after the first-ever hockey game inside of Beaver Stadium, but that figure was over doubled in the blue and white’s final game of 2026.

“Here at Penn State, we get so much support it’s insane,” forward McKenna Walsh said. “I think that people are starting to realize women’s sports really matter. So, I think that was a big step for us, and then (fans) coming here supporting us, it’s super special.”

In the season finale, 5,176 people packed into Pegula Ice Arena for Penn State’s Frozen Four clash against Wisconsin, where the Nittany Lions fell short on an attempt to make ther first National Championship. Penn State had logged its highest USCHO ranking in history, No. 3, before the postseason clash.

It wasn’t just a Penn State record, however, but a new Women’s Frozen Four record, and the Nittany Lions’ captain, Tessa Janecke, put an exclamation point on the night with her 200th career point after already setting the program career point record earlier in the season.

“I couldn’t believe how packed it was,” Janecke said. “But we don’t just want that in a Frozen Four. We want that at every game, and I think we deserve it. You can see how much it helps the team on the ice when (the Roar Zone) is there supporting.”

A plethora of player personnel also logged new career records in the season’s journey. Defenseman Kendall Butze reached the top spot with career points by a defenseman, goalie Katie DeSa snagged the top spot in career shutouts, and forward Katelyn Roberts became the sixth skater to reach 100 career points, marking the first African American to do so in Penn State women’s hockey history.

All those new additions to the record book, but one moment shines at the pinnacle. An all-time high four Penn State women’s hockey athletes participated in the 2026 Milano-Cortina Olympic Games, with Janecke bringing home the gold, an achievement never reached prior.

Janecke, the only player to earn AHA Player of the Year twice for Penn State, had her miraculous season earn the program’s first Patty Kazmaier top-three nomination. But despite not earning the award and her last collegiate game ending in defeat, Janecke stands as a program icon.

The program’s future appears to be heading in a new direction, but it’s certain that it wouldn’t be possible without the hundreds of women who skated before them and made women’s hockey at Penn State possible.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | Senoritis, laziness, or…?
author: Jadzia Santiago | The Daily Collegian
description:
I don’t want to do anything anymore.

That’s it, that’s the column. Goodbye and goodnight.

Just kidding, of course. As you know, getting me to shut up is a lot harder than that. But I’ll admit that as I’m writing this column (late, mind you), I keep stopping to blankly stare at it.

Honestly, I like many others I know have been having one of those months that’s just one thing after another, to a near-comical extent. If you told me that in 5 minutes my laptop would burst into flames, I’d just think, Yeah, sounds like my March (Why is it always March?).

So, no surprise at the apathy and despair there. But there’s been something else going on underneath for us, and I’ve been poking at what it is.

For some of us, it’s just a regular post-Spring break slump or mental health stuff we’re already managing. For others, with the weather getting warmer and the end in sight, we’ve given up on this year.

And for my seniors, the sweet luxury of senioritis is now something we can afford. Call me Candace from “Phineas and Ferb”, because I also gave up — not even going for the bronze!

(If you’re any of my current or future graduate instructors in my five-year program, I’m kidding and excited for next year. Of course.)

But what about when it’s not any of that? When your mental health is steady, you’re unfazed by Spring and you don’t have (or qualify for) senioritis, what do you call that feeling of demotivation?

Well, process of elimination would tell you that you might just be experiencing something called burnout, which is defined as “a state of emotional, mental, and often physical exhaustion brought on by prolonged or repeated stress” and is caused by a variety of factors, according to Psychology Today.

It’s hard to find many stats on this, but it’s safe to say that if this is you, you’re far from alone. Alongside oodles of anecdotal evidence, claims about the percentage of college students who experience burnout or its symptoms range between 50-60%.

Burnout has lots of symptom overlap with depression and the dreaded senioritis. So, how do you tell the difference, especially if you’re looking to reach out for help?

Well, obviously, I can’t diagnose you (if there’s such thing as the opposite of a mental health professional, it’s me), but there are some distinctions.

Unlike depression (but like senioritis), burnout tends to be based on one situation, such as a job or, in our case, school, according to Mental Health America. But burnout, especially less severe cases, can be helped by taking a step back, rest, or a shift in priorities.

However, burnout’s situational nature seems to be different from senioritis’, as it seems that senioritis is more situational than burnout.

Not only does senioritis occur at a specific point in your academic career, it doesn’t usually get rid of your excitement for what happens after, like a job, graduate school or professional school. On the other hand, burnout and depression can do exactly that.

I’ve found it helpful to ask myself, “Do I not want to be an undergraduate anymore, or do I not want to be a student or do anything in my field anymore? Am I tired of writing essays for class, or am I tired of writing altogether? And would this be fixed by a real, fulfilling break?”

However, this isn’t to say that those three things can’t coexist or feed into one another. There’s a ton of overlap that a trained mental health professional can help you navigate.

But the fact that these are distinct conditions and circumstances does still matter — tremendously so. It’s like the difference between a tension headache, caffeine headache and a migraine.

Sure, they all make your head hurt, but the approach to them is different, and in many cases, it’s harmful to dismiss one as the other — especially because they each indicate different underlying causes.

No matter where you are in your academic career, we’re at a very stressful time right now, and burnout and depression can happen to anyone. The existence of these conditions means it’s important not to dismiss your exhaustion and demotivation as laziness or senioritis.

Not only does self-dismissal lead to a lot of self-blame, it also leads to you not seeking help.

Trust me, just because burnout and its symptoms are common doesn’t mean they’re normal or should be normalized. Untreated burnout can not only lead to its symptoms worsening, but a loss of passion for the things you love most. Additionally, it can mask or worsen symptoms of depression and other mental health conditions.

And I understand the ridiculousness of me saying this in a world where taking a break is either frowned upon or impossible, which is why we as a collective need to recognize the impact burnout can have.

There’s not much we can individually do about the stacks of assignments due or the fact that summer seems eons away (Punxsutawney Phil, when I catch you…), but we can do what we’re able to support one another.

As much as you shouldn’t dismiss yourself as lazy, don’t dismiss your friends as such, either. If you can, check in on your friends, be a shoulder to lean on or just someone to sit and study with and try to be supportive and encouraging when your friends open up.

My hope for you is that things can start looking up, that you’ll get to rest and that you’ll find your passion again. And when you get there, you have my full permission to forget the Alamo.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Column | The art of making your own map
author: Annelise Hanson | The Daily Collegian
description:
There’s a pressure, especially in your early 20s, to wait for your life to begin. To follow a path that’s already been mapped out. To make safe, reasonable decisions and hope they eventually lead somewhere meaningful.

But the more I travel, the more I realize a meaningful life doesn’t happen by accident, and it’s my responsibility to carve out a place for myself in the world. Small moments like an unexpected conversation, who you sit next to on a bus or a flight booked on impulse, a chance encounter can transmute your life. You can never plan or predict.

My past 20 years have become increasingly about following my instincts, developing my senses to be savvy and staying committed to my inborn curiosity. Where you book the flight, apply for the job, the internship, the scholarship you’re not sure you’re qualified for, because if you don’t bet on yourself, no one else is going to.

To say yes before you’re ready. Planting seeds without knowing exactly where they’ll take root. Following whatever thread of interest makes you feel alive, even if it doesn’t make sense to anyone else yet.

As women, we’re often told to search for love like it’s something to be found in a specific place, or in a specific person. But lately, I’ve started to think that kind of love is incomplete. The kind that actually sustains you shows up in art, in beauty, in the feeling of being fully present somewhere new.

Travel has made this impossible to ignore.

There’s a concept I keep coming back to, something I think of as “geographical luck” and John Rawls’s Veil of Ignorance. The idea that so much of who we are is formed by where we happen to be born, and how we would want the world to be if we had no idea what shoes we would be born into.

I’ve found myself observing people in different parts of the world and wondering why my life unfolded the way it did, while theirs took a completely different route. If they were given the same opportunities, would they cherish them more and excel in other ways? Maybe if we were the same age at the same time, born in the same part of the world and spoke the same language, parts of our spirits may be the same.

We all laugh, cry, grieve and bleed the same. We share gestures, humor and interest. But the opportunity, access and expectation create entirely different realities and ways of seeing the world. We’re separated by borders, language, nationality and man-made division.

Because if so much of life is determined by circumstance, then the part that isn’t choice and risk matters even more.

I was walking around Florence with an English friend. We were discussing the cultural differences between Americans and Europeans. Americans always want more. Once you reach what you have always wanted, you move on to the next goal, instead of marveling at the reality you once dreamed of.

I find myself guilty of this all the time. I could always be more informed, more captivating, more accomplished, but why not find appreciation for where I am at this point in time?

However, if you don’t actively pave your own path, it will be carved for you, and you risk suffocation by your own stagnation. By your environment, by other people’s expectations, or by whatever feels easiest to follow. Carving your own path is a privilege in and of itself. Maybe there’s no correct way to look at it, just what brings you the most peace in a chaotic world.

On a tour recently, someone asked if my friend Sarah and I were sisters. We laughed and said no, but he shook his head and said, “You are.” He told us that we’re all brothers and sisters, all from the same father. Though the tour guide and I came from different faiths, at our core, we share humanity and being members of the human race. Suddenly, these divisions don’t feel as vast.

Then he said a word I’d heard before but never really thought about, inshallah, if God wills it.

I picked up an evil eye ring at the souks.

The vendor said it would protect me from bad energy, from people who wished me harm.

Throughout the Mediterranean to the Middle East, the evil eye was worn for centuries to deflect envy and misfortune.

Astrologically, I am a daughter of Jupiter (a Sagittarius rising). I have luck and jubilance everywhere I go, especially overseas.

It’s a kind of cosmic buoyancy that pushes me forward, even into unfamiliar places, even when I don’t know what to expect. The ring has become a physical manifestation of this belief system.

It came to symbolize the act of watching yourself, of noticing how you interact with life and how life interacts with you. Wearing it helps me cultivate a presence sharp enough to traverse the unpredictable, fragile and beautiful spaces I enter as I make my own path.

The ring was a small talisman, yes, but it was also a metaphor that the world will always be watching, and you must learn to look out for yourself with equal care.

I think traveling forces you to confront your place in the world, the privileges you carry, the choices you make and how you interact with places unbeknownst to you.

Perhaps the point isn’t to have everything figured out. Maybe it’s to stay in motion, to travel. To follow inquisitiveness and never keep your feet rooted and planted in your comfort zone, or you risk complicity in your transient existence.

To trust that the life you’re building, even though unfamiliar, will start to take shape.

Inshallah.

MORE ABROAD COLUMNS

title: Get 'hooked’ on Penn State's fly fishing club
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fly fishing season is coming up and Penn State has a group that is ready to reel in the fun.

The Penn State Fly Fishing Club is dedicated to supplying knowledge and tools to help its members succeed in the angling technique. Whether you're just starting out or already advanced, everyone is welcome to join.

From classroom meetings that go over tying demonstrations to practical outings where members have the opportunity to fish, Alex Lanyard, the vice president, said the club prepares everyone to get into the water, no matter how much prior fishing experience they have.

"We just want to teach people about the fly fishing opportunities up here and get them connected to the nature around them in State College," Lanyard, a fourth-year studying recreation, park and tourism management, said. "It's just so awesome to see someone hook their first trout ever and pull it in. The process of being able to work with someone like that on the water is probably one of the best feelings in the world for me."

Club president Sailor Walter said he views the club as a helpful resource for those who don't know where to fish, lack knowledge of the skills needed or even those who may not have a car, as the club helps members with all of that.

This idea of bringing new concepts to the table stems from the club having members from all over the country coming together to fish. Aaron Litterini spoke about the prestige of Penn State's fly fishing club and programs.

"With the club here, we're recognized as one of the best college fisheries in the country," Litterini, a fourth-year studying cybersecurity analytics and operations, said. "People come from all over the world to fish here, so being able to get out there on the water and exposing people to the ecosystem we have here and how special it is, is really big."

According to member Jordan Satterfield, the Penn State Fly Fishing Club was founded in 1990 by Penn State student John Toker, originally under the leadership of entomologist advisor Greg Hoover.

With the help of professors such as Hoover and knowledge from Joe Humphreys, a nationally known fly fisherman and former instructor of the Penn State Fly Fishing program, the club developed into what it’s today.

"There's a long legacy with fly fishing here," Satterfield, a fourth-year studying mechanical engineering, said. "Both at Penn State and in Pennsylvania as a whole."

For their fishing outings, the group tries to get out either weekly or biweekly, depending on the weather and other events.

Usually, they go to Spring Creek due to its close proximity and high-quality fishing grounds. There's no need to worry about equipment either, as the club provides most supplies needed to tie, including hooks and vices, and five rods.

"We really do believe in keeping the barrier to entry low," Walter, a fourth-year studying geography, said. "Not everyone knows if they're going to like it right off the bat, so if they can not spend too much money, see if they like it and then go from there, we believe that's the right way to go."

As the weather gets warmer, and fishing season begins, the club makes sure to be conscious about keeping the environment clean. Litterini explained during this time, fish are spawning, so the club makes an effort to educate members on not interrupting any natural breeding grounds.

"Just standing out in Spring Creek, a lot of people don't know what Pennsylvania has to offer until they see it,” Satterfield said. “A lot of the friends I've taken out fly fishing for the first time say they never even realized this spot, which is so gorgeous, is just 10 minutes off campus. You keep in mind your daily habits of keeping those areas clean, so future kids can have the same opportunity to catch a fish in that stream."

Besides the fishing outings, the club also promotes other events going on in the State College fly fishing community. In the past, some opportunities the club has participated in were casting demonstrations by renowned fly fishermen such as Humphreys and Daniel, which were big hits.

"Just listening to his (Humphreys') wisdom was amazing," Satterfield said. "And just seeing his trademark bow and arrow cast at 97 years old was remarkable."

The club’s events help bring members closer, and Walter said the sense of community the group has built is incredible.

"Personally, I've met some of my best friends from the club," Walter said. "Between the in-classroom meetings and being out on the water, you do have a lot of time to get to know these people."

Lanyard said another great benefit of the club is the mental health aspect.

"When I'm worried about my upcoming exam or an assignment, there's no better way to take yourself out of that than to go out and wade in a creek and just listen to the water," Lanyard said. "The club also gives me a chance to talk about something that I'm super passionate about."

The club encourages everyone to come and check out what the Fly Fishing Club is all about, even if they're a bit hesitant.

"It feels like a family and is very inclusive," Litterini said. "People are more than happy to help teach each other how to fish, and you being out there, soaking up the sun in a cool stream — there's nothing like it."

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: ‘Feminism is for everybody’ | Gen Z advocates at Penn State continue to fight for women's rights
author: Jade D’Agostino l The Daily Collegian
description:
As Women’s History Month marks decades of progress, Penn State students say the fight for women’s rights remains urgent in this new digital age.

A number of Penn State women’s groups are pushing for more activism and discussion within Generation Z (Gen Z) around issues women are facing in the world. Olivia Podskoch said activism has always been a large part of her college experience.

“There's been a huge push to silence young women, and it's unfortunate,” Podskoch, a fourth-year studying criminology and women's, gender and sexuality studies, said. “We need to start listening to young women, because it's the future.”

Podskoch serves as the president of Triota, the women’s, gender, and sexualities studies honor society, and said having a smaller, like-minded group at Penn State makes it easier to mobilize and advocate.

Emma Russell said every issue women face is urgent, and the state of women’s rights should anger everyone.

“If it affects one of us, it affects all of us,” Russell, a fourth-year studying biology and women's, gender, and sexuality studies, said. “Feminism is for everybody.”

Russell, a longtime member of Triota, said even if you aren’t directly affected by women’s rights issues, you should speak up on behalf of those who are.

Since the overturning of Roe v. Wade in 2022, which ended the federal right to abortion and returned the decision to the states, some students say reproductive rights are fundamental for women.

Russell said she remembers where she was upon learning of the Supreme Court decision.

“I had just graduated high school because it was June of that year … I was honestly sitting at home scrolling and it came up on my feed, and I'm like, ‘There's no way,’ like, I knew this was in the news, but they actually did it,” she said. “Nobody stopped them.”

Brooke Benevento is the director of education for the Penn State Reproductive Justice Project, a student organization founded in 2023 that aims to advance reproductive justice efforts at Penn State. Benevento, a fourth-year studying rehabilitation and human services, said the U.S. has gone backward in the past few years when it comes to reproductive health.

“I want to say I'm optimistic about how it will be addressed in the future, because we have so many young activists … but I also want to be a realist in the fact that there's a lot of people in positions of power right now who don't want that to happen,” she said. “They say we hear you and we respect you, but they haven't acted on that.”

Amaris Fultz, a member of Triota, said the current administration is not listening to young women's concerns about their reproductive health.

“I would love if they would listen to young women more, I'd love if they’d listen to anyone more,” Fultz, a first-year studying film production, said.

Fultz said social media has changed modern-day feminism by shaping views of women. She added that while social media has introduced a range of perspectives, it also comes with drawbacks.

“It’s so easy to fall into a rabbit hole on social media because of algorithms and just stay there,” Fultz said, pointing to the dangerous cycle of platforms constantly reaffirming users’ beliefs.

Though the negative effects of social media are often unavoidable, Podskoch said Gen Z’s online space can also be positive, as it offers the opportunity to learn about other perspectives and a different knowledge of the world.

“It just opens the space for us to really communicate with others in a way that other generations haven't been able to do,” Podskoch said.

While social media platforms may offer some benefits to modern-day activism for women's rights, Eloise Emel said she still doesn't think it’s as productive as traditional methods. Emel, the director of advocacy at the Penn State Reproductive Justice Project, said reposting advocacy posts on social media can create a false sense of impact.

“I definitely believe that protesting in person is far more effective than anything like social media activism,” Emel, a second-year studying psychology and women, gender and sexuality studies, said. “A lot of coalition building and resistance building in general is mainly done in person … I feel like people almost fall into that repetitive trap of feeling like social media is enough when it's not.”

She said the dynamic is complicated, noting that while social media makes it easier to spread awareness and stay informed, she thinks some people post assuming it makes them a ‘good person.’ However, students engaged in activism beyond social media still face scrutiny.

“I feel like there is kind of a negative stigma surrounding the work that people in women's studies do,” she said.

Despite that stigma, Russell said while there may be concerns about protesting on campus, it shouldn’t stop people from speaking out. She said no matter what a person advocates for, there’s always going to be someone in disagreement.

She said she doesn’t think that making one group out to be the sole reason for an issue is a great way for coalition building. Progress requires openness and establishing a common ground, Russell added.

“If you're not willing to learn, there's no fixing it,” Russell said.

Drawing on her experience with the Reproductive Justice Project, Benevento said conversations about reproductive health and justice should focus on education rather than persuasion.

“The goal shouldn't be to change someone's thinking,” Benevento said. “It should just be to give them the education and the resources for them to be able to evaluate and do that on their own.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Hockey Valley history maker | Jackson Smith’s journey to record-setting freshman season
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Before Jackson Smith first donned a blue and white sweater, he was already rewriting the Penn State history books.

Since the 6-foot-4 freshman defenseman became the highest-drafted Nittany Lion in program history this summer, his record-breaking habits have only continued.

Smith has been carving out his blue and white legacy with every electrifying slapshot and unrelenting hit to the roar — or outcry — of the crowd, becoming one of the most premier blueliners in Hockey Valley history.

“The kid is an absolute thoroughbred; his physical talents and abilities are just at a world-class level,” Guy Gadowsky said. “He does a lot for the team and a lot for the team’s confidence, because he’s a game changer.”

Last summer, the Calgary, Alberta, native was viewed as one of the top prospects potentially making the jump to NCAA hockey, with an impressive resume that put plenty of scouts on watch. When rumors started circulating that Penn State was Smith’s likely landing spot, the hockey world took notice.

The defenseman announced his commitment to the school in early June, and there was little doubt that the blue and white had secured an elite talent. Less than two weeks later, that sentiment was made official at the Peacock Theater in Los Angeles, home of the 2025 NHL Draft.

Smith said those two weeks were “incredible,” as the freshman made two career-changing decisions and moments in just a short time.

Midway through the first round, the Columbus Blue Jackets were on the clock, and the crowd rose to their feet as Meredith Gaudreau, the wife of late hockey star and Blue Jackets forward Johnny Gaudreau, took the stage.

With the No. 14 overall selection, Gaudreau announced Smith as the Blue Jackets’ first pick, making him Penn State’s first-ever first-round selection.

“Especially being able to be called by (Gaudreau),” Smith said. “You know, watching Johnny play in Calgary, it meant a lot to me.”

Before he had the status of a first-round draft pick, the defenseman’s raw talent was evident through his performance in the Western Hockey League.

Smith spent over two years with the Tri-City Americans, dominating during the 2024-25 season, putting up 11 goals and 43 assists to lead all 2007-born defensemen in the WHL.

Previously, a long-standing rule barred any Canadian Hockey League skaters from competing in NCAA Division I hockey, but the rule was struck down in late 2024.

The change altered the landscape of college hockey, and suddenly, hundreds of athletes had an important choice on where to take their career next — Smith included.

“(I wanted to) play against older, stronger, faster opponents,” Smith said. “(The NCAA) is more like the NHL, and the hockey style is too, so I think (it was) just trying to get the most NHL-ready that I can.”

So when Penn State called, Smith answered.

“They were the first school to reach out, and so when I did a Zoom call with them, it was really good,” Smith said. “Unbelievable, they had everybody on there, so that was definitely a big seller for me, just how well they presented Penn State through a Zoom call.”

Next, Smith traveled to see Hockey Valley in person. From there, it was a no-brainer.

“When I came on the visit, I saw this place, and I was like ‘This is where I want to be,’” Smith said. “It was pretty easy, honestly, to choose here. I love it here, and I couldn’t be happier with my decision.”

Smith wasn’t alone in his path from the CHL to State College. In fact, Penn State’s entire 2025-26 freshman class made the jump from one of the three Canadian junior leagues affected by the rule change.

Aside from Smith, the most notable name from the freshman group is the presumptive 2026 No. 1 overall NHL Draft pick, Gavin McKenna. The two skaters have known each other for years, sharing in the rare experience of what it takes to become a program-altering talent.

“I grew up with Jackson; we played in tournaments together,” McKenna said. “He’s a great player. The way he sees the ice is pretty special, and he’s got a lot of confidence out there, which is a very big key in hockey. I think he’s got all the tools.”

It’s common for many first-year collegiate players to go through some adversity during their transition to NCAA hockey.

“There’s a lot less time and space here,” Smith said back in October. “Everybody’s a bit older, a bit stronger, so they’re on you quick, you’ve got to make a quick play, you’ve got to know where people are.”

Smith was different, however, landing a spot on the Nittany Lions’ top defensive pairing and never looking back.

“As far as development goes, I think physically, he didn’t miss a beat,” Gadowsky said. “I think this is a bit of a different game in terms of the intensity, etcetera … and I think he adjusted fairly quickly.”

The two-way defenseman immediately became an integral part of the blue and white system.

Slotting in alongside alternate captain Jarod Crespo, Smith paired his offensive vision and blistering shot with physicality and a lock-down defensive performance night in and night out.

“He’s obviously a great player, I think our synergy has gotten really good over the course of playing together the whole year,” Crespo said. “He’s a lot more offensive-minded than I am, obviously, so kind of using that to bounce off each other … has worked out well for both of us.”

The blueliner’s pure scoring ability was evident early in his collegiate career, registering seven points in his first six games with the Nittany Lions, including two multi-point efforts.

Smith continued to dominate in his inaugural season, becoming a staple of the blue and white’s first power play unit. It only made sense that another one of the defenseman’s record-setting moments came on the man-advantage.

In Penn State’s final regular-season series with Michigan, Smith netted his first multi-goal game of the season. However, that wasn’t the only storyline of the night.

The defender recorded his first goal of the night, and eighth of the season, halfway through the first period, tying Penn State’s single-season goal record for a defenseman. The second goal was an equalizer on the power play with under four minutes left, to put Smith in sole possession of the scoring record.

“I can’t thank my teammates enough, they’ve been the biggest part of my success,” Smith said after setting the new record. “So getting that was definitely nice…that was definitely cool to get to experience that.”

After the blue and white’s exit in the semifinals of the conference tournament, the rookie had tallied 26 points on the season, leading all Big Ten blueliners. Smith’s 11 goals tied for fourth in the nation for defenseman, with seven coming on the power play, pacing all blueliners nationwide.

The defenseman’s record-setting season was recognized conference-wide, after he was named to the All-Big Ten Second Team and the Big Ten All-Freshman Team alongside McKenna. The teammates became just the second and third program rookies to earn spots on the conference’s first or second teams.

“He is, physically, the most talented hockey player I’ve ever coached,” Gadowsky said. “He makes us a much better team in the offensive zone, in the neutral zone, in the defensive zone. We’re so fortunate to have him at Penn State.”

Smith’s draft night was his first mark on the Penn State record books. Next, he became the program’s highest-scoring defenseman. Then, he collected conference awards that only a few rookies have seen before.

The defenseman’s short career as a Nittany Lion has been one monumental moment after another.

Now, the blue and white’s second-straight NCAA Tournament campaign is looming, with the hopes of capturing its first-ever national championship.As his inaugural college season reaches its final stretch, Smith has one more shot to ingrain his name in Hockey Valley history.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: ‘This is growing so fast’ | Women’s club flag football thriving at Penn State campuses
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a spike in popularity across the United States, flag football was added to the NCAA’s Emerging Sports for Women program in January 2026, positioning it to potentially achieve full NCAA championship status in the future. But even before this recognition, the sport has been thriving on Penn State campuses.

At Commonwealth campuses such as Penn State Harrisburg and Schuylkill, flag football is a varsity sport and the teams compete at the Division III level. At University Park the sport is undergoing changes as the team, initially founded as a recreational program, was approved to become a club sport earlier this year.

Club president Lauren McGoey formed the team in November 2024 and its been growing steadily ever since.

“It's been super fun and rewarding,” McGoey, a third-year studying economics major, said. “It's been an amazing outlet for everybody involved.”

McGoey had limited experience with the sport before forming the team and so did the majority of its players. However, she said the lack of experience hasn’t been an issue.

“They're versatile, so their transition into the sport has been good,” McGoey said, “Everyone’s very supportive.”

Giada Viola, the club’s vice president, said one of the sport’s biggest draws is how easy it is to start.

“Basketball, for example, has been established for so long that most of the girls playing, especially at the college level, have been playing their whole life,” Viola, third-year studying architecture, said. “Whereas you see girls here at the college level that had started only a few years ago. This is the perfect sport to pick up now, learn from those who do have experience, and grow.”

This year, there are over 30 players on the roster and the club has grown significantly since its inception. Interest in the team is increasing heavily as it develops into a more established program.

“When we first held tryouts in the spring, we had over 100 girls show up,” Viola said. “It was insane to see how much it blew up. The sport has been growing exponentially.”

In addition to being an athletic outlet for women on campus, the club has a strong social aspect, which treasurer Megan Evans believes is important at such a large university.

“I just love being in a practice environment where you get to meet up with people every week,” Evans, a second-year studying architectural engineering, said. “Especially going to a big school it's hard to find a close group of people … it makes the campus feel smaller.”

Football is central to Penn State student culture, and the club allows for individuals to get involved outside of cheering in Beaver Stadium on gamedays.

“We're such a big football school,” Viola said. “We started with nothing, minimal experience, just being a small club last year, and already we've had a bunch of outgoing support, and I think that comes with just being Penn State and having that name. It really does help.”

Following his own football career, Ben Dupree, a former starting collegiate quarterback at The Citadel, a military institution in South Carolina, is now Harrisburg’s flag football team’s inaugural head coach.

“I've been fortunate that I got blessed with some talent,” Dupree said. “There was a lot of talent already in the school.”

Penn State Harrisburg is 3-0 on the season and has outscored its opponents 161-6. Dupree intends to keep that success rolling throughout the season, and into the playoffs, but also to cultivate an environment that will lead to a successful future for the program.

“For me, obviously the goal this year is to win the conference championship,” Dupree said, “But other than that, just to make sure all our returning players come back next year. I want to make sure everybody has a good experience and that they want to return.”

Dupree said he believes University Park along with other institutions will soon begin to advance flag football to a varsity sport.

“I think soon, all the schools are going to offer scholarships to play flag,” Dupree said. “I already see girls crossing over from other sports to play flag. It's gonna hurt some other sports, especially the ones in the spring, but I do think that eventually it's going to be one of the top female sports across the country.”

High school programs across the nation are also growing, with many receiving support from local NFL franchises. Teams like the Washington Commanders, New York Jets and Pittsburgh Steelers, in addition to others have all committed to developing the sport.

Alongside the expansion of high school and college programs, flag football will make its Olympic debut at the 2028 Summer Games in Los Angeles, signalling the growth of the sport not only in the United States but internationally.

Penn State’s club team is new, but the foundation is there for it to be a mainstay in the wider flag football scene for years to come.

“This is an up-and-coming sport,” McGoey said. “This is growing so fast, and even the community outside of just this club program, how we've been able to make NFL connections and get that type of attention is absolutely insane.”

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball suffers crushing loss to Bucknell
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
After its win against St. Bonaventure last night in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park, Penn State returned in search of another chance to improve its record against the Bucknell Bisons.

The Nittany Lions fell to the Bisons, with Bucknell dominating all game to finish 9-0 against the blue and white.

The Bisons came out swinging, as senior Michael Trommer started off the game with a two-run home run, putting the team at an early lead. Penn State remained off the board, getting three outs before Cohl Mercado and Michael Anderson could run home.

Penn State responded with a strong outfield performance to keep Bucknell at two, with all hitters grounding out before reaching first base. The Nittany Lions fell short on their offensive performance, leaving the second inning off the board.

Penn State continued to struggle through the third, getting slammed on both defense and offense. The Bisons quickly loaded up the bases with only one out in the top of the third, before pitcher Ethan Bauerschmidt was relieved by Mason Butash. Despite his fast throws, it wasn’t enough as Bucknell scored three more runs.

On the offense, Nittany Lion Spencer Barnett had a power hit that was sure to be a homerun, but fell just short, hitting the wall into right fielder Chris Morabito’s glove, signaling another inning with no runs.

“We’ve had some tough stretches here, where guys have been hurt, guys have been sick, and guys not feeling well,” coach Mike Gambino said. “The guys competed tonight for the standard that we want here; it was not good enough.”

Butash redeemed himself in the top of the fourth, striking out all Bucknell hitters, giving Penn State a chance at a much-needed comeback. It looked promising with a single to third by Jayden Davis and a walk from Maddox McDonald, leaving both on base with only one out on the board. Only to be met by a strikeout and a disappointing double out off Preston Yaucher’s groundball.

Looking for a pitching change in the fifth, sophomore Harrison Lollin went in to keep Bucknell at five runs. While the Bisons put another on the board, Lollin got the job done under pressure, pitching a strikeout with the bases loaded and only one out, and first baseman Barnett finishing it off, catching a pop-up ball to get Penn State out of a nightmare setup.

However, once again the blue and white failed to make a statement in the bottom of the fifth. Even with the bases loaded and only one out, Penn State couldn’t get its batting together with both Bryce Molinaro and Davis getting struck out by Bucknell pitcher Dante Pavone, leaving morale low in the bullpen.

“I thought our effort, our attention, and our compete and our fight were not who we want to be,” Gambino said. “Whether or not we won the game or lost the game is irrelevant. We can’t be okay with that.”

After a quiet sixth inning on both sides, Bucknell kept their dominance over Penn State as sophomore John Calabrese singled into center field, allowing Zach Selfon to score, moments before Billy Fluharty hit a single into left field that slipped past the blue and white, letting in two more runs, leaving the score 9-0 at the top of the seventh.

Going into the eighth, Bucknell was just short of a mercy-rule victory, with Penn State having the same problem they had all game. Powerful hits by players like Yaucher and McDonald that kept landing wrong and getting caught.

“I thought from all nine, I didn't think we had a ton of quality bats, it was soft, weak contact in the air,” Gambino said. “Just not who we want to be,”

Another pitching switch at the top of the ninth saw senior Dimond Loosli on the mound, marking the seventh pitcher to throw for Penn State over the course of this game.

Bucknell took home the win, putting up nine runs and 12 hits, compared to Penn State’s zero runs and five hits. The Nittany Lions finish this game with a 7-16 record.

“We are talking very much about staying present and staying positive. Whatever we do, win or lose, when we show up to the park the next day, we just move forward,” Gambino said. “Good or bad, it doesn’t matter one day at a time.”

Up Next

Penn State returns home to kick off its series against Illinois this Friday at 5:30 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball defeats rival No. 15 Ohio State in four sets
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State and Ohio State are familiar foes in just about every sport – and the two squads don’t like each other very much.

The Nittany Lions and the Buckeyes met at the Fitzgerald Field House in Pittsburgh for a neutral-site showdown for Penn State’s first match in 11 days. In the end, the blue and white came out on top, winning 3-1.

The Buckeyes’ Shane Wetzel opened up the scoring in the first set, quickly tallying two service aces to give his squad the early lead. Wetzel entered Wednesday’s match with 220 kills – the most of any Buckeye this season.

Both sides traded back-and-forth for the next several points until the Buckeyes went on a 4-0 scoring run that forced Mark Pavlik and the Nittany Lions to call a timeout.

Penn State got back on the board coming out of the timeout, picking up a couple of points off errors and a kill by Owen Rose that brought the score closer at 12-9.

Wetzel notched three more kills, all from Daniel Henwood Rodriguez, who is fifth in the NCAA in assists per set, to extend the Buckeyes’ lead to 17-13

The Nittany Lions started to build momentum, going on a 3-1 scoring run to tighten the gap at 19-17, but the Buckeyes wouldn’t back down and notched another kill to stay ahead of the curve.

Back-to-back blue and white scores brought the first set to within just one point and Ohio State was forced to take a timeout as it watched its lead shrink.

Penn State took its first lead of the night thanks to a Rose service ace that put the Nittany Lions up 21-20.

The two squads made it a battle at the end of the first set after reaching a 24-24 standstill, but the blue and white got the job done with the help of a Sean Harvey kill that captured the first set 27-25 for the Nittany Lions, who fought from behind in the early running.

In the exact same fashion, the Buckeyes took an early 2-0 lead to kick off the second set due to a Michael Schwob service error and another Wetzel kill.

Six-straight kills followed and made it a 5-3 Ohio State advantage, but a 3-1 blue and white scoring run tied the set at 6-6.

Penn State moved ahead thanks to three-straight Harvey kills, but the Buckeyes recaptured their lead, going back out in front 13-10, backed by two more Wetzel kills.

Henwood Rodriguez was all over the place as Ohio State made its advantage four points, but the blue and white were keeping its foot in the door.

Harvey and Penn State outside hitter Matthew Luoma kept the blue and white alive in the latter stages of the second set, but it was all for nothing as a Henwood Rodriguez service ace shut the door on the Nittany Lions as the Buckeyes stole the second set 25-20.

A Wetzel service error put the blue and white on the board first to start the third set, as it quickly went ahead two points.

Penn State capitalized on its early scoring, staying one step ahead and matching the Buckeyes’ scoring efforts with a point of its own until it went on a 3-0 scoring run to put some separation between the two as the blue and white went up 9-6.

The Buckeyes tied it up at 11-11 after scoring three-straight points, but another 3-0 scoring run put the Nittany Lions back in front at 14-11.

A 5-1 scoring run backed by three service errors put Ohio State back on top, seemingly out of nowhere, but Penn State quickly wrote back and recaptured its lead, going up 20-17 thanks to three quick Harvey kills, in turn forcing the Buckeyes to take another timeout.

Luoma sealed the deal for the blue and white with a service ace after a Bennett Wilson kill, leaving Penn State with a 25-20 third-set victory.

Pavlik’s unit went on an early 5-0 scoring run in the fourth set, with the points coming by way of a Harvey kill and four straight Buckeye errors, as the blue and white jumped out to a 6-2 lead in the early running.

Gaige Gabriel was having a quiet night until a service ace put his squad up four points.

The sophomore entered Wednesday’s match with the second-most service aces for Penn State, with 16, just one spot behind Rose.

A Luoma service error was followed by a Gabriel attack error, giving the Buckeyes two free points. Ohio State capitalized, and Stanislaw Chacinski recorded a kill that shrunk Penn State’s lead to 15-13, causing Pavlik to take a timeout.

Harvey moved up to 20 kills on the night as Wetzel picked up his 14th not long before another Chacinski kill caused the blue and white, holding a slight 22-20 advantage, to opt for another timeout.

The winningest active coach in NCAA men’s volleyball must have given his squad enough fire in their huddle, as it was enough for Penn State to take the fourth set 25-20 after three consecutive Buckeye errors to cap off the night with the 3-1 victory.

Up Next

Penn​​ State returns to Rec Hall for the first time in a month when it hosts NJIT for a two-match series this weekend. The first match is scheduled for 6 p.m. Friday, with the second match at 3 p.m. Saturday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: 2 Penn State women's lacrosse representatives drafted to PLL
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State coach Amy Moreau and former player Kristin O'Neill were drafted by the Boston Guard. The Penn State women's lacrosse team members will join one of the four teams in the Women's Lacrosse League.

Congrats to Amy and Kristin to being drafted to the Boston Guard‼️‼️‼️🎉🎉🎉🎉 pic.twitter.com/AvEnC9KMpk— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) March 25, 2026

Moreau is currently an assistant coach for the Nittany Lions. Previously, she played at the University of Massachusetts for five years before making the switch to coaching. She has also played professional lacrosse before with Athletes Unlimited.

O'Neill is an assistant coach with Towson, a position she's held since 2024. Before that, she played four years in Happy Valley, where she earned a pair of Big Ten First Team accolades.

The WLL recently finished its championship series, where the New York Charging was crowned after beating the California Palms 18-11. The next action for the WLL will be on May 16, when New York faces Boston.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘I Love’ Malcolm Todd | SPA hosts Malcom Todd in the BJC
author: Serina Chan | For The Collegian
description:
Penn State’s Student Programming Association (SPA) welcomed Malcolm Todd at the Bryce Jordan Center Tuesday night, featuring an opening act by Laundry Day.

Todd, a singer-songwriter and producer, is known for his R&B and indie pop fusion music. His first album entitled “Malcom Todd” was a hit on social media platforms, like TikTok, leading to his single, "Chest Pain (I Love),” making the Billboard Top 100.

Doors opened at 7 p.m., and fans flooded the BJC, stocking up on snacks and beverages.

On the floor, Catherine Waropay, said she appreciated that SPA organized the venue in a simple and easy way, allowing students to enter and find their designated areas without issues.

“It’s a great chance for everyone to get together and hang out while listening to music we all like,” Waropay, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “I’m personally most excited for the song ‘Sweet Boy.’”

Unlike Waropay, some people, like Adrianna Doyle, came to the concert without knowing much about Malcolm Todd, hoping to just have a good time and listen to new music. Since Todd recently rose in popularity on social media, he’s reached over 16.5 million monthly listeners on Spotify.

“I don’t know all of his songs but I was listening to him while I was getting ready today and I just became so excited for his concert,” Doyle, a second-year studying nursing, said.

With concert prices on the rise and out of budget for a lot of fans, Doyle said the discounted price of the student tickets pushed them to attend.

“I see so many events throughout the year and I love that the tickets were discounted and marketed towards what students want,” Doyle said. “SPA does a really good job of taking feedback and putting artists of what the students want to see.”

SPA also gave out free merchandise for the event, including a clear wrap-around bag, hand sanitizers and several accessories such as stickers, pens and a stuffed animal.

Once the concert began, Todd began with one of his most popular hits, “Earrings,” followed by other fan favorites such as “Chest Pain,” “4Me 4Me,” “Mr. Incorrect,” “Bleed” and “Cheer Me On.”

Todd performed his latest single, “Breathe,” for the first time live for audience members in the BJC.

Clara Cox said Todd was a great performer, and thought he engaged well with the audience during the concert.

“He really brought the energy to the crowd,” Cox, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “But I loved how he also made us slow down and really think about living in the moment.”

Although she was impressed by Todd’s performance, Kate Tzoumas was pleasantly surprised by the organization of the event by SPA. She felt safe and well cared for when the SPA members were handing out refreshments to audience members on the floor.

“The whole concert ran so smoothly,” Tzoumas, a second-year student studying biobehavioral health, said. “I loved the trinkets and I loved the free refreshments. If they do this every time, I’m definitely going to more concerts in the future.”

Tzoumas was also excited to see SPA have such a variation in the artists they invite to Penn State.

“I love having a different type of artist coming here besides country music,” Tzoumas said. “I really appreciate it because we don’t usually get a lot of alternative music here.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball guard Kiyomi McMiller to enter transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a record-breaking first year at Penn State, star guard Kiyomi McMiller will find her third team in as many seasons.

McMiller announced her entry to the transfer portal on Wednesday, according to Talia Goodman of On3, capping off a 627-point season in Happy Valley.

Penn State’s Kiyomi McMiller plans to enter the transfer portal, she told @On3. One of the nation’s leading scorers is on the move, following a coaching change for the Nittany Lions. https://t.co/8oEZDEwJpz— Talia Goodman (@TaliaGoodmanWBB) March 25, 2026

McMiller is the second Lady Lion to enter the portal, after Maggie Mendelson did on Tuesday. The two leave after the hiring of new head coach Tanisha Wright last Thursday.

The Silver Springs, Maryland, native spent her freshman year at Rutgers, starting in 19 games while averaging 18.7 points per game.

On March 27, 2025, she entered the transfer portal and ultimately committed to Penn State a month later. Her commitment to the Lady Lions sparked a comparison to the commitment of the Ann Meyers Drysdale Award winner in June 2023.

McMiller’s Penn State career started with three straight 20-point games, followed by her first of three double-doubles this season.

While starting in 26 of Penn State’s 29 games, McMiller tied Caitlin Clark’s Big Ten record for the most consecutive 30-point games, ending the year with 210 points in February. Against USC on Feb. 25, she recorded a career-high 40 points as she crossed the 1,000-point marker.

McMiller was named to the Big Ten Weekly Honor Roll in back-to-back weeks to end the season, ultimately being named to the All-Big Ten Media Second Team and All-Big Ten Coaches Third Team.

The 5-foot-8 guard finished her Penn State career with the 10th-most points in a single season in Penn State history, the fourth-most points in a sophomore year and the third-most 30-point games in a single season.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Takeaways from 2026 NCAA Wrestling Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
The 2026 NCAA Wrestling Championships featured some major upsets, while some of the top wrestlers at weight classes rolled through the bracket en route to a championship.

Penn State finished in first place, clinching the team title in Session 5 on Saturday morning. The Nittany Lions ended the weekend with 181.5 points, breaking their record from 2025. Oklahoma State finished in second with 131 points, Nebraska in third with 100.5 points and Iowa in fourth with 92.5 points to round out the trophy winners.

Here’s some takeaways from the weekend’s showing of wrestling on the biggest stage.

Penn State continues dominance

Penn State was the heavy favorite entering the championships, but it was unknown how commanding the victory would be for the Nittany Lions. As the first two days got underway, it was clear Penn State was going to win, and it was going to win big.

The Nittany Lions sent eight wrestlers to the quarterfinals, going a perfect 8-for-8, in sending the lineup to the semifinals. As each round continued, the number of points for the team continued to grow.

In addition to this, Penn State knew it had to score bonus points, which set it apart from the other teams. This year, the Nittany Lions scored 40.5 bonus points across the six sessions. A national champion who doesn’t score any bonus points earns 20 points for the team, meaning Penn State essentially had two additional national champions.

PJ Duke, Penn State’s 157-pound freshman who lost to eventual national champion Landon Robideau of Oklahoma State, scored more points for the team than Robideau. Duke had two falls and a tech fall, earning bonus points while Robideau won each match by decision, earning him the aforementioned 20 points.

The Nittany Lions dominated the field, with four champions — Luke Lilledahl, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines and Josh Barr. They had two wrestlers finish as the runner up with Shayne Van Ness and Rocco Welsh. Duke finished in third, Marcus Blaze finished in fourth and Braeden Davis and Cole Mirasola each went 2-2 in the tournament.

Oklahoma State’s surge

The Cowboys made their presence known in the NCAA Championships. They not only finished second in the team scoring, but also finished second in individual champions.

The big thing is that all three title winners were freshman with Jax Forrest at 133 pounds, Sergio Vega at 141 pounds and Landon Robideau at 157 pounds.

While Oklahoma State clearly has cemented itself as a top team in the country under former Penn State wrestler and assistant coach David Taylor, it is still a bit away from competing with the Nittany Lions.

With the young stars Taylor has, and the impressive incoming recruiting class which features the No. 4-6 2027 recruits per FloWrestling in Dreshaun Ross and Jordyn and Jayden Raney, it has the national outlook in anticipation for a potential overtaking of the Nittany Lions.

Ross has garnered high expectations, but last summer lost to Mirasola in the U20 World Team Trials. While that’s freestyle, and collegiate wrestling is folkstyle, it’s still likely that Ross has more ground to make up in the heavyweight class than what’s perceived.

The biggest key for the Cowboys is more bonus points. They notched 20.5 bonus points, an entire national championship run’s worth behind Penn State in that category.

While it’s trending toward solidifying itself as the No. 2 program, it’s going to be a bit until Oklahoma State can catch the Nittany Lions.

Ohio State underperforms

The team that entered as the favorites to finish second, Ohio State, severely underperformed. Multiple highly seeded wrestlers failed to place, causing a severe drop in points. The Buckeyes finished in fifth place with 84.5 points after getting some hype to potentially give Penn State a run for its money.

At 125 pounds, Nic Bouzakis was the No. 3 seed. Not only did he not wrestle to his seeding, he didn’t finish inside the top eight. The big questions for him were whether he’d be at top form with having to cut to 125 pounds each day to make weight. On the second day, he lost by major decision to No. 14 seed Jacob Moran of Indiana.

Ethan Stiles at 149 pounds also didn’t place despite being the No. 7 seed. In fact, he went 1-2 in the championships, failing to earn many points at all.

Nick Feldman at heavyweight was the No. 5 seed and didn’t place inside the top eight. He went 2-2, including a loss to No. 21-seed Juan Mora of Oklahoma.

There’s likely to be some significant changes in weight classes and starters for the next season as Ohio State attempts to have its wrestlers more prepared to thrive in March.

Stanford shines, places 6th

A couple years ago, it was unknown whether Stanford would even have a wrestling program. Now, the Cardinal are coming off a sixth-place finish at the 2026 NCAA Championships with 67.5 points.

With eight wrestlers qualified, half of them earned All-American status, including national champion Aden Valencia at 149 pounds.

Nico Provo and Tyler Knox at 125 pounds and 133 pounds, respectively, each placed, as the lightweights shined for Stanford. The other wrestler who placed was Angelo Posada at 197 pounds, but guys like Daniel Cardenas at 157 pounds earned additional points for a bonus-point victory.

The intriguing aspect of Stanford is the fact it doesn’t have a single senior on roster. It’s an extremely young team that performed exceptionally well at the national championships, giving it the opportunity to build on that for years to come.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Column | Penn State softball utilizing full pitching rotation will be key to success
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State kicked off the 2026 season on a fast start, going 19-6 in early nonconference play. However, the Nittany Lions have struggled as of late, with a 1-5 record in their first two Big Ten series against Oregon and Wisconsin.

Last season, the blue and white had a formidable offense, but couldn’t find that same consistency from their arms. At the start of this year, Penn State put the pieces together that it didn’t have in 2025, with complete production in both pitching and hitting.

However, since the start of conference play, the Nittany Lions’ numbers haven’t looked the same, especially from the circle.

In 25 games played and 163 innings pitched against nonconference opponents this season, Penn State posted a 2.40 ERA, ranking second among all Big Ten teams.

Through the Nittany Lions' six games and 37.2 innings pitched against two conference foes, they’ve totaled a 7.06 ERA, which is 13th in the Big Ten. This is a far higher number in even further less innings pitched.

For a bigger perspective, before its series against Oregon started on March 13, the blue and white owned a 2.40 ERA, which ranked third in the Big Ten and 19th nationally.

Penn State’s ERA now stands at 3.28, which is still highly impressive and enough for fifth-best in the conference. Still, if the Nittany Lions want to go on a strong run through Big Ten play and finish the season how they started, then this number will likely have to improve.

The blue and white have proved that they have a pitching staff that can take the team to new heights, but with the bulk of non-conference play over, adjustments have to come against familiar opponents.

One of the main reasons Penn State was successful in the young part of the season was because it relied on a full five-armed rotation to complete games. This depth hasn’t been as evident since the start of Big Ten play, but it might be just what the blue and white need.
