The use of Penn State’s pitching rotation differed between its two conference series. In each of the first two games against Oregon, the Nittany Lions only went one deep into the bullpen, with McKenna Young coming in relief for Bridget Nemeth in Game 1 and Brevyn Kellepouris in Game 2.

However, in the series finale against the Ducks, four blue and white arms entered the circle as Abigail Britton and Mackenzie Duncan made their first appearances in Eugene.

The reason the difference in rotation between the first two games and the last is important is that in Game 3, Penn State took Oregon to the seventh inning, eventually losing on a walk-off. In the first two games, the Nittany Lions lost by a combined score of 18-3.

Flipping to the Wisconsin series, the blue and white used four arms in the first two games and three in the last, and this three-game set was much more competitive than the previous one against Oregon.

Penn State lost by a combined four runs in its two losses, while winning by six in its victory. Despite the series defeat, the improvement was definitely evident.

Every arm has its strength and the pitching staff has shown that it can build off one another and pick each other up. As the stakes get higher in Big Ten play, using all the tools in the toolbox will only make the attack from the circle better and this has been proven in the early parts of the season.

While it's understandable not to want opponents to see the same arms multiple times throughout the weekend, using all their power at once has brought the Nittany Lions success, so it's worth sticking to.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘Our spaces are for you’ | Campus groups aim to open conversations about reproductive health
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Reproductive health services and education have been at the forefront of national discourse, following the overturning of Roe v. Wade in 2022. As conversations ramp up about what constitutes sexual health care and whether it belongs in school curricula, campus organizations are aiming to provide resources and education at Penn State.

The Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity (CSGD), Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education (R-VOICE) and Days for Girls all provide reproductive health educational resources and supplies, such as free menstrual products.

Hailey Dermota, the vice president of Penn State’s Days for Girls chapter, said menstrual and reproductive health are “dramatically intertwined.” Days for Girls is a national organization that “works to eliminate the stigma and limitations associated with menstruation,” according to its website.

Penn State’s chapter is behind a number of period product initiatives on campus, including the inclusion of free period product dispensers in every newly renovated building on campus, which launched in 2023.

Currently, the organization is working on implementing the initiative in residence halls. A pilot program was launched in February to include these products in three West Halls dormitories — Watts, Thompson and Hamilton.

“Period poverty is here on campus as well,” Dermota said. “We've done a couple surveys, and we've seen some shocking numbers of how poverty affects individuals trying to get menstrual products.”

The club also helps campus offices get menstrual products to give out to students for free, including the Pride Pantry located at LL011 in the HUB-Robeson Center, CSGD and R-VOICE.

“Period products are too expensive in the market, so if that's the only place you can get it with a meal plan, it's too much,” Dermota said. “You don't always want to talk about how you're struggling financially, so it just gives a way for those students to access that with dignity, without having that shame and stigma that's always surrounded it.”

Dermota also emphasized how talking about reproductive health issues openly with peers, family and friends is critical as it helps people feel more confident in their health.

“You deserve to be helped and eased of your suffering and pain and whatever's going on that you know is not normal,” Dermota said. “Talk about menstruation openly. Talk about reproductive issues openly, because a lot of the time people are sharing similar experiences.”

Kate Rawson, assistant director of the CSGD, said there are a lot of misconceptions around who can receive assistance for reproductive healthcare.

“A lot of students within the queer and trans community do struggle with financial insecurity, food insecurity and housing insecurity, so as much as we can provide access to these things for free is wonderful,” Rawson said.

CSGD, which is in Room LL011 of the HUB-Robeson Center, and open Monday through Friday from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., hosts workshops centering queer-inclusive sexual education, consent education and helps students forge and find inclusive environments.

“Our spaces are for you,” Rawson said. “I think a lot of people think that you have to be a fully out queer-trans person to visit our center, or you have to look a certain way, or be a certain way. But our space is for everyone — allies as well. We are open to anyone, regardless of any identity, visiting our space.”

Similar to CSGD, the R-VOICE Center also gives out free products such as contraceptives and provides care for anyone who may have been affected by sexual violence.

“Our focus is all on doing outreach, education, prevention advocacy and then being able to support survivors,” Yvette Wilson, the director of R-VOICE, said. “The focus really is on meeting them where they’re at and helping them as they try to move forward, whatever that looks like.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball energized by Dollar Dog Night crowd in win over St. Bonaventure
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The grills around Medlar Field at Lubrano Park were primed and ready, awaiting hungry Penn State fans. It was the first Dollar Dog Night of the season for the Nittany Lions with lines pouring into the ballpark, after the main gates opened, all with one goal in mind: getting a hot dog.

A crowd, full of 6,350 total hot dogs, accomplished its goal and then some. Backed by the support of 2,443 fans, the blue and white ended its first Dollar Dog Night with a 8-6 win over St. Bonaventure.

“I saw a lot of hot dogs going down (in the student section) tonight,” coach Mike Gambino said. “It’s Section 814, but it’s also the other 2,500 people in the park tonight. It’s not a warm night right now. I’ve said it over and over as we are trying to build what we think can turn into a championship-caliber baseball program.”

With the temperature at 40 degrees and a wind gust of 11 mph at first pitch, jackets and hoodies were scattered throughout the stands, even the Nittany Lion was dressed up in his Dollar Dog Night best.

Roll calls, from the newly formed student section, towards the Penn State players in the field filled the air around the ballpark as the first pitch crossed the plate.

Penn State used the first two innings to get runs across the plate. Bryce Molinaro singled in Jack Porter in the first inning, followed by a Preston Yaucher RBI single in the second. Two early runs in the first couple of innings only made a restless crowd more rowdy.

“It was great, and you see it in their dugout too, that when the crowd starts going, we score and the crowd starts going,” Gambino said. “You can just feel it, and then at the end, even though it got cold and not all the crowd stayed, it was loud when we were scoring at the end.”

Praises and bows surrounded the Nittany Lions as they stepped into the batter's box. Additionally, boo’s and heckles were thrown towards the Bonnies as they stepped up to the plate.

The Bonnies eventually got some runs, in the form of a five-run fourth inning. A two-run homer followed by a few RBIs gave St. Bonaventure the lead. Starting pitcher Frankie Sanchez was able to get through the first three innings unscathed, earning three strikeouts in the process.

Mason Horwat, Matthew VanOstenbridge and Robert Brown III were the arms out of the bullpen. The three relievers totaled eight strikeouts, not allowing an earned run.

“Horwat gives up the one (hit), but beats the barrel kind of an ‘excuse me’ single,” Gambino said. “Other than that, the guys were great tonight and then Robert Brown was really, really good. He was poised, had the breaking ball going, carrying the fastball tonight, he’s continuing to get better.”

The Penn State faithful had a reason to get riled up once again after catcher Avery Smith hit a 344-foot home run, cutting the deficit to one.

Following additional runs from St. Bonaventure, the crowd erupted after Molinaro hit a go-ahead two-run homer to give Penn State the lead 7-6. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native’s home run went 426 feet over the center field wall.

“Just trusting my plan, trusting my process,” Molinaro said. “Stuff hasn’t been going my way recently, but just not giving in and trusting what I do and what I do best, I think, takes me a long way.”

Those who braved the cold rose to their feet as the first Dollar Dog Night of the season came down to a final out. Maddox McDonald sent the fans home full and happy as he recorded the last out.

The Nittany Lions have little time to rest for their next game as they return to Medlar Field at Lubrano Park at 5:30 p.m. Wednesday against Bucknell.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: SJP holds protest to ‘continue fighting until the full liberation’ of Palestine
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Chants filled the air outside Old Main as Penn State Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) organized a protest to call for the liberation of Palestine and Penn State divestment from Israel on Tuesday. The demonstration, titled “The Struggle Continues Until Liberation,” was part of SJP’s Week of Action, which is ongoing from Monday to Friday.

Members of SJP, the Muslim Student Association, the United Socialists at Penn State and Latino Caucus all gave speeches at the event. Alongside advocating for Palestinians, the groups protested in opposition of both the war in Iran and recent Israeli invasion of Lebanon.

“I think it's really important for things like the Week of Action to be able to encourage students to come together to meet each other and, of course, to get organized so we can strengthen our fight on campus and to achieve Palestinian liberation,” Sarah Dweik, an SJP executive board member, said.

Dweik, a postdoctoral teaching fellow in communication arts and sciences, said the week is a way to recommit to working toward the global liberation of Palestine.

The protest, which began at 6 p.m., saw demonstrators holding signs reading: “Hands off the Middle East,” “No Justice, No Peace” and “PSU funds genocide.”

Tahir Haideri, SJP’s speaker at the event, said protests across the country and on a global level have caused universities, companies and governments to begin divesting from Israel. He said divestment is possible at Penn State as well.

“There are people listening,” Haideri, a graduate student studying biomedical engineering, said. “It is getting bigger as time passes on and we're growing stronger, and it is our duty to continue fighting until the full liberation and return of the people of Palestine.”

The protest moved from Old Main to the Allen Street Gates, where attendees remained until dispersing around 7:15 p.m. Several cars honked their horns in solidarity throughout the protest.

Sergio Peña, an SJP board member, said it is important to continue bringing attention to Palestine, especially in light of Israel’s violations of an October 2025 ceasefire, which has resulted in the deaths of hundreds of Palestinians.

“We see how the genocide has continued to accelerate by Israel again denying humanitarian aid from entering,” Peña, a third-year PhD student studying workforce education and development, said. “When we say the struggle continues until liberation, we mean that until Palestine is free. Until the occupation has ended. Until the United States and Israel cease their genocidal violence against the Palestinian people.”

Peña said it is important that the Penn State community continues engaging with SJP and similar organizations. Becoming aware of individual power helps students better understand their collective power, according to Peña.

“The events that SJP puts on are not possible without all the amazing members we have — community members, students who see what our causes are and know that it is right and just,” Peña said. “That should hopefully give people more incentive and hope to engage in these kinds of struggles.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State baseball defeats St. Bonaventure in first Dollar Dog Night of the season
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions were back home in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park for the first Dollar Dog night of the 2026 season, where they took on the St. Bonaventure Bonnies.

Penn State brought home the win 8-6 in the top of the ninth, with strong performances across the board.

Starting pitcher Frankie Sanchez set Penn State up well in the first inning with zero runs on the board for the Bonnies, while Jack Porter scored Penn State's first run off Bryce Molinaro’s powerful single into right field.

St. Bonaventure couldn’t get the momentum going into the second inning. Nittany Lion Jayden Davis launched a ball into left center field, getting him to second base before running home on Preston Yaucher’s single to right field.

While Penn State didn’t score any runs in the third, they kept their dominance over St. Bonaventure with a strong pitching performance by Sanchez, striking out Joey Calabretti and Gavin Constantine, leaving Alex Hebenstreit stranded on first base before getting out on a run to second.

The Bonnies came in hot during the fourth inning, taking the lead 5-2 over the blue and white off a homerun by Antonio Cordeiro, letting in himself and Anthony Fernandez. Penn State’s Mason Horwat came in to relieve Sanchez, but the Bonnies remained strong, hitting in three more runs.

Avery Smith brought it back in the bottom of the fourth for Penn State, hammering the ball into left field for a home run double RBI, putting the score at 5-4.

The fifth remained quiet for both teams, as the Nittany Lions got a chance to take the lead to close out the sixth with the bases loaded. They fell just short as Saint Bonaventure’s shortstop Constantine threw a double out, leaving the blue and white just one run behind the Bonnies.

Second baseman Chris Hoalcraft had a solid seventh inning for St. Bonaventure, stealing second base and running home off of Fernandez’s hit into right field, leading the Nittany Lions to switch up their pitchers and throw the final out for the Bonnies this inning.

Determined to make a comeback, Penn State came in hot, taking the lead 7-6 in the seventh after Cole Mercado singled to second base off a throwing error, before running home off a power hit by Michael Anderson into center field. Molinaro closed it out, scoring the blue and white’s second home run of the game with a double RBI.

Penn State’s Maddox McDonald had a strong performance in the bottom of the eighth, stealing second base before scoring off Mercado’s fast hit into right field.

The Nittany Lions finished on top in their back-and-forth game with St. Bonaventure, with fourteen hits and two home runs, improving their record to 7-15.

Up Next

Penn State faces Bucknell at home at 5:30 p.m. Wednesday at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Guy Gadowsky speaks on McKenna's Hobey Baker nomination, postseason prepapation
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State coach Guy Gadowsky was greeted with a wooden and plastic hockey stick at his press conference on Tuesday, as the first round of the NCAA Tournament awaits on Friday.

The wooden stick read “McKenna Magic.” The plastic one lists all the accomplishments and statistics of forward Gavin McKenna’s season. As the Hobey Baker top-three nominations are in full swing and fan voting is active, the Nittany Lions want as much coverage of McKenna as possible.

However, it is playoff time for Penn State, and there are much bigger things at stake. The Nittany Lions are set to play Minnesota-Duluth in the Albany Region Quarterfinal game of the NCAA Tournament at 9 p.m. on Friday.

Here is what Gadowsky said leading up to the most-anticipated postseason run in Penn State hockey history.

Gadowsky makes his case for McKenna for Hobey

McKenna has been on a preposterous run since his return from World Juniors in January. The freshman has dropped 33 points in 18 games since Jan. 9, a 1.83 points-per-game pace, which includes a program-best eight points against Ohio State in late February.

McKenna is second in the country in points per game at 1.50 — trailing only Quinnipiac’s Ethan Wyttenbach — and assists with 36, behind just Michigan’s Michael Hage.

“To play with 10 different line mates, which, because of necessity, of all the injuries that Fink has had, Dane (Dowiak) is out for the year. Chuck missed a long time… He's played with so many different guys, and to put up the numbers that he has, if you compare it to, for instance, last year's Hobey winner… blows that away,” Gadowsky said. “... So to do what he did in the toughest year in college hockey ever is absolutely incredible.”

Penn State’s schedule since loss to Michigan, preparations for Friday

The Nittany Lions have had a 10-day break and counting from game action since their loss to Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament semifinals on March 14. However, they did not treat the time off as if it were a bye week. Gadowsky confirmed Penn State is back on its “typical” schedule this week as it prepares to face second-seeded Minnesota-Duluth.

“We did not have a full week off,” Gadowsky said. “We did give extra time for healing because of where we're at as a team, health-wise. So obviously, (we) would have loved to have a shot at another Big Ten title, but if it didn't happen, the extra rest was very welcome. So we certainly didn't take an entire week off, but we certainly used it to get healthy and rested.”

Further, preparation-wise, Gadowsky and the team have started scouting Minnesota-Duluth. Typically, the team begins planning for opponents on Tuesday of a game week. That is not the case with the NCAA Tournament starting Friday.

“George Lewis is absolutely incredible,” Gadowsky said. “His title is Director of Operations. He handles analytics and video as well, and he has a team of very, very smart, intelligent, motivated student managers that love this program and are really, really smart. They've been working on different teams for well over a week. So Minnesota-Duluth, as well as four other teams — they've gone over them rather extensively.”

Gadowsky reveals the team identity

It is no secret that Penn State is an offense-oriented team. Gadowsky confirmed that to be the team’s identity on Tuesday, saying the Nittany Lions are a “high-octane rush offense.”

He said the team embraces it. However, one disadvantage to that style of play is a higher likelihood of turning the puck over. Gadowsky said the team knows the risks and is “comfortable” with it.

“We’ll make more great plays than turnovers,” Gadowsky said.

However, the turnovers come with the style of the offense that Gadowsky allows his players to play with. It's one that's more open, allowing their creativity to shine through.

“I don't think that's much of a surprise, but this is a team that loves to play offense, which we like here at Penn State,” Gadowsky said. “You can't do everything, but if you're going to be really, really good at one thing, I like the fact that that's it. Our stripes are that, we're very creative. We allow our team to be very creative. We believe in the creativity of our players to get it done… I think it's very fun hockey, I think it's very creative hockey. It's very fast. And we're comfortable with those… We're comfortable with that identity.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball’s Maggie Mendelson will enter transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Maggie Mendelson is one and done after basketball at Penn State.

Mendelson will enter the transfer portal after spending just one year with the Lady Lions, according to Talia Goodman of On3.

Penn State’s Maggie Mendelson has entered the transfer portal, sources told @On3. The 6-5 senior averaged 2 ppg this season. TRACKER: https://t.co/wYv1Ze6704 pic.twitter.com/kwbYrJJWJp— Talia Goodman (@TaliaGoodmanWBB) March 24, 2026

Following the hire of new coach Tanisha Wright on Thursday, Mendelson became the second athlete to look for a new home after Class of 2026 commit Chikae Desdunes reopened her recruitment earlier on Tuesday.

The North Ogden, Utah, native joined the Lady Lions in June after spending the previous year with the Nittany Lions’ volleyball team. She finished the 2025 season with the Nittany Lions before hitting the hardwood on Dec. 20 with coach Carolyn Kieger.

Mendelson started five games for Penn State, averaging 2.0 points and 3.3 rebounds per game.

Before joining the Lady Lions, Mendelson became a national champion with the Penn State women’s volleyball team in 2024, following two years with Nebraska.

In her first year with the Cornhuskers, Mendelson played both volleyball and basketball, but switched to just volleyball in Years 2 and 3 of her career.

Mendelson is out of volleyball eligibility and was recently signed by the Atlanta Vibe, but will spend the next year playing basketball with another school.

title: Penn State wrestling’s Mitchell Mesenbrink focused on bigger picture following NCAA Championship win
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
Saturday night, Mitchell Mesenbrink defeated long-time rival Mikey Caliendo of Iowa to win his second NCAA Championship. While for most people a national championship would be cause for celebration, for Mesenbrink, the victory opened his eyes to the place the sport of wrestling holds in his life.

The redshirt junior hesitated with his feelings after winning the title.

“Sometimes it takes attaining the goal to realize that the goal was not the thing to be attained,” Messenbrink said. “No matter what your goal is, it’s never as fulfilling as you think it is, and it definitely can't be the thing that you fill your soul with.”

Mesenbrink is dominant in a 165-pound weight class that looks like he’s lapped the field against. While the success itself hasn’t filled the cup of Mesenbrink, he retains motivation by seeing constant improvement in his game.

He told a story about lessons he learned from general manager Clay Steadman and his family. Steadman’s father was an artist and told Mesenbrink how tasks like homework are activities with a sent endpoint. This contrasts to how art and wrestling are evolving processes that can grow continuously.

“When you go into practice and want to enjoy it, you’re not focused on ‘when am I getting out of here,'" Mesenbrink said.

While Mesenbrink’s wrestling is still growing, he doesn’t want it to be all he focuses on. For the entire season, Mesenbrink has made it a point to describe wrestling as only a part of his life, rather than his defining trait.

He has delved into music, chess and social media expression through the course of the season as a way to show off a different side of his personality. Mesenbrink has been met with occasional pushback as he takes on these new journeys.

“I don’t really check comments, but the one time I checked the comments, they were like ‘shut up and stick to wrestling,”’ Mesenbrink said. “I’m way more than a wrestler, there’s so many other things in my life that can be important.”

The sentiment shared mirrors how the two-time national champion has spoken for the last two years. Mesenbrink’s faith and view of life shapes his focus past awards, wins or tangible items. He believes the world falsely praises these accolades as a fill in for a greater purpose in life.

“I think we’re meant to serve something greater than ourselves,” Mesenbrink said. “We get there and think ‘this is going to feel good for a day.’ But by Monday they’re going to be talking about who’s going to win it next year.”

A large portion of the program credits their success to their religion and Mesenbrink did the same following this victory. His state of mind that leads him to think differently than others, remain himself and be a dominant competitor, he credits to the freedom his faith provides.

"When you’re constantly like ‘I gotta do it this way, it’s gotta be my way or the highway,’ you are actually making yourself a prisoner in your own mind,” Mesenbrink said. “When you surrender to God and you understand it’s not your will, you actually gain all the control.”

Despite his success and creative ventures, Mesenbrink admitted to going through some personal struggles throughout the 2025-26 wrestling season. He again credits his faith and family for pulling him up through those times.

“This year hasn’t been easy at all and I think that’s when you need to pull those people that are closest to you,” Mesenbrink said. “I really love and appreciate all those people that I know God put into my life and maybe pulled out of my life too.”

Mesenbrink talked about his experience in Cleveland off the mat more importantly than the actual matches he competed in. During his time in Cleveland, the Hartland, Wisconsin native played his guitar and spent time with his family, practicing his faith to enjoy the vacation the sport of wrestling gave him.

Mitchell Mesenbrink is once again a national champion, but he is also an artist, a person and a family man. While the mainstream will look back on his success in Rocket Arena, his fondest memories will be more personal.

“When I look back on these times over this past year, I know it won’t be about bonus points or winning. It’ll be about sitting in my hotel room with my dad, my mom and my siblings,” Mesenbrink said. “If nothing went my way this weekend, those people were with me and are going to be there today as well.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: ‘Violence is what justifies its own existence’ | Students for Justice in Palestine holds 'The Genocide is not over' display
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) held a display on the HUB lawn Monday to show solidarity for Palestinians.

This display is the first event in the SJP’s Week of Action, which is being held throughout the week to feature more informative displays and talks from speakers regarding the conflict.

“We want to convey to campus that, despite the so-called ceasefire that was enacted in October of 2025, the conditions of Gaza have continued to deteriorate significantly, especially since the US and Israel's war of aggression on Iran,” Sergio Peña, an SJP board member and graduate teaching assistant, said. “We’ve seen the conditions in Gaza worsen, especially because of the ways that Israel has continued its blockade of humanitarian aid.”

5 different “scenes” were set up around the lawn showcasing different views of current life in Palestine. Life in tents, by graves, by active warzones and by active military equipment were all displayed throughout the lawn. Despite the cold and rain, club members stood by the exhibit, ready to answer questions and inform the public.

“Every moment is worth it,” Ronnie Reiley, an SJP board member, said. “Regardless of what conditions we find ourselves in here in America, what the Palestinians are experiencing is these same conditions but worst with the active threat of genocide. So, it may be raining and it may be cold but I’m not living in a tent. I’m not living where there’s flooding, where there’s rats. I’m going to finish this day, and I’m gonna go home to my bed. And I think everyone should be able to do that.”

Reiley, a third year graduate student in the social vision and interpersonal perception lab, joined approximately 20 other students who were rotating shifts at the exhibit, including Peña and Jared Carvajal, an event volunteer. They shared drinks and snacks while withstanding the wind and rain.

“As part of SJP, it’s our job to inform the masses and tell them what’s going on, that the situation didn’t start October 7 — it started over 70 years ago,” said Carvajal, a fourth-year studying philosophy. “This is a problem. This is an issue that is long standing, and that we should never forget.”

While Reiley and Peña were at the demonstration out of outrage for the Israel-Palestine conflict, Carvajal was there for personal reasons.

“When I was growing up, I had a neighbor we called Sid,” Carvajal said. “In 1967, during the 7 day war, Israel displaced him and he was forced to leave. He lived in the US for the rest of his life, and he was buried here, but he hasn’t been able to go back. His body is still in a cemetery in the United States. And to me, that injustice, that inability to go back, really motivates me as a moral person who believes that you should try to operate in a way that does good in the world.”

Péna said improvement for Palestine means improvement for the whole world.

“We’ve seen how Israel oppresses Palestinians and forces apartheid and has committed genocide against Palestinians, which in many ways also reflects the sort of conditions we see politically in the US as well,” Peña said. “We, as a community, have the power to collectively stand up to the systems that create the conditions of violence, war, genocide, that these things are not bigger than us. They are a part of us, and we have every right to stand up to them when we think something isn’t right.”

The Week of Action will continue tomorrow with a protest on Old Main lawn, and further into the week with tabling and talks.

“Violence is what justifies its own existence," Carvajal said.

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball guard Melih Tunca will enter transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
One of Penn State’s international players is on his way out of the program.

Guard Melih Tunca will enter the transfer portal, according to Jonathan Givony of DraftExpress.

Penn State guard Melih Tunca will enter the transfer portal, his agent Tolga Beritan told DraftExpress. The 6'5 Turkish freshman averaged 7.8 points and 2.3 assists in 23 minutes per game. pic.twitter.com/RcdLgGY4w3— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) March 24, 2026

Toward the beginning of the season, Tunca was a starter for the Nittany Lions. Throughout the nonconference slate, he scored in double figures nine times, including a season-high 20 points against La Salle on Nov. 15.

However, struggles on the defensive end caused him to see fewer minutes in the Big Ten schedule. Tunca scored in double figures four times in conference matchups as he turned into a rotational player instead of a starter.

The Turkey native made 31 appearances and 12 starts this season, averaging 7.8 points, 2.0 rebounds and 2.4 assists per game with 44.9% shooting from the field.

Tunca played the 2024-25 season with Turk Telekom of the Turkish Basketball Super League, which is the best professional league in Turkey. He earned Young Player of the Regular Season honors, averaging 6.7 points, 2.2 assists and 1.1 rebounds per game.

title: Staff predictions | 4 bold predictions for Penn State entering spring ball
author: The Daily Collegian football staff
description:
Surprise, surprise, spring ball is here.

Matt Campbell began his first spring session in his new home on Tuesday.

Here’s some unexpected storylines that could emerge.

Avery Hill: Unfamiliar faces win key roles on defense

New coaches mean new everything. Matt Campbell has already started that process by bringing in an entirely new culture to Penn State. I think these changes especially ring true on the defensive side of the ball.

The expectation among many is the Iowa State starters immediately slot in the same role in Happy Valley, but I think with plenty of variety on the coaching staff defensively, both depth returning Penn Staters and non-Cyclone transfers get a clean slate to show what they’re capable of, and a few will emerge.

Namely, I expect safety Vaboue Toure, defensive end LaVar Arrington ll and defensive tackle Armstrong Nnodim to take increased roles, potentially challenging starters near the end of the spring session.

Toure has played a limited role in his two seasons as a Nittany Lion, totaling 19 tackles in 16 games, but totaled seven tackles and a sack against Clemson in the Pinstripe Bowl. The expected starters are currently Marcus Neal Jr. and Jeremiah Cooper, but the former 4-star recruit, per 247Sports Composite, could earn the third safety role for big nickel and other safety-heavy packages over Iowa State transfer Jamison Patton and others.

Nnodim, an Oklahoma State transfer, is already expected to be one of the first defensive tackles off the bench, but I think he could become a starter at some point in 2026.

In front of him is likely Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams, both of whom defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe coached at UCLA, along with Utah’s Dallas Vakalahi. Nnodim, a redshirt sophomore with a sort of buff build at 319 pounds, should prove to be more versatile than the trio, naturally moving him up in the depth chart.

LaVar Arrington ll, who will play more defensive end in 2026 after joining Penn State as a 4-star linebacker last season, could see serious strides. I don't think that will be all, though, as I think players like linebacker Cam Smith could take a big step as well.

Lexie Linderman: Brock Riker takes a strong lead in the center battle

Penn State only has one starting offensive lineman returning from last season, leaving plenty of positions up for grabs on the front five.

One of those competitions is at center, where redshirt senior Dominic Rulli and redshirt sophomore Brock Riker are set to face off throughout the spring and summer ahead of Week 1.

Although Riker is younger and a bit smaller than Rulli, spring ball is the perfect opportunity for Riker to prove he can be the Nittany Lions’ starting center, and I predict he does just that.

The coaching staff has had plenty of praise for Riker, who transferred from Texas State this offseason. He was a freshman All-American per The Athletic last season after appearing in 12 games, showcasing the potential he has.

“I thought he was the best center in the world personally,” offensive line coach Ryan Clanton said Feb. 4. “That guy's raw talent and ability is more than enough, he was extremely, obviously, highly recruited, and everybody that was recruiting him, you knew why they were recruiting him too.”

If Riker reaches his potential, there’s no doubt he could win the starting center job over Rulli, who’s been waiting his turn to be the starting center for five years. But I don’t expect this position battle to be shored up any time soon, as both Riker and Rulli have a real chance at securing the spot.

Lyle Alenstein: Wide receivers see increased work

At Iowa State, offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser ran a lot of 13 personnel, which meant three tight ends were often on the field at once. When he departed the Cyclones to follow Matt Campbell to Penn State, a trio of tight ends in Benjamin Brahmer, Gabe Burkle and Cooper Alexander joined him in State College.

If Mouser were to follow the same offensive philosophy for the Nittany Lions, the projected starters in two-tight end sets would be Andrew Rappleyea and Brahmer, with Burkle likely being the third.

But Rappleyea and Burkle are out for spring practice with injuries, which will increase reps for Alexander, Brian Kortovich and Finn Furmanek, the only other tight ends on the roster. Despite other tight ends seeing more of a workload, there’s a possibility Mouser strays away from what he’s comfortable calling plays for and increases heavy wide receiver sets.

The projected wide receivers entering spring ball are Iowa State transfers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen, as well as Koby Howard. The injuries to the likely top pass catchers in the tight end room should give them more targets in spring ball.

On top of the aforementioned trio, it results in guys competing for their spot on the depth chart, and maybe even a starting opportunity. Names to keep an eye on are Grambling State transfer Keith Jones Jr., Iowa State transfer Zay Robinson and Peter Gonzalez.

Will Horstman: Quinton Martin Jr. separates himself as No. 1 running back

With Kaytron Allen and Nick Singleton off to the NFL, the No. 1 running back spot in 2026 is up for grabs heading into spring ball. One of the candidates to earn that job is redshirt sophomore Quinton Martin Jr., who could have a chance to separate himself from the pack throughout spring practices.

Martin rarely saw any action during his first two seasons as a depth piece behind Allen and Singleton. However, he got extended playing time in the Pinstripe Bowl win over Clemson, tallying 20 carries for 103 yards in his first time seeing double-digit touches in a game.

It was a flash of the former 4-star prospect’s potential, but for Martin to earn the No. 1 running back role next season, he’d have to beat out portal additions Carson Hansen and James Peoples.

Hansen transferred in from Iowa State, where he has proven production with 952 rushing yards in 2025. Peoples had a limited role with Ohio State the last two seasons, totaling 541 career rushing yards.

It’s a three-way battle for the top spot in the running back room, but Martin could be set up well to build on his Pinstripe Bowl performance in spring ball and become the starter.

title: CATA to launch tap-to-pay option in April
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
CATA announced it will be launching a tap-to-pay option for riders in April powered by Token Transit, according to a press release.

Riders will be able to use a contactless credit or debit card to purchase CATA bus passes, as well as other mobile payment options like Apple Wallet and Google Pay.

The new system is expected to help with faster boarding times, reduce cash handling, provide greater flexibility for occasional and first-time riders and improve overall rider experience.

For those who wish to purchase the fares in advance and scan a QR code when boarding, the Token Transit app will still be available.

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell gives updates on Rocco Becht, team health
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Though the State College weather might not consistently feel like it, spring has arrived in Happy Valley, with Tuesday kicking off the first day of spring practice for Penn State.

On the premiere day of spring ball, Matt Campbell took the podium in the Beaver Stadium media room to address the state of the program.

Here’s what he had to say.

Rocco Becht

When Rocco Becht spoke to the media for the first time in February, he planned on throwing for the first time that weekend coming off a 2025 season where he tore his labrum in his non-throwing shoulder and an AC joint sprain in his throwing shoulder.

The initial plan was for him to be close to fully back by the middle of spring ball, but Campbell said the quarterback seems to be making strides.

“I think Rocco is a little bit ahead of schedule, in terms of physically. He was able to get out and do some drill work,” Campbell said. “I think the biggest thing for him is he won't get live reps. But I think things like 7-on-7, three over two routes on air. I do think he's going to get a lot of that work, which I think will be really, really good for him.”

Campbell said Becht has been throwing for probably a little over three weeks, and the important thing with him is to not rush the process. While not operating at full strength, having him participate in some football activity is proving to be valuable.

“We got to make sure we work slow and right with Rocco rather than fast and wrong,” Campbell said. “It’s certainly huge to have him out there (for individual drills) and certainly huge to have him, even today, out in the group. For those young quarterbacks to be able to watch him and to have his leadership on the field, that's been big for us.”

Early days

Since arriving in December, Campbell has frequently referred to his journey from Iowa State to Penn State as a “whirlwind.” He did that again when reflecting on his first few months in State College.

He then joked about struggling to get into the facility, but the process of building a program right when he touched down in Happy Valley has helped him get acclimated.

“I feel like, kind of hit the ground running, and the process it takes to build a football team, year in, year out, and build a football program,” Campbell said. “I don't think those things change.”

While football is an important part of his life, Campbell also cited the importance of having his family as well as the other staff member’s families adjust was as critical.

“I think the biggest thing is getting our families here, my own family, our coaching staff’s families, getting ourselves settled,” Campbell said. “That was the biggest process, and making sure everybody feels confident and comfortable coming into the facility every day ready to kind of attack the opportunity that we have.”

Kashif Moore

The last time Campbell met with the media was National Signing Day in February. At the time of that gathering, Noah Pauley was the wide receivers coach for Penn State.

Later that day, Pauley spoke for the first time — that would be the last time he would do so, as the next day he took the same role with the Green Bay Packers.

In turn, Campbell turned to Kashif Moore, who spent the same amount of time at Colorado State that Pauley did at Penn State, and was the wide receivers coach last season with UConn.

“I think with coach Moore, his background as a player, he was a captain on those great UConn teams, won the Orange Bowl his senior year as a captain,” Campbell said. “I love that. I loved his leadership ability. I loved his journey.”

Moore had various stints in the NFL, playing for the Indianapolis Colts, San Diego Chargers, Houston Texans, Pittsburgh Steelers and Kansas City Chiefs, but he never appeared in an NFL game after being a standout himself at UConn.

He then worked his way up in the coaching world, notably coaching Skyler Bell this past season to becoming a Biletnikoff Award finalist, which is given to the best wideout in college football.

“I think (Bell) last year, after what he did as a junior, and what he did as a senior, was really impressive,” Campbell said. “I think he'll be a first, second round type draft pick. You got to see some videotape evidence of his teaching as well. All those things fit to me of what we were looking for. And then I think who he is as a man …. what he was about. I think all those things were a great fit for us.”

Health

Ahead of spring ball, there will be some missing faces when the Nittany Lions suit up.

Campbell does have an availability report for guys that will be missing, which he didn’t bring with him to the press conference, but did mention how wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen won’t get live reps this spring.

“(They) can do some individual (drills), can do some routes on air.”

Other absences that were made known through reports and confirmation from players during a media availability are tight ends Andrew Rappleyea and Gabe Burkle, offensive tackle Anthony Donkoh and safety Jeremiah Cooper. Defensive end Max Granville and linebacker Tony Rojas will likely also be limited.

Despite a slight running list, Campbell isn’t that worried about the status of his guys.

“You're going to have injuries, and you're going to have guys coming back from season-ending injuries, and some of those are our major, some of those are clean up,” Campbell said. “I think from my end (going) slow and right with anybody that's coming off of an injury is still priority No. 1.”

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Penn State softball falls short in home series against Wisconsin
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Halfway through its 2026 campaign, Penn State had its first home series last weekend at Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field. The team battled against Wisconsin during a three-game series.

Over the weekend, Penn State had mixed results, losing the home opener after back-and-forth action, 6-5, then dominating Saturday morning, outscoring Wisconsin with a final score of 14-8. But for the series finale, the Nittany Lions fell 7-4.

Having only one win in Big Ten play, the blue and white have collected five of its eleven losses this season from conference opponents.

Here is what went well and what went wrong for Penn State during its home-opening weekend.

Late-inning challenges in circle

Within the last six weeks, the blue and white’s pitching staff’s depth and their performance in the circle has been its defining characteristic. For the first game at Beard Field of the 2026 season, four Nittany Lions stepped into the circle on Friday.

Freshman right-handed pitcher Mackenzie Duncan earned her fourth collegiate start, followed by junior right-hander McKenna Young, who dominated in the circle as the first reliever. Young pitched for 2.0 innings and struck out three Badgers in her first appearance at home, allowing one earned run, after transferring to Penn State during the offseason.

Left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris threw for 2.1 frames, but sophomore right-handed pitcher Abigail Britton entered for the final inning, getting the short end of the stick by taking home her third loss of the season.

Penn State played an error-free game for the 13th time this season.

For Saturday’s duel, the offense stole the spotlight. Yet softball isn’t all about the batter's box, but also the circle.

Bridget Nemeth got the start, making her return to the circle at Beard Field for the first time since 2024. The 6-foot-1 star tallied 3.1 innings, surrendering zero walks, five hits with just three earned runs, while successfully striking out four Badgers.

Avenging her final inning against Wisconsin in the first meeting, Britton got the start for the team’s final game of the weekend. She threw for 4.1 innings, scattering four hits with two earned runs allowed, while adding five strikeouts to her resume.

Nemeth returned to the circle for Sunday’s showdown. She recorded her worst performance of the season, pitching for 1.2 innings, allowing three hits and four runs.

The blue and white lacked consistency throughout the last stretch of its matchups against the Badgers.

Lineup changes deliver successful at-bats

Coach Clarisa Crowell changed up the Nittany Lion lineup for the home opening weekend, creating offensive momentum.

She gave junior designated player Jordan George her first start of the 2026 campaign for Saturday's match. She appeared as a starter for the blue and white’s batting order just for the second time since transferring to Penn State before the 2025 season.

The New Albany, Ohio, native went 3-for-3 at the plate, which included individual career bests in hits with two home runs and five RBI’s.

Freshmen have been a big part of Penn State's season so far, and outfielder Breanna Hanik added to the contribution. Hanik and George powered through the middle of the weekend series, adding to the blue and white’s record book after its dominating 14-8 win against Wisconsin.

Penn State’s pair of power hitters became the first pair of teammates to record multiple home run outings. Hanik also finished the day going 3-for-3, with three RBI and four runs scored. The Oak Lawn, Illinois, native also marked her career bests with three hits and two homers. She recorded the 24th multi-homer outing in Nittany Lion history shortly after George completed the 23rd.

This performance from the blue and white’s offense was a must after lacking in the batter's box in its West Coast series against then-No. 21 Oregon.

Junior Frankee Flesher, who retired from soccer and joined the softball program in the offseason, got her first real chance, making five appearances at the plate over the weekend.

Junior third baseman Brooke Klosowicz was another Nittany Lion successful at the plate over the course of the weekend. She finished the home opener going 3-for-4 with a run scored and one RBI. She hit her first triple of the season, finishing the day 1-for-4 with a run scored and two RBIs in Sunday’s game.

Friday and Sunday’s games showed sparks from the offense, which were quickly killed by the Badgers' defense, but that’s all that the blue and white left with. Pitching problems and a lack of concrete play resulted in the losses.

With success in at-bats, if Penn State figures out consistency, it could have more control in the result of the games as conference play continues.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: 7 Women-owned businesses in downtown State College
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
A bookstore, a clothing boutique and a toy store — what do these three seemingly unrelated stores have in common? They're all women-owned businesses located in downtown State College.

As the old saying goes, "girls rule," and in State College, that certainly seems to ring true.

If you're interested in celebrating Women's History Month one way or another, you might want to consider checking out these stores and supporting the women who run them.

After all, what's better than a little retail therapy to end off the month?

To get you started and familiarized with some of these businesses, here's seven women-owned stores that are worth stopping by this week.

1. The Makery

Not only is The Makery women-owned and founded by Amy Frank, a mixed media instructor, but the entire staff consists of women.

The Makery is an artistic space that hosts classes, open studios, parties and summer camps, featuring mediums ranging from knitting and painting to creative writing, making it the perfect scene for beginners (or advanced students) to get creative.

The Makery is located at 123 S. Fraser St. on MLK Plaza.

2. Growing Tree Toys

If you're looking for a place to buy a Jellycat or NeeDoh stress ball, Growing Tree Toys is the place for you.

Owned by Lindsay Jones, Growing Tree Toys features a wide array of toys and games to spark your inner-child with colorful stuffed animals lining the walls and shelves.

The store is located at 202 S. Allen St.

3. Webster's Bookstore Cafe

For the bookworm in your life, Webster's Bookstore Cafe is certainly a great choice.

With rows of books stretching out across the store, there's no limit to what you might find. While you're poring over those, you may get thirsty and choose to get a drink from their cafe.

Elaine Meder-Wilgus owns the used bookstore located at 133 E. Beaver Ave. and the space hosts more than books and food, as the space also hosts live events like poetry readings for the general public.

4. Connections

Although Connections has been around since 1996, it officially became women-owned in 2019 and carries everything for your clothing needs.

Located at 130 S. Allen St., the store features brands such as Free People and Boys Lie on its clothing racks, sure to help you find your next pieces for a stunning outfit.

From dresses to accessories, there's something for everyone at Connections.

5. Drip Studio

Is it time to finally get that tattoo you've been wanting or are you looking for another piercing to complete your earring stack? If so, Drip Studio may be the place for you.

Established in 2022, Drip Studio features various tattoo artists and piercers with different specialties, each with their own unique perspectives on the art form.

Jen Eisenhauer, owner of the tattoo studio, wants to "modernize the tattooing scene," according to Drip Studio's website.

Drip Studio is located at 248 E. Calder Way.

6. Lock Boutique

Another clothing store, Lock has everything you need to look stylish this Women's History Month.

Whether you're looking for something casual or classy, Lock's array of clothing is sure to rise up to the challenge.

With a heart-shaped, pink flower wreath hanging above its door, Lock creates a welcoming atmosphere for customers, and usually features unique Penn State inspired pieces for gamedays and other school related events.

Owned by Lydia Shafer, who originally opened the business in Bellefonte, Lock Boutique is now located at 216 E. College Ave.

7. Evolve Studio

Maybe you want to pamper yourself to commemorate the final days of Women's History Month. The first step to take when deciding to spoil yourself is going to a salon to get your hair cut or colored.

For these, you may want to visit Evolve Studio, a hair salon owned by Ashley Brady.

Whether you're looking for a blowout or a keratin treatment, Evolve located at 326 W. Beaver Ave. can definitely provide.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball to face rival Ohio State
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is getting ready to face one of its biggest rivals, Ohio State, from neutral territory at 6 p.m. Wednesday in Pittsburgh.

Taking the court for the final official match from Fitzgerald Field House, the No. 17 Nittany Lions look to get back in the win column after a tough week on the West Coast that resulted in three-straight losses.

But it might not be easy. A long-standing history with the No. 15 Buckeyes and a week of losses to ranked opponents leaves the blue and white desperate to get back on a good note.

Ohio State enters the contest at 12-9 on the season, while also on a three-game winning streak. Penn State, however, enters at 11-7, while on a three-game losing streak. But it won’t be easy for either team on Wednesday.

The Buckeyes will take the floor in Pittsburgh at No. 14 in the NCAA in hitting percentage this year. They also rank No. 18 in aces per set.

The blue and white, however, sit much farther down in both categories. At No. 32 in hitting percentage and No. 28 in aces per set.

Service has been a struggle for the Nittany Lions this season, especially during the West Coast road trip. Across the three games on the road, the blue and white only tallied nine service aces. All season, the Nittany Lions have 83 total.

Ohio State, however, has totaled 116 thus far. And that’s not the only area where it's putting up impressive numbers.

The Buckeyes currently rank No. 4 in the NCAA in kills per set, averaging 12.86. With a total of 887 kills on the year, it will be crucial that Penn State’s defense comes out hot to combat that strong attack from Ohio State.

With 220 kills and a 3.55 kill per set average, Ohio State’s Shane Wetzel could be a problem on Wednesday. In just one game this season, the senior recorded 19 kills, posing a major threat to the blue and white.

But it might not be a complete walk in the park for Ohio State. At No. 3 in the NCAA in blocks per set, the Nittany Lions are a force to be reckoned with at the net.

In fact, Penn State middle blocker Owen Rose currently leads the NCAA in blocks per set. With a 1.328 block per set average, the redshirt senior has totaled a massive 81 blocks this year.

Not too far behind Rose, however, is Eliel Salva-Torres of the Buckeyes. At No. 8 in the NCAA in blocks per set, he could hinder the blue and white’s attack.

But when thinking about strong defensive players, Ryan Merk cannot be ignored.

The libero earned his seventh-career EIVA Defensive Player of the Week award following the West Coast series. He averaged 2.57 digs per set over two matches, while also tallying a 39-of-40 serve receive rate.

The age-old rivals always bring the heat, and Wednesday shouldn’t be any different.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘A dream come true’ | New Penn State women’s basketball coach Tanisha Wright rejoices joining her alma mater
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After donning the blue and white during her collegiate playing career in the early 2000s, Tanisha Wright was named the seventh head coach in Lady Lion history last Thursday.

On Monday, she had an opportunity to speak to the media for the first time in her introductory press conference, expressing her excitement in returning to Penn State.

“This is a dream come true for me after being gone for 20 years,” Wright said. “Never could I have imagined sitting in front of you today as the new head coach.”

Following Wright’s time in Happy Valley — where she became one of the greatest players in Lady Lion history — Wright became the highest-drafted WNBA player in Penn State history, going 12th overall in the 2005 WNBA Draft.

Between a 14-year playing career and a six-year coaching career in the professional leagues, the West Mifflin, Pennsylvania, native amassed another dominant career at the highest level. During the latter years of her WNBA career, she bounced around as an assistant coach and eventually head coach with the Atlanta Dream and various teams, gathering a strong coaching background.

“I’ve learned how important it is to really build those relationships and how important it is to really dive in and allow the athletes to know how important they are as people and not just players,” Wright said.

That relationship is what brought Wright to Penn State, where she returned home in a dream opportunity.

“I’m here at Penn State because this is an amazing opportunity, and I get a chance to really impact and influence young athletes,” Wright said. “I get a chance to impact the program that has meant so much to me and that’s given so much to me.”

Following a year with several new coaching hires for the blue and white, Wright marks the latest of the big-name hires whom vice president for intercollegiate athletics Pat Kraft has made.

“I think Pat’s vision for Penn State Athletics is something that really drew me to wanting to be back,” Wright said. “I think seeing all the success and all the different programs with women’s hockey, men’s hockey, women’s volleyball, the things that are going on with football, is really what drew me.”

While the Lady Lions haven’t been a top program over the last several years, with just one winning season during former coach Carolyn Kieger’s seven years with Penn State, Wright hopes to return Penn State to its former glory.

“There’s so many of you who want to see this program back to where it has been in the past,” Wright said. “We’re looking to rebuild that.”

Just five days into her new role, Wright is ready to attack the transition as the seventh coach in Lady Lion history, speaking volumes to what she hopes to do with the program.

“What I can promise is that I will give my all, I will fight, and I will continue to represent Penn State in a way that we know Penn State should be represented,” Wright said.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Allon Clay, Marie-Eloise LeClair named Big Ten Athletes to Watch
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off a historic indoor season, big things are in store for Penn State this outdoor season.

KEEP YOUR EYES ON THEM 👀Marie-Éloïse LeClair and Allon Clay have been named Big Ten Athletes to Watch!#WeAre #PSUTF pic.twitter.com/p8u0tfsGaB— Penn State Track & Field/Cross Country (@PennStateTFXC) March 23, 2026

The Nittany Lions got the ball rolling with a pair of Big Ten Athletes to Watch in sprinter Marie-Eloise LeClair and mid-distance runner Allon Clay.

LeClair joined the blue and white during the 2025 summer offseason and didn't compete with the team. However, she holds a personal best of 11.19 seconds in the 100-meter from her time with Simon Frazier, which is good enough for a school record at Penn State.

Clay is coming off a world championship appearance where he finished sixth in the World Athletics Indoor 800-meter finals and earned a bronze medal at the NCAA championship two weeks ago.

title: Letter to the Editor: The genocide is not over. The struggle continues until liberation.
author: Letter by Students for Justice in Palestine at Penn State
description:
This letter was written by Students for Justice in Palestine, an organization at Penn State dedicated to fighting for the liberation of Palestine and the freedom and self-determination of the Palestinian people. Anyone interested in submitting a letter to the editor may do so here.

It has been six months since the “ceasefire” in Gaza was announced. It was supposed to halt Israel’s aggression and genocide against the Palestinian people. Yet, in the past six months, Israel has continued launching airstrikes while blocking necessary and life-saving aid from entering Gaza and escalating attacks in the West Bank. Just last month, Israel’s security cabinet approved a series of policies that would result in de facto annexation of the occupied West Bank as Israeli settlers invaded Gaza and planted trees to mark their first step towards building Jewish-only settlements on ethnically cleansed lands.

Israel’s continued genocide in Gaza further extends their brutal war of extermination against all forms of Palestinian life. The Palestinian News & Information Agency reported on Feb. 5 that over 9,300 Palestinians are imprisoned in Israel, where they are starved, tortured and deprived of basic need. The Middle East Eye reported on Feb. 9 that Israel has advanced legislation mass execution of Palestinian prisoners. Meanwhile, Israel has canceled Friday prayers at Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem — one of the holiest sites in Islam — during the month of Ramadan under the pretext of “security concerns” due to its war with Iran, while continuing to allow Jewish Purim celebrations around the city.

As we witness the simultaneous aggression against the Palestinian people, bombing of Iran and invasion of Lebanon, we are reminded that the U.S. and Israel are the biggest threats to life in the Middle East. U.S. imperialism and Zionism, at their core, are hell-bent on the extermination of anything that stands in the way of their ambitions, from control of land to resource extraction. The people of Palestine, Lebanon and Iran refuse to submit or surrender. In the face of relentless bombings and mass murder, they have remained steadfast against U.S. and Zionist aggression.

It is our duty to challenge the manifestations of U.S. imperialism and Zionism on campus, whether it be university investments in weapons manufacturing companies, study abroad programs in Israel or collaborations with the military industrial complex. As Penn State SJP, we have witnessed how Zionism impacts not only Palestinian students, but also all marginalized students across campus. From the desecration of our memorial in Fall 2024 to Zionist opposition of our town hall for marginalized students, we must build a resilient movement against the destructive forces of U.S. imperialism and Zionism.

The Palestinian liberation movement continues to teach us that collective liberation requires collective struggle. Although Zionists seek to isolate SJP and our allies, we remain steadfast in our convictions. By organizing against Zionism and U.S. imperialism, including divesting our campus from weapons manufacturing, we can dismantle the war machine raging against the Middle East and within our own borders.

Join Penn State SJP for our week of action from March 23-27.

Monday: “The Genocide Has Not Ended” displayTuesday: “The Struggle Continues Until Liberation” protest at 6 p.m. at Old MainWednesday: Tabling in the HUBThursday: Keynote speech by American labor activist Chris Smalls in at 6:30 p.m. in Thomas 102Friday: Palestinian futurism film screening and art activity at 6:30 p.m. in Thomas 207

We look forward to building community and continuing the struggle for Palestinian liberation on our campus together.

MORE LETTERS TO THE EDITOR

title: Penn State baseball returns home against St. Bonaventure on Dollar Dog Night
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is coming off a recent win against Purdue on Sunday, where it crept away with a 14-9 win. While it won on Sunday, the blue and white didn’t win the first two games of the three-game series after losses on Friday and Saturday.

Now, the Nittany Lions will take on St. Bonaventure on Tuesday and Wednesday, with Tuesday being the first Dollar Dog Night of the season. First pitch is scheduled for 5:30 p.m. Tuesday.

Coach Mike Gambino’s squad has struggled in nonconference play this season and is now 6-15 overall. While in West Lafayette, Indiana, the team struggled defensively in the first two games, totaling four errors in its 2-1 loss on Saturday.

In Penn State’s win on Sunday, the crew relied on the second inning and late-game spurts of energy. In the second inning, the blue and white totaled 11 runs, providing it with extra insurance as Purdue chipped away at the lead and the Nittany Lions’ offense fell silent.

While Penn State escaped with a win on Sunday, Purdue nearly took the game away, chopping a 10-run lead to a three-run margin.

Going into this matchup against St. Bonaventure, the Nittany Lions need to not only figure out a way to score runs early and build offensive momentum, but also do so consistently, for more than one inning at a time.

St. Bonaventure has had an eerily similar start to the Nittany Lions, now 5-16 on the season.

While the Bonnies won four of their nine opening games, they went on an 11-game losing streak from March 1 to March 21.

This win drought finally ended on March 22 when the Bonnies picked up a win against Saint Louis, 14-3. This could be the confidence boost that coach Jason Rathbun’s crew needs heading to Medlar Field at Lubrano Park on Tuesday.

Against Saint Louis, freshman Christian LaMothe earned a career-high five RBI. Anthony Fernandez and Antonio Cordeiro also dominated at the plate, each earning three RBIs, out of a split total of nine at-bats. This trio could be dangerous against a patchy Nittany Lion defense.

On the contrary, Penn State’s Bryce Molinaro had a big hitting day against Purdue, knocking a triple and a homerun, directly earning Penn State two runs, and indirectly setting it up for a third.

Michael Anderson also had three RBIs in the game, one hit being a three-run home run, during the Nittany Lions’ 11-run second inning.

The last matchup between Penn State and St. Bonaventure was just last year, where the Nittany Lions won 10-5.

History is on Penn State’s side as four of the last six meetups have swayed in the blue and white’s favor, however, not by a large run difference.

The teams will meet in Happy Valley during Penn State’s first Dollar Dog Night of the season. With the promotion, the fans could play a factor in the game as the blue and white attempt to get things back on track.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling has 4 finalists for Dan Hodge Trophy
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Following a successful NCAA Championships in Cleveland, four Penn State wrestlers were named finalists for the Hodge Trophy.

Luke Lilledahl, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines and Josh Barr were among the seven finalists for the Hodge Trophy.

The Hodge Trophy is an award given out annually to the top wrestler in the country under four different criteria. The four criteria are record, dominance/bonus-point percentage, quality of competition and sportsmanship.

Lilledahl finished with a 25-0 record and a 58.3% bonus rate. Mesenbrink had a 27-0 record at 165 pounds with a 96.3% bonus rate. Haines closed out his final season with a 26-0 record and an 80.8% bonus rate. Barr wrestled to a 24-0 record and a 95.8% bonus rate.

All four of the Nittany Lions won their individual championship as part of Penn State's record-setting weekend.

The other three finalists are Oklahoma State's Sergio Vega at 141 pounds and Jax Forrest at 133 pounds. Both wrestlers are true freshmen, with Vega's bonus rate at 45.8%. Forrest joined in the second half of the season, enrolling early, and ended with a 88.8% bonus rate.

The last finalist is NC State heavyweight Isaac Trumble. The Wolfpack ended with a 21-0 record and a 71.4% bonus rate.

The winner will be announced at 1 p.m. EST on Tuesday, March 31.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball’s offense thrives in second conference series loss of the season
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State hit the road for its second Big Ten series of the season. After losing their first conference series to Iowa, the Nittany Lions headed to Indiana for a three-game series against Purdue.

In what was a hard-fought series, the blue and white left Alexander Field with a 2-4 conference record after losing two out of the three to the Boilermakers.

Despite coming up short in the two games, Penn State was able to show off its offense, totaling 23 runs over the weekend. However, the pitching problems continued to remain relevant.

The hot bat

The series consisted of two high-scoring affairs sandwiching a defensive battle in a 2-1 Purdue win. The bats were hot for Penn State as a few Nittany Lions showed off their strength over the three games, most notably Michael Anderson.

The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native leads the team in home runs with ten; two of his most recent came against the Boilermakers. The senior was a part of four total home runs for Penn State over the weekend; two came in the second inning of the final game. The Nittany Lions now hold the sixth-most homers in the Big Ten with 27 on the year.

Additionally, the four home runs add to the 21 total RBI over the weekend, the most from a series this season. In Game 3, Penn State was able to score 11 runs in the top of the second, the most in an inning since 2022. This dominant inning earned the Nittany Lions a 14-9 win, returning to Happy Valley fresh off a conference victory.

Pitching woes remain relevant

Although the offense was able to get back on track, the pitching for Penn State still continued to remain imperfect. The Nittany Lions continue to hold the worst ERA in the Big Ten at 8.15. The blue and white gave up 23 runs over the weekend and went to its bullpen seven times over the three games.

Colin Fitzgerald got the nod for the opening game, in which he earned five strikeouts and allowed three earned runs. However, after his outing, the bullpen gave up three additional runs. Combined with four errors, Purdue took the opener 12-7.

In what was a bright spot in the pitching over the weekend, Ben Hudson had a better outing than his previous two. The Medford, New Jersey, native struck out three batters and only allowed one run. The middle game was a back-and-forth battle that ultimately went in favor of the Boilermakers.

The lone victory in the series came in a high-scoring finale. Isaiah Shayter took the bump, recording three strikeouts and giving up four earned runs. The offense of the Nittany Lions continued to keep them in the game as four pitchers from the bullpen gave up five runs.

With the bats finding life, Penn State will now need support from its pitching staff to be able to keep up with its opponents.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Tanisha Wright introduced as Penn State women’s basketball’s new head coach
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Less than a week after being announced as the newest head coach for Penn State women’s basketball, Tanisha Wright took the stand inside the Beaver Stadium media room.

With a big smile on her face and vice president for intercollegiate athletics Pat Kraft’s face, Wright discussed the future of Lady Lion basketball and bringing it back to its roots, as well as the opportunity of a lifetime to return to her alma mater.

Here’s a look at what Wright said as the new era of Lady Lions’ basketball begins in Happy Valley.

Bringing one of our own home

“Tanisha is Penn State Lady Lion basketball,” Kraft said in the first few lines of his address.

Throughout his speech, he not only highlighted the success Wright has had as a player and a coach, but also marveled over characteristics that didn’t show up on her resume.

“When you talk to Tanisha, one thing becomes very clear — this place matters to her,” Kraft said. “This is personal.”

After four seasons of success with deep NCAA tournament runs at Penn State, Wright spent 20 years in the WNBA as a player, head coach and assistant coach. On March 19, she was announced as the next head coach, and just five days later, she made her first remarks donning the blue and white.

“This is a dream come true after being away for 20 years,” Wright said. “Never could I have imagined sitting in front of you today as the new head coach.”

Handling the collegiate landscape

While Wright has a lot of experience coaching in the WNBA, including being the 2022 AP Coach of the Year, she has very little collegiate coaching experience. In an era with an increased NIL and transfer portal environment, Wright’s new role will be a lot different than her role when she played at Penn State.

“I think my experience with being in pro, dealing with roster management, dealing with contracts, dealing with agents and building those relationships positions me to be able to handle the landscape that’s happening now,” Wright said.

In the five days since she was hired, Wright said she’s been “evaluating and assessing” what Penn State currently has and making decisions from there.

“Even though this is home, I’m like the new kid on the block,” Wright said. “Nobody in the neighborhood is the same people in the neighborhood, so it’s just time for me to sit back, evaluate the things I need to evaluate, and then we’ll assess from there.”

Staying on the path

Despite joining a Penn State team that has struggled in recent years, Wright didn’t have any worries or concerns about jumping right back into things and pushing for success.

“My goal is to build one day at a time,” Wright said. “Build it, build it back up. We know this place has success because we’ve seen it.”

While there are increased expectations with such a successful player and coach now at the helm, Wright didn’t make any promises for success in Year 1.

Instead, she went back through the history of the Lady Lions, and talked about the day-to-day growth that happened during her time at Penn State, as well as the Susan Robinson era — who was also in attendance — and noted that was the kind of growth and success she wanted.

“We’re going to put the energy and the effort in, and we’re going to rebuild this program back to what we knew it to be,” Wright said.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE
