title: Penn State women's hockey suffers overtime loss to Wisconsin in Frozen Four, ending season
author: Daniel Studer | The Daily Collegian
description:
What No. 3 women’s hockey coach Jeff Kampersal once described as one of the loudest locker rooms he’s ever been a part of is now filled with silence after Penn State’s season came to an end at the hands of the defending National Champions No. 2 Wisconsin.

The Badgers advanced to the National Championship with a 4-3 win over the Nittany Lions on a Friday night in front of a record-breaking 5,176 fans at Pegula Ice Arena.

Wisconsin head coach Mark Johnson was enamored with the atmosphere that came with the playoff hockey environment.

“(I) appreciated anybody in the student section that came out tonight because it was a treat for our players to play in front of," Johnson said. "Hopefully, for the Penn State players, it was a treat to them because it was a special night.”

In most hockey movies, it's the underdogs who win the big game and complete the upset. Unfortunately for Penn State, Hollywood is closer to Madison, Wisconsin, than State College, Pennsylvania. The star of this film was Kirsten Simms, who rolled the credits with her 100th career goal at Wisconsin in OT.

“It’s an honor,” Simms said. “But also, at the same time, it’s a testament to who I’ve gotten to play with over the last four years.”

Just one month ago, captain Tessa Janecke skated alongside the likes of Caroline Harvey and Simms at the Olympics, but showed no sign of friendship after giving Penn State an early 1-0 lead, nor when she tied the game with Penn State facing its final five minutes of the season.

The Badgers entered play on Friday with the top-ranked penalty kill in the nation, killing 90% of penalties, but a lack of discipline proved costly in the first period.

If there were any nerves following the opening puck drop, they were settled with ease after Penn State was gifted an early power play on an interference call against Charlotte Pieckenhagen following a hit in front of the Penn State bench.

On a one-time feed adjacent to the Badger goaltender Ava McNaughton, Janecke gave the blue and white a 1-0 lead just 10 seconds into the power play. It was the 31st time this season the Nittany Lions beat their opposition to the scoring sheet.

Not only did the goal serve as the first Frozen Four goal in program history, but it was also the 200th career point for the Orangeville, Illinois, native, making her the first player in Penn State hockey history to accomplish the bicentennial mark.

“It’s obviously a testament to this team and just how much better this program has got. Obviously, goals don’t come without assists, and not having those people on the ice, I wouldn’t have been able to do that,” Janecke said. “To get to do it at home in the Frozen Four against Wisconsin, it was pretty cool.”

“She left it all out there,” Kampersal said. “She can go out very proudly. I really do think she doesn’t care about points.”

The Nittany Lions relinquished that lead after roughly three minutes when Simms executed a perfect pass to the stick of Laila Edwards, who beat every Penn State skater and performed a nifty backhand move to beat Katie DeSa with ease.

A lack of discipline from Wisconsin was on full display after Laney Potter was assessed a minor penalty for high-sticking. Similar to the first Penn State power play, the blue and white only needed a brief moment to add to their lead.

Following an offensive-zone faceoff win, Maddy Christian redirected a pass from Mikah Keller towards the net, where the puck was lodged under the right pad of McNaughton. With no whistle for a frozen puck, a second-chance effort from Abby Stonehouse pushed the puck past the goal line to put Penn State back up 2-1.

After allowing the goal to Edwards, DeSa was consistently tested by the Badgers’ offense, but came up with an answer when needed. That included a pair of back-to-back saves from low in the slot that preserved the Penn State lead.

Similar to their penalty kill numbers, the Badgers lead the nation in power-play percentage, 35%, and power-play goals with 42.

In an effort to protect her goaltender after Wisconsin huddled around DeSa following a frozen puck, Nicole Hall took exception, which earned her a trip to the box for hitting after the whistle.

The red and white couldn’t capitalize on the short time remaining in the first period, giving them approximately 90 seconds on a power play to open the second period. However, any hope of adding to their power-play numbers vanished when Edwards was called for interference against Stonehouse, who tried to make a last-second effort for the puck as the horn signaled the end of the period.

After wasting the power play earned in the first period, Edwards made up for it in the second with her second goal of the night after Mya Vaslet was given a minor penalty for tripping.

The senior winger rocketed a one-timer from the near-side faceoff circle over DeSa’s blocker on a feed across the zone from Simms to even the score at 2-2. Penn State challenged on the grounds of a missed offside call, but after review, the call on the ice stood.

Building on the momentum of a successful power play, the Badgers continued to play at a pace for which Penn State had no answer. After leading the AHA conference in shots during the regular season, the Nittany Lions were held to just three shots on goal during the second period, an unusual fate.

With just shy of four minutes remaining in the middle frame of action, Šapovalivová picked up a loose puck after a battle in a corner and handled the puck around the net to get a shot off on a wrap-around attempt. DeSa made the initial save with the paddle of her stick, but the puck trickled through her five-hole, giving Wisconsin its first lead of the evening.

Penn State opened the final 20 minutes of regulation with an extra skater after Wisconsin’s fifth penalty of the game and just 41 seconds in, its sixth. Even with chants of “Let’s go Lions” raining down from the filled seats, the blue and white failed to capitalize on the two extra skaters.

With their season slipping away, the blue and white played the first half of the third period with a sense of desperation, needing a goal to tie the game. The only problem they ran into was that McNaughton was just as desperate.

Facing an onslaught attack from the Penn State offense, McNaughton lived up to her .900 winning percentage, the highest in the league, tied only by her counterpart across the ice in DeSa, and made a set of sprawling desperation saves. Minutes later, the Nittany Lions had another chance to the left of McNaughton, but were denied at the doorstep.

That was until Hall found Janecke all alone in the neutral zone to send her on a breakaway. Creeping in on McNaughton, Janecke faked a shot and transferred to her backhand and effortlessly put the puck around McNaughton's left skate to send the Pegula crowd into a frenzy with the score tied 3-3.

Penn State, playing less well-behaved as the game progressed, faced a deficit early in overtime after Kendall Butze was given a minor penalty for boarding. The Badgers challenged for a major call, but were unsuccessful, forfeiting their timeout.

Following the brief review, Simms' third point of the night was the game-winner just 50 seconds into overtime to send Wisconsin back to the National Championship to face Ohio State for the third consecutive year in pursuit of its ninth title.

Johnson is responsible for all eight previous titles, and looks to win his fourth in six seasons and to be the first team to win back-to-back championships since Clarkson in 2017 and 2018.

“We’ve got a big hill to climb right now. We gotta figure out how to try to win the hockey game Sunday. Ohio State’s playing at a real high level,” Johnson said.

While the Badgers prepare for the Buckeyes on Sunday, Penn State will prepare to clean out its lockers for the final time this season, including a group of seniors, most of whom have been on the team the last four years and earned four AHA Conference Championships. Kampersal had one last note about that particular group.

“I’ve been coaching a long time, and I just really hope that group stays together, Kampersal said. "Like connected through social media, through gathering here and there. It’s an awesome group in terms of humans.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey's breaks single-game Pegula Ice Arena, Frozen Four records
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The most historic game in program history continued to break records.

Thanks to you, #HockeyValley, we have SHATTERED our attendance record with 5,176 fans inside Pegula Ice Arena tonight 🤩#WeAre pic.twitter.com/KVRO2TrNmH— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 21, 2026

The Frozen Four drew the highest attendance in Penn State women's hockey history, as 5,176 fans packed into Pegula Ice Arena at 7:30 p.m. on Friday for the 2026 Women's Frozen Four against No. 2 Wisconsin. It stands as the highest attended Women's Frozen Four in history.

Previously, the single-game record at Pegula stood at 1,804 when the Nittany Lions faced Merrimack College on Oct. 6, 2018. This season's Beaver Stadium game set the previous single-game record at 1,838, but that figure was more than doubled.

The crowd immediately made a difference as Penn State scored early in the first period to break the ice. Captain Tessa Janecke recorded her 200th career point on the first goal of the night.

title: Tessa Janecke notches 200th career point in Frozen Four's first period
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's captain reached another milestone.

Tessa Janecke opened the 2026 Frozen Four game against No. 2 Wisconsin with a short-angle goal on the power play for her 200th career point.

With her powerplay goal in the first period, Tessa Janecke has hit 200 CAREER POINTS 🐐#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/aULxO3hERP— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 20, 2026

The goal was Janecke's 25th of the season and second in the national tournament, as she scored a goal against No. 6 UConn in the Regional Finals.

The Olympic gold-medalist now sits at 88 goals in her collegiate career as Penn State hockey's highest point-scorer in history.

title: Penn State baseball falls in series opener against Purdue on the road
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions made their 2026 debut at Alexander Field in West Lafayette, Indiana, to kick off their three-game series against Purdue.

Purdue took the win, finishing 12-7 against Penn State. While the game marked another loss for the team, they put up a strong fight in the end, leaving them with positive momentum for tomorrow’s game.

The first two innings remained quiet as neither team was able to put any runs on the board, making it seem like this would be an evenly matched game, and a chance for Penn State to come back from its most recent loss to the Iowa Hawkeyes.

However, the Boilermakers dominated the third inning, putting up five runs with an RBI single, with runs put up by Ali Banks and Brandon Rogers, before Aaron Manias closed out the inning with a home run into right-center field with a double RBI.

Spencer Barnett was able to respond in the fourth with a run for the blue and white from third base as Jesse Jaconski singled to shortstop, but fell short as Purdue’s Dylan Drake scored the team’s sixth run.

Cohl Mercado earned Penn State its second run with a homerun down right field, but was quickly met by three more runs by the Boilermakers, leaving the fifth inning 9-2.

Once again, the game continued its back-and-forth, as Michael Anderson, one of Penn State's strongest hitters, scored his seventh home run of the season in the top of the seventh, but was met by two more Purdue runs by Jackson Bessette and Drake from Purdue.

Things started looking up for Penn State, starting with a hit-by-pitch on Preston Yaucher, enabling Jayden Davis to score a forced run. Mercado hit the ball down the right field line, where Yaucher, Justin Turcovski, and Bryce Molinaro brought in three runs for the team, leaving it 11-7.

Purdue brought in Jackson Greer from the bullpen to relieve pitcher Noah Filer to close out the inning, leaving Penn State on a high note going into the ninth.

While they fell short of a comeback, Penn State left off on a relatively high note for the rest of their weekend series in Indiana.

Up Next

Penn State will return to Alexander Field for its second game in the series against the Boilermakers tomorrow at 2 p.m. EST.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Centre County PAWS receives 37 dogs amid ongoing humane investigation
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Centre County PAWS announced in a press release that they have received 37 dogs, including 3 litters of puppies, as a result of an ongoing humane investigation by Centre County Humane Society Police.

When the dogs arrived at the facility they were in “poor condition,” according to the press release. All the animals had flea infestations, never received veterinary care, were covered in urine and fecal matter and showed signs of neglect.

Upon their arrival to Centre County Paws, the dogs all received immediate medical care including bathing, microchipping and core vaccinations. Currently, the animal care team is working on evaluating the dogs’ medical and behavioral needs, according to the release.

At this time, Centre County PAWS is asking for support from the community in helping the dogs receive the care they need while also allowing the group to help other community animals. Donation, wishlist and foster home information can all be found on Centre County PAWS’ website.

The dogs are not currently up for adoption due to their need for further medical treatment and behavioral evaluation.

As this is an active humane investigation, further details of the case cannot be shared at this time, according to the press release.

title: ‘I walked out of there with a smile on my face’ | Colin Jost sparks laughter at his show at the BJC
author: Sara O'Connell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Colin Jost’s comedy performance at the Bryce Jordan Center gave the crowd a night of jokes and hilarious anecdotes after he took to the stage on Wednesday evening.

The audience cheered as Kam Patterson, a “Saturday Night Live (SNL)” performer, entered the stage to begin the comedy show featuring Michael Longfellow and main act Colin Jost.

Patterson’s opening brought a bout of laughter from the crowd as he made jokes about his sex talk with his father, as well as his sister and dad’s conversation regarding her sexuality. His jokes lit up the room, and served as a predecessor for the other acts.

Elena Cirello came to the event after seeing free tickets being advertised and convinced her roommate to join. She said Patterson’s routine was entertaining and a great way to start off the night.

“He did a great job of warming up the crowd and got us all really excited,” Cirello, a third-year studying pharmacology and toxicology, said.

Following Patterson, Longfellow, a former “SNL” performer, took the stage. He began crowd work with the audience by playing a game of “f—, marry, kill,” featuring Nick the dog, Michael B. Jordan and a Penn State student picked from the audience.

Longfellow’s dry comedic style brought rolls of laughter, according to audience member, Avanti Vairagkar.

Despite being drawn to the show by Colin Jost, Madelyn Grim said she enjoyed both of the openers.

“I've seen them on ‘SNL’ as well, and they're both hilarious,” Grim, a second-year studying music education, said. “So I was very pleasantly surprised when they announced the openers.”

As Longfellow exited the stage, the crowd enthusiastically cheered for Jost as he walked to the center stage.

To connect to the large numbers of college students in attendance, Jost read off Penn State fun facts at the beginning of his performance in his well-known “Weekend Update” style.

Jost amused the audience with jokes that included both political references and other content SNL wouldn’t typically allow. The crowd roared as he recounted stories from his life regarding his children and a fantastical story about a fully cooked chicken at the grocery store.

Grim said she’s a longtime fan of “Saturday Night Live” and particularly loves Jost’s segments.

“I’ve been a huge fan of ‘SNL’ and ‘Weekend Update’ for as long as I can remember.” Grim said. “I love his comedy style, and the second I saw that they announced he was coming to the BJC, I just knew I had to get tickets and see him in person.”

While there were many stand-out moments to the show, Cirello and Vairagkar, a PhD student studying veterinary and biomedical sciences, agreed that Jost’s retelling of an incident regarding marijuana and a rotisserie chicken was a hilarious top contender.

Cirello thoroughly enjoyed the performance, and said she was pleased at the turnout of the event.

“I like seeing that we can all connect on a show, and I feel more connected to Penn State,” she said.

Vairagkar said she's seen Jost on “Saturday Night Live,” and she wanted the chance to see how his stand-up comedy would fare. She said she wouldn’t have been able to see him without this performance at Penn State.

“I’m from India, so these are people I’ve dreamt of watching live, and in India, I would’ve never gotten that opportunity,” Vairagkar said.

Because of her rigorous course load, she was happy to be able to take a break from classes and have a good laugh.

“As a PhD student, the experience is different from undergrad,” Vairagkar said. “I feel like it’s so much work and co-hort and lab, so I get to get out of that space and experience everything else the university has to offer.”

Grim said Penn State is "lucky" to have performances from celebrities like Jost because many smaller schools don’t get that opportunity. Through this show, she felt more connected to, not only Penn State, but the State College community.

“I walked out of there with a smile on my face,” Grim said. “I had had a tough day earlier in the day, and I was just looking forward to the comedy show, and it definitely made my day so much better.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling goes perfect in NCAA Championships’ quarterfinals, achieves 8 All-Americans
author: Wrestling staff
description:
CLEVELAND — Penn State entered Day 2 of the NCAA Championships with a 15.5 point lead in the team standings. The second day of the weekend is the most important, as point totals take a jump for each win.

The Nittany Lions extended the lead in commanding fashion, now leading with 105.5 total points over second place Nebraska with 66. Penn State went a perfect 8-for-8 in its quarterfinal matches, as the group all achieved All-American status. Both wrestlers in the consolations won at least a match, but then failed to advance to the blood round.

Here’s how Penn State did in Session 3.

125 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs No. 8 Dean Peterson (IOWA)

Luke Lilledahl entered this match having defeated Dean Peterson in both previous matchups this season.

Both Lilledahl and Peterson attempted to take each other down, but both showed elite defense early. The sophomore let Peterson up almost instantly to start the second period. Not much else occurred as the match continued, as both wrestlers seemed to be more conservative.

Lilledahl escaped in three seconds and got a hold of Peterson’s leg, but was unable to get a hold of the other leg, forcing the match to sudden victory. Lilledahl came close to another takedown, but couldn’t wrap both legs.

The sophomore went for a takedown, but almost got caught by Peterson with him almost getting taken down by Lilledahl right after. Lilledahl escaped in 11 seconds, and the sophomore stayed on top for the rest of the tiebreaker and got the victory, 2-1, in tiebreakers.

133 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 3 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs No 6. Drake Ayala (IOWA)

This is the third match between these two this season, with Marcus Blaze being victorious in both.

Blaze scored first with an escape, and turned it into a takedown, to take a 4-0 lead. Blaze kept Ayala on the mat for most of the third period. Blaze got called for stalling and allowed Ayala to escape, but managed to get the victory, 5-3.

141 pounds

Wrestlebacks: No. 14 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs No. 29 Jordan Titus (WVU)

Braeden Davis started out as the aggressive wrestler. The junior threw Jordan Titus on to the mat twice and somehow gave up the first takedown. The call was under review for quite some time and it was overturned.

Davis got the takedown and Titus was awarded with a reversal, which gave Davis a 4-2 lead after an escape. The junior gave up the lead shortly after, as Titus got a takedown. Davis escaped and started to turn it up a notch.

The junior secured his third takedown of the match to take a 9-5 lead and get the 10-6 victory.

Wrestlebacks part 2: No. 14 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs No. 11 CJ Composto (PENN)

It was a stagnant first period for both Braeden Davis and CJ Composto. Neither scored any points and were feeling each other out.

Composto showed up in the second period with a reversal and a takedown to take a 5-1 lead. Composto continued to be the better wrestler even when Davis would attempt to get something going offensively, but there was no way around Composto’s defense.

Composto got the victory 5-2. Davis ends his NCAA Championships with a 2-2 record.

149 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs. No. 8 Casey Swiderski (OKST)

The first matchup between Penn State and Oklahoma State featured Shayne Van Ness and Casey Swiderski.

The match started out with a feeling out process, as neither wrestler did much. Swiderski got Van Ness on a single leg, but after a scramble, neither wrestler scored.

Swiderski escaped in the second period, and Van Ness had an opportunity to do the same to begin the third. However, Van Ness reversed Swiderski, giving the Nittany Lion a 2-1 lead.

A Swiderski escape sent the match to sudden victory. After getting in a precarious position, Van Ness seemingly had the takedown, but a neutral was called to restart the play.

With 15 seconds to go, Van Ness and Swiderski traded attacks, second-after-second, until Van Ness grabbed the legs and secured the takedown with one second remaining, taking the 5-2 victory.

157 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 PJ Duke (PSU) vs. No. 8 Brandon Cannon (OHST)

What was once a highly anticipated matchup has now been completely shut down by the top-ranked wrestler. PJ Duke and Brandon Cannon met for the second time this season, and the scoring from the Nittany Lion was on showcase.

Under 30 seconds in, Duke secured his first takedown over Cannon. Under a minute later, he scored his second before getting the third takedown of the period with three seconds remaining.

Leading 9-2, Duke went back on the attack in the second frame. He matched his three takedowns in the first period with three takedowns in the second period. Under 30 seconds into the third period, the No. 1 seed notched his seventh takedown, securing the 21-5 tech fall over Cannon.

165 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs. No. 9 Bryce Hepner (UNC)

Mitchell Mesenbrink battled against Bryce Hepner in the quarterfinals with an All-American bid on the line. Mesenbrink consistently took shots, but Hepner’s defense stood strong, getting himself out of scrambles to keep the match scoreless.

In the middle of the first period was a lengthy break, as medical staff checked on Hepner. While a stretcher came out for Hepner, the Tar Heel got himself back to his feet and was able to continue the match.

Mesenbrink started on top to begin the second period, and worked Hepner to his back to earn four-nearfall points. The reigning national champion escaped to begin the third period and couldn’t get in on any attacks.

Mesenbrink’s 6-0 victory meant his 100% bonus rate was no more, but he became a three-time All-American and advanced to the semifinals.

174 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 9 Beau Mantanona (MICH)

Beau Mantanona began this match by immediately dropping to a knee, as Levi Haines attempted to lock up with him. Mantanona confused the strategy, keeping Haines at bay for the first half of Period 1. Once the referee reset the bout to the center circle, Haines took down Mantanona. Haines rode out the rest of the period, nearly pinning Mantanona, but the clock expired after he was awarded two-nearfall points.

After starting in neutral for period two, it took Haines 45 seconds to secure his second takedown, and 45 seconds after that secured his third. He led 11-1 after the second frame with over two minutes of riding time.

Haines started the third period on top, and Mantanona escaped to gain a second point. Haines quickly secured two more takedowns to secure a tech fall with the riding-time point, winning 18-3.

184 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs. No. 8 Silas Allred (NEB)

Much like Haines’ bout, Rocco Welsh struggled to get early action out of a defensive Silas Allred. Although Welsh picked Allred’s leg in the period, he couldn’t leverage more than a stalemate. The first period ended 0-0.

In period two, Welsh began on the ground and escaped six seconds into Allred’s ride. He began bouncing around Allred looking to score, and landed the first takedown of the bout within the first minute. The Nittany Lion extended his lead to 7-1 before the period expired.

At the start of the third period, Allred suffered an apparent leg injury and was helped off the mat, advancing Welsh to the semifinals via injury default.

197 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs. No. 9 Angelo Posada (STAN)

Unlike the two undefeated Nittany Lions who wrestled before him, Josh Barr didn’t need to spend the first period in a scoreless stalemate. He had Angelo Posada on the ground 30 seconds into the contest.

Posada was unable to move under Barr as he rode out the period, and tallied over 2:30 of riding time and a four-point near fall on the edge of the mat.

Barr took Posada down twice after a thirty-second chase in the second period, and began looking for a tech fall. One last takedown secured the 19-3, tech-fall victory for Barr.

Following Mesenbrink's decision victory, Barr is the only wrestler in the country with a 100% bonus-point rate.

285 pounds

Wrestlebacks: No. 9 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs. No. 23 Stephan Monchery (APP)

Cole Mirasola began his day at 3:13 p.m. after his roster went undefeated throughout Session 3 before he entered the Rocket Arena floor. Mirasola didn’t want to wait to add his name to the winners circle, taking down Stephan Monchery 30 seconds into his bout.

After riding Monchery for 28 seconds, Mirasola went back on offense for a second takedown. After his third takedown of the period. Mirasola focused on trying to turn Monchery over for a pin. Although he wasn’t successful in a first-period pin, he amassed 2:03 of riding time.

He was once again close to a pin, after takedown No. 4 left Monchery on his shoulder, but Monchery rolled to his stomach to avoid the danger. In the third period, Mirasola tripped Monchery twice for back-to-back takedowns, finishing off the 19-3 victory.

Wrestlebacks Pt. 2: No. 9 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs. No. 18 Christian Carroll (WYO)

Penn State’s final match of the afternoon served as Mirasola’s ticket to the blood round, and a chance to avenge an early season loss for the redshirt freshman. Mirasola fell to Christian Carroll during Penn State’s dual in Wyoming earlier this season.

The first period served as a shoving match between the two heavyweights without any true attack opportunities for either.

Despite getting up quick from the bottom position in the second period, Carroll took down Mirasola as he tried to escape off the mat. After standing up, Mirasola returned the shot to take a 5-4 lead.

The score was knotted 5-5 in the third period until Mirasola gave up a stall point, and then gave up a takedown to Carroll. Mirasola was eliminated from the consolation bracket with a 9-6 loss to Carroll.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball delays home opener first pitch against Wisconsin to 7 p.m.
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fans will have to wait a couple of hours longer than anticipated to see action at Beard Field.

After previously being scheduled for 5 p.m, Penn State's home opener against Wisconsin will now start at 7 p.m. on Friday due to anticipated rain.

Due to inclement weather in the area, first pitch has been delayed to 7 p.m. (ET)! Can't wait to see everyone back at Beard Field tonight! Remember, ADMISSION IS FREE🆓‼️#WeAre | #AllIn https://t.co/rvCoMBvYMC— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) March 20, 2026

The Friday night meeting marks the first of three games between the Nittany Lions and Badgers this weekend.

The blue and white is looking for its first Big Ten win after going 0-3 at Oregon last weekend.

MORE SOFTBALL CONTENT

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse begins home Big Ten play against Michigan
author: Aeryn Ajot | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State’s start to the Big Ten play in Happy Valley is in a game against Michigan at noon on Sunday. The Nittany Lions' record stands at 9-1, with their only recent loss last week against then-No. 19 Princeton, their first against a top-20 school.

Can Penn State’s momentum be regained after its fresh loss, or can No. 8 Michigan pull a win over the No. 21 blue and white?

Past matches

Since 2014, Penn State has competed against the Wolverines every year. Taking a year off from the clash during the COVID closures, they have competed 12 times, with varying results. The first four years of matchups proved to be smooth victories for the Nittany Lions, with the smallest point difference being five goals in 2016.

But 2018 was the breaking point for Michigan, where the formula for winning was found, and Penn State started to lose. Since that turning point, in nine more games Penn State would only turn to win twice: in 2021 in an overtime home game and in 2025 in a close 13-12 match win. The total record against Michigan for the 12 games is 6-6. The next game will come the week after break, where the Lions will get some rest and relaxation and have settled back into Happy Valley, ready for the opportunity to rematch against a Big Ten school.

2026 Michigan

The Wolverines haven’t had a perfect season. They come into the game this weekend with a 6-2 record, with losses against No. 25 Notre Dame and No. 16 Yale.

In the game schedule, the losses came with a quick turnaround, establishing a now three-week winning streak, gaining victorious momentum.

Michigan has proven to be a valuable contender with success against high-ranking schools like No. 5 Florida, No. 6 Boston and No. 14 Colorado. However, the lower-ranking schools have been the cause of its losses. The lack of a pattern between ranked schools and its effect on Michigan’s 2026 team makes predictions difficult; however, a winning streak and recent history against Penn State suggest the Wolverines may have the upper hand.

2026 Penn State

The Lions' ranking before break dropped; before they sat with a No. 17 ranking, off the backs of victories over ranked teams in Pittsburgh and Cornell.

The blue and white also opened its Big Ten slate with a 19-8 win over USC in the West Coast.

However, the weekend after Penn State’s spring break, it suffered its first loss, 14-11, to No. 22 Princeton. The momentum built over eight games has been lost, and the winning streak is over.

The blue and white have had momentum since February, but the loss less than a week ago proves to be a question on their ability to defeat Michigan. The record shows that competition between the Wolverines has always been fierce, but it proves that this game is going to be a show out on talent, strength and which is the better Big Ten school.

Stars on both sides

Penn State has seen amazing sportsmanship and talent through its 2026 team, with Erika Ho, Sydney Manning and Rocquette Allen pulling 2026 Big Ten Women’s Lacrosse Preseason Poll attention.

These positive remarks are not singular to Penn State. The Big Ten put out that, in the rankings, it would place the Lions No. 5, just slightly below the Wolverines’ No. 4 ranking. Similarly, they also had three strong players to look out for in Grace Callahan, Ava Class, and Calli Norris.

With a better predictive ranking and comparable players to watch, Michigan plans to bring the heat against the blue and white, making Sunday's noon game one to tune into.

MORE LACROSSE CONTENT

title: Column | Grading the hire of new Penn State women’s basketball coach Tanisha Wright
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
On Thursday afternoon, Penn State brought in a Lady Lion great to replace former head coach Carolyn Kieger.

Tanisha Wright was named the seventh head coach in Penn State history, following a 21-year career in the WNBA as both a player and a coach.

Here’s how I’d grade the hire, including individual grades for different aspects of the coaching role.

Overall: A-

Coming off a former coach who had just one winning season in seven years, almost anything can be an improvement for the blue and white. Wright, on the extreme, should be a major improvement.

Wright comes to Happy Valley as one of the greatest Penn State players to ever take the court, leaving the Lady Lions as a three-time Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year.

After her dominant collegiate career, Wright went on to the WNBA as the highest drafted player in Penn State history, being drafted 12th overall to the Seattle Storm. There, she was consistently one of the best defensive players of the year.

Wright didn’t just play on the biggest stage in the WNBA. She played at the highest level on the biggest stage, helping the Storm to the WNBA title in 2010.

With the Lady Lions, she should be able to elevate them to a level where they’re competing for championships someday, something Kieger always claimed but never fulfilled.

Winning culture: A+

While Wright never experienced a national championship with the Lady Lions, she helped her squad clinch a tournament appearance in each of her four years.

In her first year with the blue and white, the squad rode into the NCAA tournament with a 23-12 record and made its way to the NCAA tournament Sweet 16, which it also lost in the following year.

In her third year, Wright helped the Lady Lions earn their most recent Elite Eight appearance with a 28-6 regular season, entering the NCAA tournament as the No. 1 seed. In her final year at Penn State, she helped the blue and white to a first-round appearance.

Outside of the WNBA title, Wright found team success with the Storm, including conference semifinal appearances in all but one of her 10 years in Seattle. With the Liberty, she earned an Eastern Conference finals appearance in 2015 and fell in the second round the following year.

For a Penn State squad that hasn’t made the NCAA tournament in over 10 years despite Sweet 16 aspirations in two of the past three years, Wright has the winning experience to help the Lady Lions.

Collegiate experience: D

The only reason Wright wasn’t atop my list to replace Kieger was her lack of collegiate coaching experience. But sure enough, here we are.

Outside of her four years playing with the Lady Lions from 2001-05, Wright only has a few years as an assistant coach in college, spending four years with Charlotte in the late 2010’s.

While she helped the 49ers to a pair of winning seasons during her time in the south, she did so at a mid-major program where she assisted the perimeter guards.

Now, she’ll be tasked with leading the Lady Lions in arguably the most difficult era of college coaching with NIL, the transfer portal and more. Wright will have her hands full in Year 1 with very little collegiate experience, likely relying on whatever assistant coaches she brings along to help her acclimate.

Player retention: B+

While Wright’s lack of collegiate coaching may scare some players, her success in the WNBA will likely be a big boost to keeping star talent in Happy Valley.

For players like Kiyomi McMiller and Gracie Merkle, the WNBA is likely on their horizons, and Wright is just the coach who can help them prepare for it. While the Lady Lions have had former WNBA players in Shenise Johnson and Adrienne Motley on the coaching staff, Wright is arguably more successful than the two and can implement her entire program to build toward the WNBA if she desires.

Even those who don’t have WNBA aspirations but want to succeed will likely be enticed by Wright’s new position.

Wright currently sits as the eighth-highest scorer in school history with 1,995 points and could push whichever players stick around in Happy Valley.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 3 questions surrounding Penn State ahead of spring practice
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Although the temperatures this week in State College imply otherwise, spring ball is just around the corner for Penn State.

For the first time since 2013, the Nittany Lions’ spring practice window will be led by someone other than James Franklin — Matt Campbell will be managing practice of Penn State’s new-look roster instead.

With plenty of new players and a new coaching staff, there’s plenty of uncertainty surrounding how Penn State’s 2026 season will play out, but some of those questions can be answered in the upcoming weeks.

Here are three questions surrounding the Nittany Lions heading into the spring.

Who’s stepping up in the running back room?

For four years, No. 10 and No. 13 have been the only two numbers lining up next to Penn State’s quarterback. But Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen are off to the NFL, leaving very large shoes to fill for whoever steps up in 2026.

Three potential guys could step up at running back: Carson Hansen, Quinton Martin Jr. and James Peoples. At this point, though, it’s unclear who will land the first spot on the depth chart.

Spring practice should be the first step for Campbell in determining the top running back, as it’s the first real chance for one of the three potential starters to stand out.

Hansen has the most on tape and is likely the favorite for the job after putting up 952 yards last year at Iowa State under Campbell, but Martin should be firmly in the conversation after his great performance at the Pinstripe Bowl.

The running-back battle won’t be shored up in the spring, but it’s certainly a chance for someone to become a frontrunner.

How different will practices be?

In a sense, football is just football. But each coach has a preference for how practice should be run, and Campbell likely has a different way of doing things than Franklin.

Campbell already had all of the previous signage taken down from the walls of the practice field — showcasing the fresh start that’s begun at Penn State — and his new way of doing things is set to make its way to the practice field for the first time.

Players said that practices were much shorter under interim head coach Terry Smith, and there wasn’t as much pressure on them. Considering what the Nittany Lions have said about their early impressions of Campbell, it wouldn’t be a shock if practices under him are similar to that.

Regardless, this will be some of the players’ first taste at a true practice under Campbell, and it’ll surely be different than what they experienced under Franklin.

What does a bigger defensive line look like?

Penn State had a complete overhaul at the defensive line position this offseason, as it lost its top three interior linemen from last year and both of its starting defensive ends.

With that overhaul came a different identity, as defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe and defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn have historically preferred large interior linemen. That’s exactly what the Nittany Lions went and got in the transfer portal.

The two most experienced additions are Keanu Williams and Siale Taupaki, both of whom followed Malloe from UCLA and are 310 lbs and 320 lbs, respectively. Penn State’s starting defensive tackles weighed 287 lbs and 300 lbs last season.

It’ll be interesting to see how Penn State’s new defensive tackles look at practice, and the differences from the Nittany Lions’ previous teams that featured much smaller interior linemen.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Red carpet achievements | Women awarded for excellence at 2026 Oscars
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 98th Oscars were held on Sunday, and the star-studded event highlighted the accomplishments of many in the entertainment industry.

Airing right in the middle of Women’s History Month, this year’s awards saw multiple women take home some of the biggest awards of the night. From hair and makeup designers, to actresses, directors and more, let’s take a look at how women rule the screen.

Best Casting

For the first time in Oscars’ history an award for “Excellence in Casting” was presented. Producer and Casting Director Cassandra Kulukundis, who worked on Best Picture Winner “One Battle After Another,” was the inaugural recipient.

Kulukundis is also known for her casting on films such as “Inherent Vice,” “Licorice Pizza” and “There Will Be Blood.”

Best Cinematography

“Best Cinematography" was a stacked category with big names and top rated movies. Nominees included Dan Laustsen for “Frankenstein,” Darius Khondji for his work on “Marty Supreme,” Michael Bauman with “One Battle After Another” and “Train Dreams,” with Adolpho Veloso and the sole female nominee Autumn Durald Arkapaw for her work on “Sinners.”

Arkapaw took home the award, becoming the first woman to receive the honor. She’s worked on other well known projects such as “The Sun Is Also A Star” and “Black Panther: Wakanda Forever.”

Best Original Song

“KPop Demon Hunters” took the world by storm, becoming Netflix’s most-watched animated film. So it was no surprise when its original song “Golden,” with over 1.5 billion streams on Spotify, took home “Best Original Song” at the Oscars.

EJAE, a South Korean-American singer, is the singing-voice of the film’s main character, Rumi.

In addition to performing the song, she helped co-write it and performed it live at the awards ceremony. EJAE made history as “Golden” was the first K-pop song to win this category at the Oscars.

Best Actress in a Supporting Role

This year, Amy Madigan won an Oscar for her role as Aunt Gladys in “Weapons.” Some of Madigan’s most famous works include “Uncle Buck,” “Streets of Fire” and “Field of Dreams.”

This was Madigan’s second nomination since her first in 1986 for “Twice in a Lifetime.” Not only was this Madigan’s first Oscar win, but she also represented the entirety of “Weapons” at the Oscars, as the only nomination from the film.

Best Actress in a Leading Role

Following Amy Madigan, Jessie Buckley won an Oscar for her role as Agnes, William Shakespeare's wife in “Hamnet.” This was Buckley's second nomination and first win, as she was nominated back in 2022 for best supporting actress in “The Lost Daughter.”

In addition, Buckley made history by becoming the first Irish woman to win in the category.

Best Costume Design

Costumes are what make movies come to life, as they can show a character’s personality visually and help transport you into the film’s world.

This year, the award went to Kate Hawley, whose other work includes costuming for “Suicide Squad” and “Crimson Peak.”

This year, Hawley was lauded for the work done on “Frankenstein,” which also took home the awards for best makeup and hairstyling and best production.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: A ‘cap-tivating look’ | Penn State male students on why they wear hats
author: Kelsey Bonifer | For the Collegian
description:
For hundreds of years, men have worn hats. The accessory serves as a practical means of protection from the weather, a way to show one's support for sports teams and other organizations and has been used as a status symbol in the past.

Hats became a staple of men’s fashion beginning in the early 18th and 19th centuries, particularly in the United States and Europe. As you walk throughout Penn State, you’ll notice many male students wearing hats regardless of the season, or time of day. The reasons for wearing vary across male students.

Jonathan Starasinich said everyday he likes to wear a Penn State baseball cap to show his school spirit and support for the team.

“What's on a person's hat can signal to me what the person likes and if we have any similar interests,” Starasinich, a first-year studying cybersecurity analytics and operations, said.

For Max Kehler, hats are like his second skin. When choosing a hat, he said he usually goes with what’s most practical for him on that day.

“Picking out what hat I am going to wear is part of my everyday routine,” Kehler said. “I don’t go out in public without one.”

Kehler said he also wears hats to protect himself from the elements as they keep the sun out of his eyes, and keeps him somewhat dry when it rains.

Joseph Egidio said he thinks you can learn about a person's personality based on how they wear their hat.

“A more laid back attitude could be conveyed by wearing it backwards or titled.” Egidio, a first-year studying engineering, said

Even though hats have been around for centuries, Egidio said he finds it unlikely they would ever fade to obscurity.

“Hats are popular, and will probably always be,” Egidio said. “I don’t see anyone stopping anytime soon. If anything, hats are expanding into the fashion industry, like in 2020 with bucket hats.”

Brett Lyons said his choice in hats is often influenced by how others might perceive him.

“I think I pick my hat for the day based on what other people are going to see,” Lyons, a first-year studying cybersecurity analytics and operations, said. “When I wake up and decide to wear a sports cap in the morning, I choose that because I want people to notice that I am into Penn State Football”

While some men may be initially influenced by trends to start wearing hats, Lyons noted for some, the habit eventually becomes more personal.

“I started to wear hats in high school only because everyone else was wearing them as well, but when I came to college, I didn't even notice other people wearing hats,” Lyons said. “It feels natural to see people wearing them.”

For Lyons, sometimes wearing a hat is now more about comfort than it is about appearance. He thinks the experience of not wearing one can feel unusual.

“When I put on my hat, I don’t feel like a different version of myself — I just feel like me,” Lyons said. “It's almost like the opposite. When I don’t have a hat on, I feel like I’m in disguise.”

He said even on days when his hair looks it’s best, he still chooses to wear a hat.

“I feel my most comfortable when I’m wearing a hat,” Lyons said. “It's just something I’m used to always doing and don’t feel the need to change that.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse set to open Big Ten play against No. 11 Maryland
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
The nonconference slate is over for No. 9 Penn State, and coach Jeff Tambroni’s squad might be thankful for that.

On one hand, the Nittany Lions were able to knock off high-level foes like No. 3 Princeton and No. 9 Cornell en route to a sweep of their Ivy League opponents, but the team conversely lost matchups to lesser opponents like Villanova and Navy.

Last week saw the blue and white drop their third game of the non-conference slate, a neutral-site matchup with No. 6 North Carolina that finished 10-8 in favor of the Tar Heels.

Three losses before Big Ten play isn’t something that happens often for the Nittany Lions. The last time the nonconference slate was so unsuccessful was in the 2022 season, which saw Penn State fall to six non-Big Ten opponents.

A meeting with No. 10 Maryland this Saturday could provide an opportunity for the Nittany Lions to start conference play on the right foot after the shaky start to the season.

Star attackman Hunter Aquino has been on the sidelines for both the win over Cornell and the team’s most recent loss to North Carolina, and there’s no indication whether or not the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year will be in action this week.

The Terrapins are 3-3 on the year, though those losses are of far higher quality than Penn State’s.

Maryland started its season off strong with a 19-10 victory over in-state rival Loyola after its initial season-opener against breakout squad Richmond was cancelled. After that, however, it was a rough few weeks for the Terrapins.

They lost three consecutive games against top-15 opponents, two of them being ranked in the top 2. A road trip to No. 2 Syracuse ended 11-9 in favor of the Orange, and No. 12 Princeton defeated the Terrapins 13-12 the next week.

The road got even harder from there, as No. 1 Notre Dame came into College Park and beat Maryland 11-8. Maryland bounced back in the weeks that followed, with a 13-8 win over Delaware and a triple overtime 13-12 victory over a Virginia team that received votes in the most recent rankings.

Former Yale attackman Leo Johnson has been the Terrapins’ strongest offensive weapon to date, with a team-high 25 points and 17 goals in his first year on the team. Fellow attackman Braden Erksa has recorded a team-high 11 assists.

One of the most intriguing battles within Saturday’s matchup will be at the dot, as Maryland’s faceoff specialist Henry Dodge leads the nation with a .705% faceoff percentage. His Nittany Lion opponent, Reid Gills, isn’t far behind with a .657% mark that ranks fourth in the country.

Historically, the Terrapins hold a dominant advantage over Penn State. Maryland is 47-4 against the Nittany Lions in a series that dates back to 1916, with two games going the Terrapins’ way last season in both the regular season and Big Ten tournament. Those wins came on Maryland’s ride to the NCAA championship game, where they fell to Cornell 13-10 to cap a 14-4 season.

This season’s matchup between Penn State and Maryland, labeled as a White Out, is set for a 3 p.m. faceoff from Panzer Stadium and will be the blue and white’s penultimate home game.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Review | Only the best at Eden Valley Coffee Co.
author: Talia Rittenhouse | The Daily Collegian
description:
The sweet aroma of espresso and warm vanilla greets you as you walk into Eden Valley Coffee Company on 165 Elmwood St.

Penn State students religiously drink coffee, matcha or tea to begin their long day of classes. What better way to do so than at an inviting, cozy coffee shop in our small town?

Eden Valley offers many different caffeinated drinks and a welcoming place to have a slow, relaxing morning, and a place to get a jump-start on your work for the day.

This coffee shop is my favorite in the area, and as a loyal coffee drinker, I’m eager to share four of Eden Valley’s lattes from their intriguing menu.

Honey Bee

This latte was so nostalgic for me — the flavors reminded me of a bowl of cinnamon and Honey Nut Cheerios combined. How perfect is that?

Because of this, the coffee has a very comforting taste and sets you in a fantastic mood.

The Honey Bee latte has a subtle but perfectly sweet honey flavor to it, not needing any more or any less, it’s just right.

From the outside, you can see the amount of cinnamon in the drink, which is a great flavor I didn’t expect at first from a latte labeled “honey. The cinnamon really enhances the taste and makes it come together nicely.

If cinnamon isn’t your thing, then this drink may not be for you. But I adored it, and I’m sure many others would, too.

Rating: 9/10

Maple Morning

While this latte appears to be a regular coffee with a tan exterior, it’s filled with a delightful maple flavor.

This drink tastes like a slow Sunday morning in a cup, with the maple reminding you of brunch pancakes doused in syrup.

Just imagine yourself on a porch in the early morning, hearing the sounds of nature and sipping on a nice cup of coffee — this is the Maple Morning from Eden Valley.

If you’re looking for a coffee that is not as sweet as some popular lattes usually are, this one’s for you. The flavor is subtle and not overpowering, giving you the sense of maple while still letting you truly taste the espresso in your drink, something other coffees may lack.

Rating: 8.5/10

Salted Caramel Truffle

This is my go-to order as it’s consistent and delicious every time.

With the first sip, I tasted the saltiness of the caramel, something I haven’t tasted in drinks from many other coffee shops. The cup is decorated with chocolate and caramel syrups, creating a beautiful appearance.

The chocolate doesn't overwhelm the drink, so it’s not like a mocha. In some sips, you can catch a slight nostalgic hint of chocolate milk mixed with the more mature flavor of espresso.

I can’t say enough good things about this latte. It will remain one of my top favorites among all the lattes here at Eden Valley, or ever, so I highly recommend everyone give it a try.

Rating: 10/10

Strawberry Oat

The presentation of this coffee is very similar to the caramel macchiato at Starbucks — there’s a split between the milk, coffee and the strawberry syrup resting on the bottom.

This latte is very refreshing and great for spring or summer because of its fruity flavor, but it’s truly just as perfect in any season.

If you are a fan of Elixr’s strawberry oat milk latte, then you’ll love this drink at Eden Valley.

Each sip is so refreshing, which is typically unusual for lattes. But this one really hits the spot. The espresso mixes so well with the strawberry, completing the latte with creamy oat milk.

It’s different from many other coffees you can get, thanks to the sweet strawberry addition, and let me tell you, it’s one you don’t want to miss.

Rating 9.5/10

Final Thoughts

You can’t go wrong with any coffee order at Eden Valley. They’re all so delicious and flavorful — anyone would fall in love with just one sip.

Each coffee is unique and consistent, unlike some chain coffee places. Through the sweet flavors and smooth espresso, there’s really no other place I’d rather go in State College for a good cup of joe.

So, if you’re now craving a latte or are extremely sleep-deprived, I think you know where your next stop should be.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s gymnastics hosts No. 5 Ohio State for Senior Day in regular season finale
author: Jimmy Blair | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 6 Penn State looks ahead to the final meet of the regular season at home against conference rival No. 5 Ohio State on Sunday.

The Nittany Lions arrive off a disappointing 326.250-311.550 defeat to No. 4 Michigan. With a loss to Nebraska in February, this marks two losses in a year for the blue and white, both coming to conference opponents in dual meets.

Coach Randy Jepsen and the squad are hoping for a bounce-back performance that would give the team immense momentum going into the Big Ten Tournament. If the blue and white come up short against the Buckeyes, the team would undoubtedly be dragging its feet into the conference and national tournaments, especially after putting up the second-lowest score of the season against Michigan.

The squad’s seasoned veterans will look to come through on senior day. Matt Underhill has been one of the blue and white’s most consistent performers all year, with a season high of 14.700 in the Still Rings at the Sam Mikulak Invitational.

Kacper Garnczarek has been another senior leader for the blue and white this year. He’s been consistent on the pommel horse all year, with a high of 13.700 against Army in January.

No. 4 Ohio State, like the Nittany Lions, are coming off a loss to Michigan, to a score of 328.700-326.550. Despite this result, the Buckeyes have been a force in the Big Ten all year.

One thing to keep an eye on regarding Ohio State is its performances this season in quad meets. They only managed second-place finishes in the Ohio State Open and the Stanford Open. While this doesn’t necessarily spell trouble for the Buckeyes,

The Buckeyes will look to rely on senior Parker Thackston as the team gets closer to the end-of-season tournaments. The Robbinsville, New Jersey, native will use his experience and skill to come through for the Buckeyes down the stretch. He was an All-American on the Pommel Horse in 2024 and 2025, and has a 15.250 personal best on the pommel horse.

These two schools could very well meet more than once as the end of this season unfolds. Both sides are top-ranked forces looking to take not only the Big Ten Tournament but also the National Championship. The blue and white will eye a momentum-saving win at home against the Buckeyes, going into the Big Ten Tournament.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: ‘A great opportunity’ | Ahead of annual hack-a-thon, students highlight its impact, benefits
author: Saxtyn Lutz I For the Collegian
description:
Penn State’s largest student-run hack-a-thon, hosted by Hack at Penn State, will take place on March 28 and 29 at the Engineering Collaborative Research and Education Building (ECoRE).

Hundreds of students spend 24 hours, from 2 p.m. Saturday to 2 p.m. Sunday, creating a project from scratch. This entails coming up with an idea for their projects, designing a solution and then building it. The purpose of the projects are to address real world problems and apply practical solutions.

“It’s a very exciting environment,” Vishnu Venugopal, a tech organizer, said.

Due to increasing popularity over the last few semesters, there have been changes to the admission process. Last semester, 1,000 people registered for the event so organizers decided to employ more selective admissions.

It’s not meant to restrict numbers but to find the people who really want to spend the full 24 hours working, Venugopal, a third year studying computer science and mathematics, said. He added eligibility for admissions is determined more by interest than experience.

Venugopal will be a returning attendee at this year’s hack-a-thon and said he has fond memories from past ones.

Joe Boppell is the tech director for Hack. He and 13 other members of the tech team spent the last three months developing the technical side of the new application system which, he said, ended up being a personally rewarding process.

“That was a really, really important moment because I’d never before in my life built something that important and huge,” Boppell, a third year studying computer science, said.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming more prominent in project design, according to Boppell. He said people involved in the hack-a-thon are excited about AI. Participants are allowed to use AI in their project, though they cannot use it to generate their entire piece.

“It just kind of opened a whole new door of the types of projects that people can make,” Boppell said. “I’ve noticed a lot of people actually building their own AI models and implementing them in their own way, which has been really good to see.”

Rather than students spending a large chunk of their limited time on coding, Boppell said AI takes care of the busy work. This allows students to focus their time on project design and other creative elements of the project.

By nature of the event, most students involved have technical backgrounds. The majority of participants are studying computer science, but it also extends to those studying mathematics, data sciences, information sciences and technology or computer engineering.

Anna Jiang recalls being unsure of how to create a project in her first hack-a-thon. However, other members of her team had participated in hack-a-thons before and were able to guide her through the process and structure the timeline.

Jiang, a third year studying computer science, is currently the sponsorship director for Hack. She described the relationship between the organization and sponsors as mutually beneficial.

Sponsors are needed in order to make this event possible, and Hack at Penn State provides those companies with recruitment and advertising opportunities. Sponsors can observe first-hand a student's performance with technical projects, which is a different type of skill assessment than a job interview.

“While the event is going on, students can go up to them to network and connect,” Jiang said. “Companies can recruit for jobs that they’re offering, or internships, but also our PSU departments can come to try to garner students into their realm.”

The money that sponsors provide goes toward meals and merchandise needed to support the hundreds of participants.

Kyle Chiem, a second-year studying computer science, is a technical organizer. He was inspired to participate in Hack at Penn State in his first semester because the hack-a-thon was a way for him to get technical experience.

As Chiem spent more time at the hack-a-thon, he realized it’s “a lot more than just getting your first experience.” He said this led him to join Hack at Penn State as an organizer which allows him to contribute first-hand to the event and for students.

When the 24-hour countdown is over, there’s a judging expo where teams present their projects to the organizers. An open-sourced algorithm from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology is used to make judging quicker and more accurate.

During the closing ceremony, there’s a showcase for winners to receive prizes. Hack at Penn State gives out cash prizes totaling $1,000 and sponsors give out prizes tailored to their specific company.

“I think it's just a great opportunity for students to just learn a lot and see what it's like to code in a really fast-paced environment,” Chiem said.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State wrestling remains hot, advances 8 wrestlers to NCAA Championships quarterfinals
author: Wrestling staff
description:
CLEVELAND — Penn State returned to the mats on Thursday evening to continue the opening day of the NCAA Championships.

The Nittany Lions carried their momentum from earlier in the day into Session 2, earning seven-bonus point victories en route to eight wrestlers in the quarterfinals and the top spot in the team rankings with 40.5 points.

Here’s how Penn State did in Session 2.

125 pounds

Second round: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs. No. 17 Jett Strickenberger (WVU)

Luke Lilledahl stood on the mat pacing for three minutes as he waited for the ESPN broadcast intro to clear the way for his match with Jett Strickenberger. When the whistle finally blew, the bout started eerily similar to his opening contest.

While Lilledahl attempted to be the aggressor, Strickenberger used his reach to push Lilledahl away throughout the round. Lilledahl caught Strickenberger’s leg one time in the period, but Strickenberger used his strength to escape the Nittany Lions’ grasp.

In the second period, Lilledahl caught Strickenberger’s leg, which Strickenberger responded by attempting to spin out, which set off a catfight looking scramble that lasted nearly half a minute. When the dust settled on the scrum, Lilledahl secured the only takedown of the bout, going on to win, 4-2.

133 pounds

Second round: No. 3 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs. No. 19 Gage Walker (MIZZ)

Gage Walker attempted to be the aggressor early in his bout against Marcus Blaze, but after avoiding a couple of early shots, the pressure shifted in favor of the Penn State freshman.

As time ran out in the first period, Blaze tripped Walker and caught him on his back to receive four-near fall points and take an 8-0 lead into the second frame. Halfway through the second period, he caught him in the same spot, and extended the lead to 13-2.

In the third period, Blaze escaped Walker in the first 15 seconds and went back on offense. While circling Walker, Blaze hooked his leg and his neck, seeking a pin attempt, but let go and settled for a final takedown to secure the 17-2 tech fall.

141 pounds

Second round: No. 14 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 3 Brock Hardy (NEB)

Braeden Davis opened this contest with Brock Hardy looking more patient than their previous bout during the regular season. After a minute of handfighting, Davis caught Hardy for the first takedown of the day. However, Davis being overzealous cost him again, as while trying to roll Hardy over, he allowed Hardy to reverse the takedown and gain control.

After a 45-second ride by Davis to start the second period, Hardy was awarded a takedown in controversial fashion after a visual stalemate between the two wrestlers. Davis signaled toward the bench to throw a challenge brick, but the request was denied, and Davis entered the third period facing a 6-4 deficit.

A similar instance occurred as Davis tried to net a lead-securing takedown, where Hardy was awarded a takedown, while Davis still had his hands wrapped around the Cornhusker. After the whistle blew, Davis signaled to the referees and his coaches, but once again no challenge was thrown. Hardy won 9-4 and Davis quickly shook hands with him and the bench and left the floor.

149 pounds

Second round: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs. No. 16 Jacob Frost (ISU)

Shayne Van Ness took on Iowa State’s Jacob Frost, and the aggression from the Nittany Lion continued.

Van Ness couldn’t secure a takedown on his first attack, despite having one of Frost’s legs. However, the second time was successful for the Somerville, New Jersey, native, notching his first takedown of the match.

Shortly after, Van Ness notched his second takedown, this time getting Frost on his back and getting the pinfall. It made it two straight matches for Van Ness ending in either a tech fall or pinfall, scoring bonus points for the team score.

157 pounds

Second round: No. 1 PJ Duke (PSU) vs. No. 17 Luke Mechler (WIS)

Under 10 seconds into the match between PJ Duke and Luke Mechler, Duke scored the first takedown. Twenty seconds later, Duke scored once again as the scoring barrage began.

An immediate release of Mechler allowed Duke to instantly score, and seconds later, notch his fourth takedown in the first minute. Moments later, Duke locked up the cradle after his fifth takedown and secured the pin at 1:38.

165 pounds

Second round: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs. No. 16 Paddy Gallagher (OSU)

Mitchell Mesenbrink began his match the same way he does every match with instant attacks. While the first few attempts didn’t find success, he took down Paddy Gallagher with 42 seconds on the clock.

Mesenbrink earned a fourth point in the opening frame on stalling calls from Gallagher. A second-period takedown extended Mesenbrink’s lead to 8-0, putting him in bonus territory once again.

Gallagher was tagged with stalling two more times in the third period, giving Mesenbrink three additional points with a fourth one coming from over three minutes of riding time. Mesenbrink rolled to the quarterfinals on a 12-0 major decision.

174 pounds

Second round: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 16 Nick Fine (COL)

Levi Haines faced off against Nick Fine in the second round for a spot in the quarterfinals. In under 30 seconds, Haines notched his first takedown, ending the first period with a total of three to reach a 9-2 advantage.

Haines rolled from there, scoring two takedowns in the second period to pretty much secure bonus points. In the final period, Haines took down Fine two more times, getting the tech-fall victory and advancing to the quarterfinals with his second straight bonus-point victory.

184 pounds

Second round: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs No. 16 Rylan Rogers (LEH)

Rocco Welsh started this match the same way as he did during the first session — in control. Welsh secured his first takedown 15 seconds into the match. The sophomore continued to dominate, as he shortly after secured his second takedown over Rylan Rogers.

It was a bit more stagnant through the match until late in the third period, when Welsh extended his lead with two more takedowns and got the victory by major decision, 13-4.

197 pounds

Second round: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs No. 17 Dillon Bechtold (BUCK)

Josh Barr didn’t get the first takedown immediately, but he still secured two in the first period. The sophomore started his reign of dominance after the first period.

Barr escaped from Dillon Bechtold and didn’t allow him to get much distance on the mat. Barr got another takedown to extend his lead to 10-2, but Bechtold got back up to take Barr out of the major-decision victory, but Barr had the riding time so an 11-3 major-decision victory it was for him.

285 pounds

Second round: No. 9 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs No. 8 Ben Kueter (IOWA)

Entering the bout, the season series between Cole Mirasola and Ben Kueter was tied at 1-1, with Mirasola winning on the road in the regular season, and Kueter emerging victorious at the Big Ten Championships.

The match started out with both wrestlers feeling each other out. Kueter got up immediately, as he has done a lot of this season — Mirasola had the chance to take down the Iowa heavyweight, but couldn’t get it done.

Mirasola, down 1-0, decided to go neutral. It didn’t work out in his favor, and Kueter got the takedown and was on top for the rest of the match, winning 4-0.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: ‘We just don’t get respect’ | Penn State women’s hockey looks to make statement in Frozen Four
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The hours continue to tick down before No. 3 Penn State makes its first Frozen Four appearance in program history. After their practice before the historic game, Tessa Janecke and Katie DeSa met with the media before their last weekend as a Nittany Lion.

In what was their final word before the action commences, the two seniors focused on the respect they believe the program should be given and the magnitude of reaching their first-ever Frozen Four.

“I think it just means having us as (No. 3 in the country) and just no question about it,” DeSa said. “I think when we first broke into that top-five, there were definitely some people being like ‘oh, their schedule’s so weak, they don’t deserve it.’ But, I think we’ve shown it with the competition we’ve played and the games we’ve won.”

This season has been nothing short of historic for the Nittany Lions. After earning its highest ranking at No. 3 and ending the season with the most wins in program history with 33, the blue and white have reached new grounds.

A program that has only earned a winning season once before coach Jeff Kampersal took the position, is now two wins away from winning a national championship for the first time in Penn State hockey.

With multiple seasons resulting in defeat, the level of respect from its peers had to be built from the ground up. For the players, reaching the Frozen Four serves as an example as to why the program should be looked at differently.

“We just don’t get respect, I think in the entire NCAA as a whole. But, hopefully just making this Frozen Four and putting up a good game tomorrow will hopefully change that narrative, and get people to want to come here, to the best facilities and hopefully the best team.” Janecke said.

Regardless of how Penn State ends its season, the Frozen Four has been a goal for the seven departing seniors since entering the program. With that now in their grasp, the class looks to continue building a reputation that draws in players even when they leave.

“I think that was kind of always our goal, to get over that hump of being a Top-10 team and getting up to a top-five team and now in the Frozen Four,” Janecke said. “So, I think what we’ve done as a senior class, and just a leadership group in general, is kind of amazing. What we’ve done with the program just in these four years, and I know it’s just going to continue now and once we leave, it’s going to get better and hopefully remain a powerhouse.”

The Nittany Lions have already faced a top-five team this season in Ohio State, resulting in two lopsided losses.

Learning from this early-season defeat, both players seemed confident that their leadership group would brave the upcoming challenge.

“I think it would just be a testament to our team, to our leadership,” Janecke said. “I think it’s going to test both leadership groups, not just ours but theirs as well, just to see what we both can bring. I’m excited for it and I know our team’s ready for it, and to get this win will be huge.”

MORE HOCKEY CONTENT

title: Penn State women’s hockey head coach Jeff Kampersal named 2026 CCM/AHCA Women’s Division I Coach of the Year
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Head coach Jeff Kampersal earned the 2026 CCM/AHCA Women’s University Division I Coach of the Year, his first national Coach of the Year award.

The honor comes after Kampersal guided No. 3 Penn State through its most historic season, culminating in a first-ever Frozen Four appearance.

The best coach in the country resides in #HockeyValley Coach Kampersal has been named the CCM/AHCA Women’s University Division 𝐂𝐨𝐚𝐜𝐡 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 👏#WeAre pic.twitter.com/5JKaI3h8Bp— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 19, 2026

Following a win against Delaware in the middle of the season, Kampersal earned his 500th career win as head coach.

Kampersal and the Nittany Lions will take on No. 2 Wisconsin at Pegula Ice Arena tomorrow at 7:30 p.m. with a spot in the National Championship game on the line.
